text: In this paper, we present EdgeFlow, a novel approach to interactive segmentation that leverages the concept of edge-guided flow to achieve practical and efficient segmentation of images. Interactive segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves allowing a user to interact with an image to indicate the regions of interest. However, existing methods for interactive segmentation are often limited by their reliance on iterative user input, which can be time-consuming and may not accurately capture the user's intended segmentation. EdgeFlow addresses these limitations by introducing a new approach that combines the efficiency of edge-guided segmentation with the practicality of interactive segmentation. Our approach uses a novel edge-guided flow algorithm that propagates user input along the edges of an image, allowing for fast and accurate segmentation of complex images. We demonstrate the effectiveness of EdgeFlow through extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets, showing that it outperforms state-of-the-art interactive segmentation methods in terms of both efficiency and accuracy. In addition, we explore the versatility of EdgeFlow by applying it to a variety of applications, including object segmentation, medical image segmentation, and video segmentation. Our results show that EdgeFlow can handle a wide range of scenarios, from simple object segmentation to complex medical image segmentation, and can accurately segment objects in real-time. Overall, EdgeFlow represents a significant advancement in the field of interactive segmentation, providing a practical and efficient approach that can be used in a variety of applications. Its ability to accurately segment images with minimal user input makes it an ideal tool for a wide range of users, from researchers to practitioners.
label: ai
title: EdgeFlow: Achieving Practical Interactive Segmentation with Edge-Guided Flow

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to interactive segmentation called EdgeFlow, which leverages edge information to guide the segmentation process and achieve practical interaction efficiency. Existing methods often suffer from slow convergence or rely on heuristics that limit their accuracy, making them impractical for real-world applications. EdgeFlow addresses these limitations by introducing an edge-guided flow algorithm that combines the benefits of both boundary-following and gradient-descent-based methods. The proposed method efficiently explores the image space while adaptively adjusting its behavior based on local edge features, ensuring accurate segmentation and minimizing user input. We evaluate EdgeFlow on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art methods in terms of efficiency, accuracy, and usability. Our results show significant advantages in terms of both computational cost and user effort required to obtain high-quality segmentations. Additionally, we demonstrate the versatility of EdgeFlow through applications in various domains such as object recognition, medical imaging, and video analysis. The key contributions of this work can be summarized as follows: * Introducing EdgeFlow, a practical solution for efficient and accurate interactive segmentation guided by edge information. * Developing a novel edge-guided flow algorithm that adapts to local edge features, promoting accurate and efficient segmentation. * Showcasing superior performance of EdgeFlow compared to existing methods in terms of both computational cost and user effort required. * Demonstrating the applicability of EdgeFlow across diverse domains, including object recognition, medical imaging, and video analysis. In conclusion, our proposed method presents a substantial improvement over traditional interactivesegmentation techniques and has far-reaching implications for applications where precise segmentation is critical. We envision EdgeFlow serving as a valuable tool for researchers, practitioners, and users seeking efficient and reliable segmentation solutions.
label: ai
title: EdgeFlow: Achieving Practical Interactive Segmentation with Edge-Guided Flow

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using semi-supervised contrastive learning. Our approach leverages the large amount of unlabelled data available in medical imaging to improve segmentation accuracy, while reducing the need for annotated data. We introduce a new framework that combines contrastive learning with a segmentation model, enabling the model to learn from both labelled and unlabelled data. The proposed method is evaluated on several medical image segmentation tasks, including brain tumor segmentation and lung segmentation, and shows superior performance compared to supervised learning methods that rely solely on annotated data. Our results demonstrate the potential of semi-supervised contrastive learning for label-efficient medical image segmentation, and highlight the importance of exploiting unlabelled data to improve segmentation accuracy.
label: ai
title: Semi-supervised Contrastive Learning for Label-efficient Medical Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using semi-supervised contrastive learning. Our method leverages the vast amount of unlabeled data available in medical imaging datasets to improve segmentation performance, while minimizing the need for labeled data. Existing approaches to medical image segmentation rely heavily on supervised learning methods that require large quantities of labeled data. However, labeling medical images is a time-consuming and expensive process, making it challenging to collect sufficient amounts of labeled data. In contrast, contrastive learning methods have shown promising results in various computer vision tasks by utilizing large amounts of unlabeled data to learn robust representations. Our proposed method combines semi-supervised contrastive learning with a novel self-paced learning strategy to effectively leverage both labeled and unlabled data. The self-paced learning strategy gradually increases the proportion of labeled data during training, allowing the model to adapt to the limited availability of labels. We evaluate our method on several publicly available medical image segmentation benchmarks, including brain tumor segmentation and liver segmentation. The experimental results show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation models while requiring significantly less labeled data. Additionally, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in dealing with imbalanced class distribution, which is a common issue in medical image segmentation tasks. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: * A novel semi-supervised contrastive learning approach for medical image segmentation that leverages large amounts of unlabeled data. * A self-paced learning strategy that adaptively incorporates labeled data into training, reducing the requirement for annotated data. * State-of-the-art performance on multiple medical image segmentation benchmarks, outperforming fully supervised models while using fewer labels. * Robustness to class imbalance, a critical issue in medical image segmentation. This work has significant implications for improving the efficiency and accuracy of medical image segmentation tasks, enabling clinicians and researchers to make more informed decisions with reduced annotation effort.
label: ai
title: Semi-supervised Contrastive Learning for Label-efficient Medical Image Segmentation

text: In the field of medical image segmentation, imbalanced data can pose significant challenges, particularly when dealing with multi-organ segmentation tasks. The scarcity of certain organ instances or classes can result in biased models that perform poorly on unseen data. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel loss function called Combo Loss, which is designed to handle input and output imbalance in multi-organ segmentation tasks. Combo Loss combines two main components: a class-balanced loss function and a spatially-aware loss function. The class-balanced loss function addresses output imbalance by up-weighting the minority classes, while the spatially-aware loss function addresses input imbalance by penalizing the model for incorrectly segmenting organs that are spatially close to the true positive instances. The authors evaluate Combo Loss on two publicly available datasets: the Medical Segmentation Decathlon dataset and the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) dataset. The results show that Combo Loss outperforms state-of-the-art loss functions, including Dice loss and Lovász softmax, in terms of segmentation accuracy and robustness to input and output imbalance. Furthermore, the authors perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the two components of Combo Loss. The results demonstrate that both components contribute to the improved performance, and that the combination of the two components leads to better results than using either component alone. The paper also discusses the limitations of Combo Loss and potential avenues for future work. The authors note that Combo Loss may not be suitable for tasks with more than two classes, and that exploring other techniques, such as oversampling the minority class or using class-balanced batch sampling, may be necessary in certain cases. In conclusion, Combo Loss offers a promising solution to the challenges of input and output imbalance in multi-organ segmentation tasks. Its ability to handle both class and spatial imbalance makes it a valuable tool for medical image segmentation tasks, where imbalanced data is common. The paper provides a thorough evaluation of
label: ai
title: Combo Loss: Handling Input and Output Imbalance in Multi-Organ Segmentation

text: In medical image segmentation, imbalanced input and output data can significantly affect the performance of deep learning models. This issue is particularly challenging in multi-organ segmentation tasks, where multiple organs with varying sizes, shapes, and locations need to be accurately segmented. To address this problem, we propose a novel loss function called Combo Loss that efficiently handles input and output imbalance in multi-organ segmentation. Combo Loss combines two main components: an asymmetric loss and a clustering loss. The asymmetric loss encourages the model to focus on the accurate segmentation of smaller organs while the clustering loss promotes spatial consistency among pixels belonging to the same organ. We evaluated our proposed method on several publicly available datasets, including the Medical ImageNet dataset, which contains various abdominal organs, and compared it against state-of-the-art methods. Our experiments demonstrate that Combo Loss outperforms existing loss functions in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, it achieves a higher mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) score and requires fewer annotations. Moreover, we perform extensive ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of each component of Combo Loss. These results provide valuable insights into how different design choices impact the overall performance of the loss function. Finally, we showcase the versatility of Combo Loss by applying it to other medical segmentation tasks such as brain structures segmentation from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. Our work makes significant contributions to the field of medical image segmentation, especially in handling input and output imbalances. Combo Loss offers an adaptive solution that can be easily integrated into existing deep learning architectures, paving the way for better performance and robustness in various applications, including computer vision and beyond.
label: ai
title: Combo Loss: Handling Input and Output Imbalance in Multi-Organ Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for training an attention-based 3D seismic fault segmentation model using a limited number of 2D slice labels. Our approach leverages the power of deep learning to accurately identify and segment faults in 3D seismic data, while significantly reducing the amount of labeling effort required. Existing methods for 3D seismic fault segmentation typically require a large number of manually labeled 3D samples, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our approach uses a few 2D slice labels to train a 3D attention-based model that can accurately segment faults in the entire 3D volume. The proposed method consists of two main components: a 2D labeling step and a 3D training step. In the 2D labeling step, we manually label a small number of 2D slices from the 3D seismic data to create a set of 2D fault labels. These labels are then used to train a 2D attention-based fault segmentation model. In the 3D training step, we use the trained 2D model to generate a set of 3D attention weights, which are then used to train a 3D attention-based fault segmentation model. We evaluate the performance of our approach using a dataset of 3D seismic data with manually labeled faults. Our results show that our approach can accurately segment faults in 3D seismic data with a high degree of accuracy, while significantly reducing the amount of labeling effort required. We also compare our approach with existing methods and demonstrate its superior performance. The main contribution of this paper is the development of an attention-based 3D seismic fault segmentation model that can be trained using a limited number of 2D slice labels. Our approach has the potential to significantly reduce the cost and time required for fault segmentation in 3D seismic data, making it a valuable tool for the oil and gas industry, as well as for academic researchers.
label: ai
title: Attention-Based 3D Seismic Fault Segmentation Training by a Few 2D Slice Labels

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for training an attention-based 3D seismic fault segmentation model using a limited number of labeled 2D slices. Our proposed method leverages a combination of convolutional neural networks and attention mechanisms to effectively learn from sparse annotations and accurately segment faults in 3D seismic data. Existing approaches for 3D seismic fault segmentation typically require large amounts of manual annotation effort, which can be time-consuming and expensive. To address this challenge, we propose a weakly supervised learning framework that utilizes only a few labeled 2D slices to train a 3D segmentation model. The key idea is to use the scarce labels to guide the attention mechanism, which focuses on the most informative regions of the input data and improves the accuracy of the segmentation model. We evaluate our approach on several synthetic and real-world datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in comparison with fully supervised models and other state-of-the-art methods. Our results show that the attention-based approach achieves competitive performance while requiring significantly fewer annotated samples. This suggests that the proposed method has great potential for reducing the labeling effort required for 3D seismic fault segmentation. The main contributions of this work are twofold: (1) We introduce a novel attention-based 3D seismic fault segmentation model that learns from a small set of labeled 2D slices. (2) We demonstrate the efficiency and efficacy of our approach through extensive experiments, showing that it outperforms existing weakly supervised methods while substantially reducing the annotation overhead. Overall, this paper presents a significant step forward in addressing the challenges associated with 3D seismic fault segmentation and highlights the promising potential of attention-based machine learning techniques in geophysical imaging applications.
label: ai
title: Attention-Based 3D Seismic Fault Segmentation Training by a Few 2D Slice Labels

text: In this paper, we present Segmenter, a novel approach to semantic segmentation that leverages the power of Transformers to accurately and efficiently segment images into their constituent parts. Semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision, with applications in a wide range of domains, including autonomous driving, medical imaging, and robotics. Existing approaches to semantic segmentation rely on convolutional neural networks (CNNs), which are limited in their ability to capture long-range dependencies and contextual information. In contrast, Transformers have proven highly effective in natural language processing tasks, where long-range dependencies are crucial. We hypothesize that Transformers can similarly improve performance in semantic segmentation tasks. Our Segmenter model replaces traditional CNNs with Transformers, using self-attention mechanisms to capture contextual information and long-range dependencies between image pixels. We evaluate Segmenter on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-based models in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. In addition to its improved performance, Segmenter has several advantages over traditional CNN-based models. It requires fewer parameters and computations, making it more computationally efficient and easier to train. It is also more scalable, allowing for larger input sizes and faster inference times. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of Transformers in semantic segmentation tasks, and highlight the potential of Segmenter as a valuable tool for a wide range of applications. We further analyze the contributions of different components of the Segmenter model, providing insights into the role of self-attention and other design choices in its performance. Overall, our work represents a significant step forward in the field of semantic segmentation, and demonstrates the versatility and power of Transformers in computer vision tasks.
label: ai
title: Segmenter: Transformer for Semantic Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic segmentation using the transformer architecture. Traditional methods rely on fully convolutional networks (FCNs) that suffer from limited receptive field and inability to model long-range dependencies. Our proposed method, called Segmenter, leverages the self-attention mechanism of transformers to overcome these limitations and achieve state-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets. We begin by introducing our dataset preparation process, which includes data augmentation techniques and a new semi-supervised learning framework that enables us to train on large amounts of unlabelled data. Next, we outline the architecture of Segmenter, consisting of a multi-layer encoder and decoder with attention mechanisms. We also introduce a novel technique called Spatial Attention Masking (SAM), which allows the model to focus on relevant regions and reduce computational complexity. Our experimental results show that Segmenter outperforms FCNs on multiple datasets including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and CamVid. Specifically, we achieve an increase of +4.3% mIoU on Cityscapes and +5.1% mIoU on CamVid compared to the baseline FCN model. Additionally, we provide extensive ablation studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of SAM and other key components of Segmenter. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: * We introduce Segmenter, a transformer-based architecture for semantic segmentation that models long-range dependencies and outperforms traditional FCNs. * We develop a novel technique called Spatial Attention Masking (SAM) to improve efficiency and accuracy. * We establish new state-of-the-art performances on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and CamVid. Overall, this work demonstrates the potential of transformers in computer vision tasks, particularly in semantic segmentation where long-range dependencies are crucial.
label: ai
title: Segmenter: Transformer for Semantic Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for semantic segmentation that goes beyond traditional image-based methods by incorporating contextual information from various sources. Semantic segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that aims to assign a semantic label to each pixel in an image. While state-of-the-art methods have achieved impressive results using deep learning techniques, they are limited by their reliance on image data alone. We argue that contextual information, such as textual descriptions, object categories, and scene information, can provide valuable cues for improving segmentation accuracy. Our approach, called Contextual Segmentation Network (CSN), leverages a multimodal fusion framework to integrate image and contextual information in a single neural network. The CSN model consists of three main components: an image encoder, a context encoder, and a fusion module. The image encoder extracts features from the input image using a convolutional neural network (CNN), while the context encoder processes the contextual information using a natural language processing (NLP) technique. The fusion module combines the image and context features using a novel attention mechanism, enabling the model to weigh the importance of different contextual cues for each pixel. We evaluate the CSN model on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art image-based segmentation methods. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic segmentation that leverages contextual information beyond the image itself. Traditional methods of semantic segmentation rely solely on the visual content of the image, neglecting valuable contextual information that can improve accuracy and robustness. We introduce a framework that incorporates external knowledge and priorities to enhance the feature representation and overcome the limitations of standard CNN-based segmentation models. Our approach consists of two main components: (1) a knowledge injection module that incorporates external knowledge from various sources, such as textual descriptions, object categories, and scene understanding, into the feature extraction process; and (2) a priority-aware module that adapts the feature representations based on the importance of each pixel in the image. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO, and demonstrate significant improvements over state-of-the-art segmentation methods. The key contributions of this paper are: * A novel framework that effectively incorporates contextual information beyond the image into the semantic segmentation process. * An efficient knowledge injection module that integrates external knowledge from diverse sources without requiring manual annotation. * A priority-aware module that adapts the feature representations according to the importance of each pixel, enhancing the overall performance and robustness of the model. Our experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of mining contextual information beyond the image for semantic segmentation tasks. This work has important implications for applications where accurate segmentation is critical,such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for referring image segmentation that incorporates comprehensive multi-modal interactions between the user and the system. Our approach allows users to interact with the system through various modalities, including speech, gesture, and touch, to improve the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation process. We propose a deep learning-based framework that integrates multiple modalities and leverages their complementary information to improve the segmentation performance. The framework consists of several components, including a speech recognition module, a gesture recognition module, a touch-based interaction module, and a segmentation module. These components work together to enable the user to provide rich and diverse inputs that can effectively convey their desired segmentation goals. We evaluate our approach on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superiority compared to traditional single-modal interaction methods. Our experiments show that the proposed approach can significantly improve the segmentation accuracy and reduce the interaction time, making it more efficient and user-friendly. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We propose a comprehensive multi-modal interaction framework for referring image segmentation that integrates speech, gesture, and touch modalities. * We develop a deep learning-based model that fuses the complementary information from multiple modalities to improve the segmentation performance. * We conduct extensive experiments to evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach and demonstrate its superiority compared to traditional single-modal methods. Our work has important implications for various applications, such as medical imaging, robotics, and human-computer interaction. The proposed approach can help improve the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation tasks, making it more accessible and user-friendly for a wide range of users.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to referring image segmentation that leverages comprehensive multi-modal interactions between humans and machines. Our proposed method enables users to interact with an image using natural language commands, gestures, and visual attention, allowing them to accurately refer to specific regions of interest within the image. We propose a unified framework that combines these multiple modalities into a single coherent representation, enabling effective communication and collaboration between humans and machines. This is achieved through a combination of deep learning techniques, including convolutional neural networks (CNNs), recurrent neural networks (RNNs), and multi-modal fusion algorithms. The key contribution of our work lies in its ability to effectively integrate and reconcile the strengths of different modalities to improve image segmentation accuracy. We demonstrate the efficacy of our approach on several challenging benchmark datasets, where it outperforms existing methods that rely solely on language or vision inputs. Through extensive user studies, we also investigate the impact of modality switching strategies and identify optimal combinations of modalities that maximize performance. Our findings have important implications for applications such as object recognition, medical imaging analysis, and human-robot interaction, where effective multi-modal communication can significantly enhance task efficiency and accuracy.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to few-shot segmentation, which is a challenging task in computer vision that involves segmenting objects from a small number of labeled examples. Our approach, called Few-Shot Segmentation with Global and Local Contrastive Learning (FSS-GCL), combines the strengths of global and local contrastive learning to improve segmentation accuracy. Global contrastive learning has been shown to be effective in learning high-level representations that are useful for few-shot learning. However, it may not capture local information that is crucial for segmentation tasks. Local contrastive learning, on the other hand, can capture local information but may not generalize well to new examples. In FSS-GCL, we propose a two-stage training strategy that first uses global contrastive learning to learn high-level representations and then uses local contrastive learning to refine the segmentation masks. We also introduce a novel mask-based contrastive loss function that encourages the model to produce accurate segmentation masks. Experiments on several benchmark datasets show that FSS-GCL outperforms state-of-the-art few-shot segmentation methods, including those that use global or local contrastive learning alone. We also perform ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the different components of our approach. Our main contributions are: * A novel few-shot segmentation approach called FSS-GCL that combines global and local contrastive learning * A two-stage training strategy that leverages the strengths of global and local contrastive learning * A mask-based contrastive loss function that encourages accurate segmentation masks * State-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets Overall, FSS-GCL demonstrates the effectiveness of combining global and local contrastive learning for few-shot segmentation, and provides a promising direction for future research in this area.

title: Few-Shot Segmentation with Global and Local Contrastive Learning

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to few-shot segmentation, which leverages both global and local contrastive learning to achieve state-of-the-art performance. Our proposed method, called FSGL (Few-Shot Segmentation with Global and Local Contrastive Learning), combines the strengths of global contrastive learning and local contrastive learning to improve the accuracy and efficiency of few-shot segmentation. Global contrastive learning is used to learn high-level features that are robust across different datasets and tasks, while local contrastive learning is employed to learn task-specific features that are important for segmenting specific objects or regions in an image. By combining these two types of contrastive learning, our model can learn both generalizable and task-specific knowledge, leading to improved performance on few-shot segmentation tasks. FSGL consists of two main components: a global feature extractor and a local feature refiner. The global feature extractor uses a self-supervised loss function to learn high-level features from a large dataset, while the local feature refiner uses a supervised loss function to fine-tune the learned features for a specific segmentation task. We also propose a novel technique called multi-scale dense connections to integrate the global and local features effectively. Experiments conducted on several benchmark datasets show that FSGL outperforms existing state-of-the-art few-shot segmentation methods by a significant margin. Notably, FSGL achieves an average IoU score of 86.5% on PASCAL VOC, which is 10.2% higher than the previous state-of-the-art method. Moreover, FSGL requires only 320k parameters, making it much more efficient compared to other state-of-the-art models. Our work demonstrates the effectiveness of incorporating both global and local contrastive learning into few-shot segmentation models. The proposed FSGL model has important implications for real-world applications where accurate segmentation is required, such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel interactive segmentation framework for correcting mispredictions during the clinical evaluation of medical images. Our approach is efficient, generic, and can be applied to various medical image segmentation tasks, making it a valuable tool for clinicians and researchers. Existing segmentation methods often produce errors, especially when dealing with complex or low-quality images. These errors can lead to incorrect diagnoses and treatments, which can have serious consequences for patients. Interactive segmentation methods, which allow users to correct segmentation errors, can mitigate this problem, but current approaches are often time-consuming, task-specific, and require extensive user interaction. Our proposed framework addresses these limitations by providing an efficient and generic interactive segmentation solution. We introduce a novel algorithm that combines machine learning and graph-based segmentation techniques to accurately identify and correct mispredictions. The algorithm is designed to be fast and efficient, allowing for real-time feedback and correction during clinical evaluation. We evaluate our framework on several datasets of different medical imaging modalities and compare it to state-of-the-art interactive segmentation methods. Our results show that our approach significantly improves segmentation accuracy and efficiency, while also reducing user interaction time. We also demonstrate the versatility of our framework by applying it to various medical image segmentation tasks, including tumor segmentation, organ segmentation, and vessel segmentation. Our interactive segmentation framework has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation, and ultimately, the diagnosis and treatment of diseases. Its efficiency, genericity, and real-time feedback capabilities make it an ideal tool for clinicians and researchers who need to quickly and accurately segment medical images.

title: Efficient and Generic Interactive Segmentation Framework to Correct Mispredictions during Clinical Evaluation of Medical Images

text: In this paper, we propose an efficient and generic interactive segmentation framework to correct mispredictions during clinical evaluation of medical images. Our approach leverages the strengths of both machine learning algorithms and human expertise to improve the accuracy of image segmentation models. We present a novel method that enables domain-expert annotators to interact with a deep neural network through user-friendly interfaces, allowing them to correct errors in real-time while providing feedback to the model. Our proposed framework is capable of adapting to various types of medical images and segmentation tasks, making it a versatile tool for clinical applications. The key components of our method include an intuitive interface design, a robust data storage system, and an algorithm that utilizes user input to refine the segmentation output. Through extensive experiments on several datasets, we demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of our approach in reducing mispredictions and improving overall segmentation accuracy. We evaluate our framework by comparing its performance against state-of-the-art methods as well as existing approaches that rely solely on machine learning or manual annotation. Our results show that the incorporation of human expertise significantly enhances the precision and recall of the segmented regions. Furthermore, we conduct a usability study that shows the feasibility and practicality of using our framework in real-world scenarios. The main contributions of this work are: (1) the development of an efficient and generic interactive segmentation framework that combines the strengths of deep learning and human expertise; (2) the creation of a user-Friendly interface that allows domain experts to correct errors in real-time; (3) the demonstration of improved segmentation accuracy compared to traditional machine learning or manual annotation methods; and (4) the potential applicability of our framework across various medical imaging domains. Overall, this paper offers significant insights into the design and implementation of interactive segmentation frameworks and their ability to improve the accuracy of medical image analysis. It highlights the importance of integrating domain knowledge into the process and provides evidence that such collaboration can lead to better outcomes than relying solely on computational techniques.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to constructing prediction sets that do not rely on any distributional assumptions and simultaneously control the risk of over-prediction. Our method, called distribution-free, risk-controlling prediction sets (DFRCPS), is based on a non-parametric, data-driven approach that leverages the concept of conformal prediction. We demonstrate that DFRCPS can be used to construct prediction sets with a guaranteed level of confidence, while also providing a risk-controlling mechanism to prevent over-prediction. We evaluate the performance of DFRCPS through simulations and real-world case studies, comparing it to existing methods such as conformal prediction and Bayesian prediction. Our results show that DFRCPS outperforms these methods in terms of both accuracy and risk control, particularly in situations where the underlying distribution is complex or unknown. The key contribution of this paper is the development of a distribution-free, risk-controlling approach to prediction set construction that does not require any distributional assumptions. Our method provides a principled way to balance the trade-off between accuracy and risk control, and can be applied to a wide range of prediction problems, including classification, regression, and time-series forecasting. The paper is organized as follows: In Section 2, we review the related work on prediction sets and conformal prediction. In Section 3, we introduce the concept of DFRCPS and its construction method. In Section 4, we present the theoretical properties of DFRCPS, including its risk control and confidence guarantees. In Section 5, we evaluate the performance of DFRCPS through simulations and case studies. Finally, we conclude with a discussion of the results and future directions in Section 6. Overall, this paper provides a novel approach to prediction set construction that addresses the limitations of existing methods and provides a distribution-free, risk-controlling alternative. The proposed method has broad applications in fields such as machine learning, statistics, and decision-making under uncertainty.

title: Distribution-Free, Risk-Controlling Prediction Sets

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to constructing prediction sets that are distribution-free and risk-controlling. Existing methods for constructing prediction sets typically rely on assuming a specific distribution for the data, which can be limiting in many real-world applications where the underlying distribution is unknown or complex. Our proposed method, on the other hand, does not require any distributional assumptions and instead relies on a robust optimization framework to construct prediction sets with guaranteed coverage probabilities. Additionally, our approach allows for controlling the risk of the prediction set, ensuring that the error probability is upper bounded by a specified level. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through simulations and real-data experiments, showcasing its ability to provide accurate predictions while maintaining rigorous control over the risk level. We also compare our approach with existing state-of-the art methods, and show that it provides better performance in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. Moreover, we discuss the implementation details of our algorithm and illustrate how it can be applied to various machine learning models, including neural networks and decision trees. Our main contribution is thus a powerful tool for constructing reliable prediction sets that do not impose any distributional assumptions, enabling their application in diverse domains such as medical diagnosis, financial forecasting, and autonomous systems, among others. By providing a principled way to balance accuracy and risk, our work advances the field of uncertainty quantification and decision making under uncertainty.

Similarity learning is a technique that learns a similarity metric between images, which can be used for various tasks such as image retrieval, duplicate removal, and object recognition. The key idea behind DSD is to distill the similarity knowledge from a pre-trained teacher model into a smaller student model. However, achieving robust general medical image segmentation remains a challenging problem due to variations in image quality, modality, and anatomy. In this paper, we propose a novel approach towards robust general medical image segmentation, which combines the strengths of deep learning and traditional computer vision techniques. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to general medical image segmentation that leverages recent advances in deep learning and uncertainty quantification to achieve robust performance across various modalities and pathologies. Our method is based on a hybrid framework that combines the strengths of fully convolutional networks (FCNs) with the versatility of probabilistic graphical models (PGMs). First, we train an FCN to learn high-level features from a large dataset of labeled medical images. Then, we use these features as input to a PGM, which models the spatial relationships between pixels and enforces label consistency across neighboring regions. The PGM is trained using a variety of datasets with different modalities and diseases, allowing us to learn a robust representation of the underlying anatomy. To improve our model's ability to handle variations in image quality and artifacts, we incorporate uncertainty estimates into the segmentation process. Specifically, we use dropout regularization to induce aleatoric uncertainty during training, and combine it with epistemic uncertainty from the PGM's predictions. This allows us to generate high-quality segmentations even when dealing with noisy or low-resolution inputs. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets encompassing diverse medical imaging modalities, including CT, MRI, and US. The results demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and robustness. Furthermore, we perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the contributions of individual components and provide insights into the working mechanism of our proposed method. The main contribution of this work lies in its ability to effectively integrate prior knowledge and uncertainty information to achieve robust medical image segmentation. Our approach has important implications for clinical applications where accurate and reliable segmentations are essential, such as tumor detection and monitoring, organ segregation, and treatment planning.

We evaluate our approach on a large dataset of cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans and compare it to supervised and unsupervised baselines. text: In this paper, we present CHASE, a novel approach to visual tracking that leverages the power of differentiable neural architecture search (NAS) to optimize the tracking performance. The proposed method consists of two main components: a cell-level differentiable neural network, and a tracking loss function that is designed to optimize the tracking performance. The cell-level differentiable neural network is trained to learn a robust representation of the target object by optimizing a novel tracking loss function that encourages the network to produce a stable output in the presence of occlusions, illumination changes, and other challenges. The tracking loss function is designed to be differentiable with respect to the network's architecture, allowing us to use backpropagation to optimize the network's parameters. We use a combination of gradient descent and evolution strategies to search for the optimal architecture that maximizes the tracking performance. We evaluate CHASE on several challenging benchmarks, including the PASCAL Visual Object Tracking (VOT) dataset, the Temple-Color-Tracking (TCT) dataset, and the LaSalle-Color-Tracking (LCT) dataset. Our experiments show that CHASE outperforms several state-of-the-art tracking methods, including deep learning-based methods, and achieves a high success rate in various challenging scenarios. The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel differentiable neural architecture search method for visual tracking, which allows the network to adapt to changing object appearance and learn a robust representation of the target object. The proposed method has important implications for a wide range of applications, including object tracking in videos, autonomous driving, and robotics.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to weakly supervised segmentation, called Inter Extreme Points Geodesics (IEPG), which leverages the concept of geodesics in the space of probability distributions to segment objects in images. Unlike traditional segmentation methods that rely on strongly supervised learning or fully convolutional networks, IEPG only requires weak supervision in the form of bounding boxes around the objects of interest. The key insight behind IEPG is that the geodesic distance between the probability distributions of pixels in the image and the bounding box can be used to identify the pixels that belong to the object. We propose an efficient algorithm to compute the geodesic distance and use it to optimize a segmentation mask that separates the object from the background. We evaluate the performance of IEPG on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and show that it achieves competitive segmentation accuracy compared to state-of-the-art methods, while requiring significantly less annotated data. We also demonstrate the versatility of IEPG by applying it to various segmentation tasks, such as instance segmentation, semantic segmentation, and video segmentation. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We introduce a novel approach to weakly supervised segmentation based on inter extreme points geodesics, which only requires weak supervision in the form of bounding boxes. * We propose an efficient algorithm to compute the geodesic distance between probability distributions and use it to optimize a segmentation mask. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of IEPG on several benchmark datasets and show that it achieves competitive segmentation accuracy with significantly less annotated data. * We highlight the versatility of IEPG by applying it to various segmentation tasks, including instance segmentation, semantic segmentation, and video segmentation. Overall, this paper provides a new perspective on weakly supervised segmentation and offers a promising solution for segmenting objects in images with minimal annotation effort.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to weakly supervised segmentation called Inter Extreme Points Geodesics (IEPG). This method leverages the concept of extreme points, which are points in the image that have the highest similarity to the boundary of the object of interest, and geodesic distances, which provide a metric for comparing the similarity between pixels. The IEPG algorithm consists of two main steps: 1) extracting extreme points from the image using a modified Canny edge detector, and 2) computing the geodesic distance matrix between all pairs of extreme points. The resulting matrix is then used to construct a graph, where each node represents an extreme point and edges connect points with similar geodesic distances. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes. Our experiments show that IEPG outperforms traditional weakly supervised segmentation methods, such as random walker and GCUT, in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Additionally, we provide a comprehensive analysis of the impact of different parameters on the performance of IEPG, providing insights into how to optimize its use in practice. Our work has important implications for applications where obtaining large amounts of annotated data is difficult or expensive, such as medical imaging or satellite imagery. By leveraging weak annotations, IEPG enables accurate segmentation without requiring a large amount of manual annotation effort.

Our method uses adversarial networks to learn a context-based segmentation model that can accurately separate objects from their surroundings, even in cases where the object boundaries are not well-defined or there is significant background noise. Traditional image segmentation methods rely on hand-crafted features and heuristics, which often lead to suboptimal performance when dealing with complex scenes or low-quality data. Moreover, obtaining high-quality labels for training these models can be time-consuming and expensive. In contrast, our approach leverages recent advances in deep learning to learn a segments representation directly from the input data, without relying on manual feature engineering. The key insight behind Points2Polygons is to use adversarial training to learn a polygon-based representation of the image, where each polygon corresponds to a coherent region of the scene. This allows us to effectively capture object boundaries and handle irregularly shaped objects, while also propagating information across neighboring regions to produce more accurate segmentations. We evaluate Points2Polygons on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, comparing it against state-of-the-art segmentation algorithms. Our results show that our approach significantly outperforms traditional segmentation methods, especially in situations where the label quality is poor or there is significant contextual variation between training and test data. Overall, this work represents an important step towards developing robust and efficient image segmentation techniques that can operate effectively in real-world scenarios. The proposed method has broad applications in computer vision, robotics, medical imaging, and other domains where image understanding is critical.

text: In this paper, we present a comprehensive analysis of exact inference in graphs from the degree-4 sum-of-squares hierarchy. We explore the capabilities and limitations of this powerful inference technique, which has been widely used in various fields, including computer vision, machine learning, and social network analysis. Our work provides a thorough understanding of the degree-4 sum-of-squares hierarchy, including its theoretical foundations, computational complexity, and applications to graphical models. We demonstrate the versatility of this approach by applying it to a variety of graphical models, including Bayesian networks, Markov random fields, and Gaussian graphical models. Our main contributions include the development of novel inference algorithms that leverage the degree-4 sum-of-squares hierarchy to perform exact inference in graphs. We also derive tight computational complexity bounds for these algorithms, providing insights into their scalability and performance. Our experimental evaluation on several real-world datasets demonstrates the effectiveness and efficiency of our proposed methods. We show that they can accurately perform exact inference in large-scale graphs, outperforming existing methods in terms of computational speed and scalability. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including image segmentation, natural language processing, and recommendation systems. It also provides a foundation for future research in graphical models and exact inference, opening up new avenues for developing more powerful and efficient inference techniques. In summary, our paper offers a comprehensive view of exact inference in graphs from the degree-4 sum-of-squares hierarchy, providing novel algorithms, computational complexity analysis, and experimental evaluation. Our work has significant implications for both theoretical and practical applications, advancing the state-of-the-art in graphical models and exact inference.

The paper also explores the connections between the SOS hierarchy and other approaches to exact inference, such as belief propagation and linear programming relaxations. Finally, the authors investigate the relationship between the SOS hierarchy and the statistical properties of graphs. They prove lower bounds on the size of the smallest witnesses required by the SOS hierarchy to refute independence claims in certain families of graphs, and provide evidence for the conjecture that the SOS hierarchy is optimal for testing independence in some regimes. Overall, this work provides a thorough view of the power and limitations of the degree-4 SOS hierarchy for exact inference in graphs, and establishes it as a fundamental tool for future research in this area.

text: This paper presents a novel approach for high-resolution segmentation of tooth root fuzzy edges based on polynomial curve fitting with landmark detection. The proposed method aims to accurately separate the tooth roots from the surrounding dental tissues in digital dental images. Existing methods for tooth root segmentation are often limited by their reliance on hand-crafted features, low accuracy, and high computational complexity. In contrast, our approach leverages the power of polynomial curve fitting to accurately model the fuzzy edges of tooth roots and detect landmarks that define the boundary between the tooth roots and the surrounding tissues. The proposed method consists of three stages: 1) preprocessing, 2) polynomial curve fitting, and 3) landmark detection. In the preprocessing stage, the input image is filtered to enhance the contrast and remove noise. In the polynomial curve fitting stage, a cubic polynomial curve is fitted to the fuzzy edge of the tooth root using a non-linear least squares optimization algorithm. The curve is then used to separate the tooth root from the surrounding tissues. Finally, landmarks are detected by identifying the points on the curve that correspond to the maximum gradient values. The proposed method was evaluated on a dataset of 20 digital dental images using a leave-one-out cross-validation scheme. The results show that our method outperforms existing methods in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and robustness. The mean absolute error was found to be 0.15 mm, and the processing time was less than 30 seconds per image. The proposed method has important implications for dental image analysis and related applications such as orthodontic treatment planning, dental implant planning, and oral disease diagnosis. With its high accuracy, efficiency, and robustness, the method has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of dental procedures and contribute to better patient outcomes.

text: This paper presents a novel approach for high-resolution segmentation of tooth root fuzzy edges using polynomial curve fitting with landmark detection. The proposed method utilizes the theory of algebraic curves to accurately model and segment the complex boundaries of teeth roots in medical images. Existing methods for tooth root segmentation suffer from limitations such as low accuracy, sensitivity to noise, and difficulty in handling ambiguous or unclear boundaries. In contrast, our proposed approach leverages the power of polynomial curve fitting to precisely capture the underlying shape and structure of tooth roots, even in the presence of image noise or irregularities. The proposed method consists of three stages: (1) preprocessing, where the input image is enhanced and filtered to reduce noise and enhance contrast; (2) landmark detection, where strategically located points along the boundary of the tooth root are identified and used as control points for the polynomial curve fit; and (3) segmentation, where the fitted polynomial curve is employed to separate the tooth root from the surrounding tissue. The performance of our method was evaluated using a dataset of 40 dental cone beam computed tomography (CBCT) scans, with manual annotations by experts serving as ground truth. Quantitative evaluation metrics included mean absolute error, intersection over union (IoU), and Dice similarity coefficient. Our results demonstrate an average IoU score of 95.6% and an average Dice similarity coefficient of 87.3%, outperforming several state-of-the-art approaches. This investigation establishes polynomial curve fitting with landmark detection as a reliable and efficient means for high-resolution segmentation of tooth root fuzzy edges. The proposed technique has significant implications for improving accuracy in various applications within dentistry and oral healthcare, including computer-aided diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring of orthodontic and periodontal treatments.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to active contours, called deep active contours using locally controlled distance vector flow (DAC-LCDVF). Active contours, also known as snakes or active contours, are a popular method for image segmentation and object recognition. They are based on the idea of evolving a contour to minimize an energy functional that incorporates information about the image and the desired boundary. However, traditional active contour methods can be limited by their reliance on hand-crafted features and their inability to handle complex, high-dimensional data. Our proposed method, DAC-LCDVF, addresses these limitations by combining the power of deep learning with the flexibility of active contours. We use a deep neural network to learn a representation of the image that captures its relevant features, and then use this representation to define a locally controlled distance vector flow (LCDVF) that drives the evolution of the contour. The LCDVF is a generalization of the traditional active contour energy functional, and allows for more flexible and efficient optimization of the contour. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DAC-LCDVF on several challenging image segmentation tasks, including segmenting medical images and object recognition in natural images. Our results show that DAC-LCDVF outperforms traditional active contour methods and other state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. We also provide a thorough analysis of the contributions of the different components of our method, and show that the combination of deep learning and LCDVF is essential for achieving high-quality segmentation results. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including medical imaging, computer vision, and robotics. By providing a powerful and flexible tool for image segmentation and object recognition, DAC-LCDVF has the potential to enable new applications and improve the accuracy and efficiency of existing ones.

Traditional active contour models rely on global optimization techniques that can be computationally expensive and may not effectively capture complex image structures. Our proposed method utilizes a locally controlled distance vector flow to drive the evolution of the contour, allowing for efficient and flexible segmentation of images with varying levels of complexity. We propose a new energy functional that incorporates both image information and user interaction, enabling real-time feedback and control over the segmentation process. The energy functional combines traditional image intensity terms with a novel distancevectorflow term that encourages the contour to move towards regions of high gradient magnitude. This allows the model to adaptively adjust its behavior based on local image features, leading to improved accuracy and efficiency in segmenting complex objects. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed method through experiments on several benchmark datasets, including medical imaging and natural scenes. Our results show that our method outperforms traditional active contour models in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency, while also providing more intuitive and user-friendly interactions. We further validate our approach by applying it to real-world scenarios such as tumor segmentation in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. Overall, our work presents a significant advancement in the field of active contour models, providing a powerful tool for image segmentation tasks that can be used in a variety of applications. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to referring segmentation that leverages cross-modal progressive comprehension to improve object segmentation in images. Referring segmentation is the task of segregating objects in an image based on their mentions in natural language descriptions. Existing approaches have relied heavily on visual features and suffer from limitations in handling complex scenes with multiple objects or non-descriptive language. Our proposed method addresses these challenges by incorporating cognitive theories of human language understanding and vision, which posits that humans use language to guide attention and eye movements when describing visual stimuli. We propose a two-stage framework that first generates region proposals using a DeepLabv3+ model and then refines them using a Cross-Modal Attention Module (CMAM). The CMAM integrates information from both modalities through multi-layer perceptron networks, allowing it to learn how to weigh the importance of different words in relation to the visual context. We evaluate our model on three benchmark datasets and show significant improvements over existing methods, especially in cases where the language description contains incomplete or ambiguous information. Our experiments also provide insights into the role of each modality in referring segmentation, revealing that both visual and linguistic cues play crucial roles in achieving optimal performance. Furthermore, we analyze the effectiveness of our attention mechanism and demonstrate its ability to focus on relevant regions and dismiss irrelevant ones, even in presence of distractors. Overall, this work represents an important step towards developing more accurate and robust real-time referring segmentation systems that can enable improved scene understanding capabilities for various applications such as robotics, autonomous vehicles, and virtual assistants.

text: Abstract: Food image segmentation is a fundamental task in various applications, including nutrition analysis, meal planning, and food recognition. Despite the growing interest in this task, there is a lack of a large-scale benchmark dataset that can evaluate the performance of food image segmentation algorithms. In this paper, we introduce a large-scale benchmark for food image segmentation, which consists of 10,000 images with diverse food categories, backgrounds, and styles. The dataset is annotated with pixel-level segmentation masks, providing a comprehensive evaluation of segmentation performance. We also propose a set of evaluation metrics that take into account the unique challenges of food image segmentation, such as the variability in food appearance, the complexity of food structures, and the presence of artifacts. Our benchmark dataset and evaluation metrics provide a standardized platform for researchers and practitioners to compare and improve their segmentation algorithms. We perform experiments on several state-of-the-art segmentation algorithms, demonstrating the effectiveness of our benchmark and highlighting the challenges and opportunities in food image segmentation. Our work contributes to the development of more accurate and robust food image segmentation algorithms, which can have a significant impact on various applications in the food industry, healthcare, and beyond.

Our approach has significant implications for applications where labeled data is scarce or difficult to obtain, such as medical imaging, robotics, and autonomous vehicles. By harnessing the power of unlabeled data, our method enables more efficient and effective semantic segmentation, leading to improved overall system performance.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for polyp localisation in colonoscopy videos using ensemble learning and particle swarm optimization. Our approach combines the predictions of multiple classifiers with different confidence levels to improve the accuracy of polyp detection. We first train a set of classifiers on a dataset of colonoscopy images and then use their predictions to generate a set of candidate polyp locations. We then apply a comprehensive learning particle swarm optimization algorithm to optimize the locations of the candidates based on their confidence levels and spatial relationships. The proposed approach is evaluated on a dataset of 100 colonoscopy videos and shows superior performance compared to traditional machine learning methods. The results show that our approach can accurately detect polyps in real-time with an accuracy rate of 96.5%. In addition, we perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of our approach and demonstrate that the combination of ensemble learning and particle swarm optimization is crucial for achieving high accuracy. Our approach has the potential to improve the efficiency and accuracy of polyp detection in colonoscopy procedures, which can help doctors to quickly identify and remove polyps, reducing the risk of colon cancer. The proposed approach can also be applied to other medical image analysis tasks where accurate detection of small objects is critical.

title: Ensemble Learning based on Classifier Prediction Confidence and Comprehensive Learning Particle Swarm Optimisation for polyp localisation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to ensemble learning for polyp localization in colonoscopy images. Our method combines classifier prediction confidence and comprehensive learning particle swarm optimization (PSO) to improve the accuracy of polyp detection. First, we train multiple deep learning models as base classifiers to predict the presence or absence of polyps in colonoscopy images. We then calculate the prediction confidence of each classifier and use it as a weighting factor to combine their outputs. This ensures that the final prediction is made by combining the predictions from all classifiers while giving more weightage to the ones with higher prediction confidences. Next, we employ PSO to optimize the hyperparameters of the ensemble model. PSO is a population-based optimization algorithm that simulates the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. It searchs for optimal solutions by iteratively updating the positions of particles in a population, which correspond to the hyperparameters of our model. By using comprehensive learning PSO, we are able to simultaneously optimize both the weights and the biases of the ensemble model. We evaluate our approach on a benchmark dataset consisting of 1000 high-resolution color images of colonoscopies. The results show that our proposed ensemble learning strategy significantly outperforms any single classifier, achieving an improvement in detection accuracy of up to 23%. Additionally, the visualization of the learned features reveals meaningful patterns in the data that can help doctors and researchers better understand the characteristics of polyps and their precursors. Our findings have important implications for improving the efficiency and accuracy of computer-aided diagnosis systems in gastroenterology. The proposed method has the potential to support clinical decision-making and reduce the risk of missing polyps during colonoscopic examinations.

text: Pneumothorax, a life-threatening condition characterized by the presence of air in the pleural space, is a common complication in critically ill patients. Accurate and timely diagnosis of pneumothorax is crucial for effective treatment and prevention of further complications. However, current diagnostic methods, such as chest X-rays and computed tomography (CT) scans, are limited by their low sensitivity and specificity. In this paper, we propose a deep learning-based image segmentation approach for the accurate detection and segmentation of pneumothorax in chest X-ray images. Our proposed method utilizes a convolutional neural network (CNN) to learn the features and patterns in chest X-ray images and segment the pleural space, lung tissue, and pneumothorax. We evaluated the performance of our proposed method on a dataset of 100 chest X-ray images, including 50 images with pneumothorax and 50 images without pneumothorax. The results showed that our method achieved an accuracy of 96.5% in detecting pneumothorax, with a sensitivity of 93.1% and a specificity of 98.1%. Additionally, we compared the performance of our method with that of radiologists and found that our method outperformed human interpretation in terms of accuracy and efficiency. Our study demonstrates the potential of deep learning-based image segmentation for the accurate diagnosis of pneumothorax. The use of CNNs can help radiologists and clinicians to quickly and accurately diagnose pneumothorax, leading to timely and effective treatment. Future studies can focus on refining the algorithm and expanding the dataset to improve the generalizability of the method. Keywords: pneumothorax, image segmentation, deep learning, chest X-ray, convolutional neural network.

text: Few-shot segmentation is a challenging task in computer vision that involves segmenting novel objects or classes with only a few labeled examples. Most existing segmentation models rely on supervised learning, which requires a large amount of labeled data to achieve good performance. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called self-guided and cross-guided learning, which enables few-shot segmentation using both self-supervised and cross-supervised learning. Our approach consists of two main components: a self-guided learning module and a cross-guided learning module. The self-guided learning module uses a self-supervised loss function to train the segmentation model on unlabeled data, while the cross-guided learning module uses a cross-supervised loss function to fine-tune the model on labeled data from other related tasks or domains. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes. Experiments show that our self-guided and cross-guided learning approach outperforms traditional supervised learning methods, and achieves state-of-the-art performance on few-shot segmentation tasks. We also perform ablation studies to analyze the contributions of different components of our approach, and show that both self-guided and cross-guided learning are important for achieving good performance. Our work has important implications for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce or difficult to obtain. By leveraging large amounts of unlabeled data and related tasks or domains, our approach can learn to segment novel objects or classes with only a few labeled examples, which can significantly reduce the need for manual annotation and improve the efficiency of segmentation model development.

title: Self-Guided and Cross-Guided Learning for Few-Shot Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to few-shot segmentation that combines self-guided and cross-guided learning. Few-shot segmentation is a challenging task due to the limited number of training examples available for each class, which can lead to overfitting and poor generalization to unseen data. To address this challenge, we introduce a framework that leverages both self-guided and cross-guided information to improve the segmentation performance. The proposed method consists of two main components: (1) a self-guided module that uses intra-modal similarity to learn a robust representation of each class, and (2) a cross-guided module that utilizes inter-modal correspondence to refine the segmentation results. The self-guided module encourages the model to learn a compact and discriminative feature space where samples from the same class are close together, while the cross-guided module aligns the features across different modalities to enhance the segmentation accuracy. We evaluate our framework on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and demonstrate its superiority compared to state-of-the-art few-shot segmentation methods. Our experiments show that the combination of self-guided and cross-guided learning leads to improved performance in terms of segmentation accuracy and robustness to variations in the input data. We also conduct an ablation study to analyze the contribution of each component and provide insights into the working mechanism of our framework. Our work has important implications for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce or difficult to obtain. By enabling accurate segmentation with fewer annotations, our approach has the potential to reduce the cost and increase the efficiency of segmentation tasks in various domains such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for image segmentation using scribbles as supervision. Our method, called Learning to Segment from Scribbles using Multi-scale Adversarial Attention Gates (LASSO), leverages the power of adversarial attention mechanisms to effectively learn from scribble annotations. Existing approaches for segmentation from scribbles often rely on simplistic heuristics or labor-intensive manual annotation of large datasets. In contrast, LASSO uses a multi-scale adversarial attention mechanism to learn a robust representation of the image that can be used for accurate segmentation. Our approach consists of two main components: a scribble encoder and a segmentation decoder. The scribble encoder maps the scribble annotations to a set of attention weights, which are then used to guide the segmentation decoder. The decoder consists of a series of convolutional layers that refine the segmentation output at multiple scales. We evaluate LASSO on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods for segmentation from scribbles. Our experiments demonstrate that LASSO is able to accurately segment objects even when the scribble annotations are noisy or incomplete. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the different components of our approach, providing insights into the role of each component in the segmentation process. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach for image segmentation from scribbles using multi-scale adversarial attention gates. * We introduce a scribble encoder that maps scribble annotations to attention weights, which are used to guide the segmentation decoder. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of LASSO on several benchmark datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods for segmentation from scribbles. * We perform ablation studies to analyze the contribution of each component of our approach, providing insights into the role of each component in the segmentation process. Overall, our work has important implications for image segmentation tasks, particularly in scenarios where annotated data is scarce or difficult

text: This paper presents a novel approach to image segmentation, Learning to Segment from Scribbles using Multi-scale Adversarial Attention Gates (MSAAG). The proposed method leverages the power of adversarial attention mechanisms to learn a robust and accurate segmentation model from sparse annotated data. Existing methods rely heavily on fully supervised learning, requiring large amounts of manually labeled data. In contrast, MSAAG uses scribble annotations, which are significantly easier and faster to obtain, yet still provide valuable information about the location of objects in an image. By incorporating multi-scale features and adaptive attention gates, our model is able to effectively focus on the most relevant regions of an image and learn rich semantic representations. The key innovation of MSAAG lies in its ability to integrate spatial and channel-wise attention into a single module. This enables the model to learn both local and global context, leading to improved performance compared to traditional attention-based approaches. Additionally, we introduce a new loss function that encourages the model to produce smooth and consistent segmentation masks, further enhancing overall accuracy. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MSAAG outperforms state-of-the-art weakly supervised segmentation models on several benchmark datasets. Our approach achieves superior performance with fewer annotated examples, exhibiting its capability to efficiently leverage limited annotation resources. Moreover, we perform ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components within the MSAAG framework, providing insights into the contributions of each component to the overall performance gain. In summary, this work introduces a novel image segmentation approach that leverages scribble annotations and advances in adversarial attention mechanisms to achieve high accuracy with minimal manual effort. The proposed MSAAG model has important implications for applications where labeling data is costly or challenging, such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for segmenting fluorescence microscopy images using deep neural networks. Our approach leverages the concept of meta-structures, which are high-level abstractions of the underlying image structure that can be learned from a set of training images. By incorporating meta-structures into the network architecture, we enable the network to learn a hierarchical representation of the image data that can be used for accurate segmentation. We evaluate our approach on several datasets of fluorescence microscopy images and demonstrate its superior performance compared to traditional segmentation methods. Our experiments show that the use of meta-structures improves the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation process, and enables the network to generalize well to new images. The main contributions of this paper are: * The introduction of meta-structures as a new concept for segmenting fluorescence microscopy images using deep neural networks. * The development of a novel network architecture that incorporates meta-structures to learn a hierarchical representation of the image data. * The evaluation of our approach on several datasets, demonstrating its superior performance and generalization ability compared to traditional segmentation methods. Our work has important implications for the analysis of fluorescence microscopy images, which is a critical tool in many biological and biomedical applications. The proposed method can help to automate the segmentation process, reduce the amount of manual effort required, and improve the accuracy and consistency of the results.
