title: Deep Neural Networks Learn Meta-Structures to Segment Fluorescence Microscopy Images

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for segmenting fluorescence microscopy images using deep neural networks. We propose a meta-learning algorithm that learns to recognize and segment different types of structures in fluorescence microscopy images. Our approach leverages the fact that different structures in these images have distinct patterns and arrangements, which can be learned by a deep neural network. We evaluate our method on several datasets of diverse fluorescence microscopy images and show that it outperforms traditional image segmentation techniques. Specifically, we demonstrate that our method accurately segments various subcellular structures such as mitochondria, lysosomes, and nuclei in cell samples, as well as cells and tissues in whole mount images. Furthermore, we show that our method is able to generalize well to new images and can handle variations in imaging conditions, staining intensities, and artifacts. Our main contribution is the development of a deep learning architecture that learns meta-structures, which are abstractions of the underlying structure patterns in fluorescence microscopy images. This allows the network to learn from a small set of training examples and generalize to unseen data. We also introduce a novel loss function that takes into account the spatial relationships between neighboring pixels, improving accuracy and robustness. The proposed method has potential applications in various fields, including biology, medicine, and materials science, where fluorescence microscopy is commonly used to study complex sample morphologies at high resolution. Overall, this work advances the state-of-the-art in fluorescence microscopy image analysis and opens up possibilities for new discoveries in life sciences research.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to unsupervised medical image segmentation using contrastive registration. Contrastive registration is a technique that aligns images by maximizing the similarity between their intensity patterns, without relying on explicit correspondences or annotated data. We apply this technique to the task of medical image segmentation, where the goal is to separate the image into its constituent parts or structures. Our approach consists of two main components: (1) a contrastive registration model that aligns images, and (2) a segmentation model that separates the aligned images into their constituent parts. We train the contrastive registration model using a large dataset of unlabeled medical images, and then use the aligned images to train the segmentation model. We evaluate our approach on several datasets of medical images, including MRI scans, CT scans, and ultrasound images. Our results show that our method outperforms traditional unsupervised segmentation methods, and achieves high accuracy on par with supervised methods that require annotated data. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in handling various types of medical images, including images with different modalities and resolutions. Our main contribution is the introduction of contrastive registration for unsupervised medical image segmentation, which does not require annotated data and can handle diverse types of medical images. Our approach has the potential to significantly reduce the cost and time required for manual annotation, and can enable the development of AI models for medical image analysis in a wider range of applications.

text: Title: Contrastive Registration for Unsupervised Medical Image Segmentation Abstract: Unsupervised medical image segmentation is a fundamental task in medical imaging analysis, which aims to separate different structures or tissues within an image without any prior knowledge of class labels. However, achieving accurate segmentation results remains challenging due to factors such as varying image intensities, artifacts, and subtle boundaries. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a novel approach called contrastive registration for unsupervised medical image segmentation. By aligning images with a similarity metric that captures the spatial relationships between pixels, we encourage the model to learn meaningful features that can be used for subsequent segmentation tasks. The proposed method consists of two main components: (1) contrastive registration, which aligns images by maximizing their similarities while preserving their inherent spatial relationships; and (2) feature learning, where the aligned images are passed through a encoder-decoder architecture to obtain high-level features. These features are then utilized for segmentation using a clustering algorithm. Extensive experiments on various medical datasets demonstrate the superior performance of our approach compared to state-of-the-art methods, including traditional registration techniques and deep learning-based segmentation models. Our key contributions include: * The introduction of contrastive registration for unsupervised medical image segmentation, which leverages the power of self-similarity to improve feature representation. * The development of a robust feature learning framework that integrates contrastive registration with a hierarchical encoder-decoder architecture. * Comprehensive evaluations on multiple datasets, highlighting the effectiveness and generalizability of our technique across diverse medical image modalities. Overall, this work has significant implications for improving the accuracy and efficiency of unsupervised medical image segmentation processes, enabling better diagnosis and treatment planning in clinical settings.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for estimating food portion sizes from monocular images using cross-domain feature adaptation. Existing methods for food portion estimation rely on manual annotation of portion sizes, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our approach leverages large-scale datasets of images with object annotations to learn a model that can accurately estimate portion sizes from a single monocular image. We address the challenge of adapting features from object-annotated images to the target task of portion estimation by proposing a domain adaptation technique that aligns features across domains using a shared embedding space. Our approach consists of two stages: 1) pre-training a feature extractor on a large-scale object detection dataset, and 2) fine-tuning the feature extractor on a smaller dataset of images with annotated portion sizes. We evaluate our approach on several datasets with varying levels of complexity and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods for food portion estimation. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of cross-domain feature adaptation for addressing the challenge of limited annotated data in the target domain. We also perform a thorough analysis of the contribution of different components of our approach, providing insights into the importance of domain adaptation and the choice of feature extractor architecture. Our work has important implications for the development of automated food portion estimation systems, which can help address issues related to food waste, nutrition, and health. The proposed approach can be applied to a wide range of applications, including food portion estimation in restaurants, cafeterias, and home environments.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semi-supervised medical image segmentation, which leverages the concept of dual-task consistency to improve segmentation accuracy. Our approach is based on a deep neural network architecture that is trained on a combination of labeled and unlabeled data, where the labeled data is used to learn the segmentation task and the unlabeled data is used to learn a auxiliary task. The key idea is to encourage the network to produce consistent outputs for both tasks, which leads to improved segmentation performance. We evaluate our approach on several publicly available medical image datasets, including brain tumor segmentation and lung segmentation. The results show that our method outperforms traditional semi-supervised learning methods, and achieves performance comparable to fully supervised learning methods, but with significantly less labeled data. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach to semi-supervised medical image segmentation through dual-task consistency * A deep neural network architecture that integrates the segmentation and auxiliary tasks * An effective way to leverage large amounts of unlabeled data to improve segmentation accuracy The paper is organized as follows: In section 2, we review related work on semi-supervised learning and medical image segmentation. title: Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation through Dual-task Consistency

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to semi-supervised medical image segmentation, which is a challenging problem due to the limited availability of labeled data. Our method leverages the concept of dual-task consistency to improve segmentation accuracy. Specifically, we train two separate neural networks for image segmentation and boundary detection, respectively, using both labeled and unlabeled data. We then enforce consistency between the predictions of these two networks, encouraging them to produce identical outputs for the same input images. This consistency regularization term helps to propagate knowledge from the well-performing boundary detection task to the segmentation task, leading to improved performance. We evaluate our proposed method on several benchmark datasets, including CelebFaces, LUNA16, and MM2M, and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the art semi-supervised segmentation methods. Additionally, we perform ablation studies to analyze the impact of different components of our framework, providing insights into the effectiveness of the dual-task consistency approach. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: (1) we introduce a novel semi-supervised learning framework for medical image segmentation that combines dual-task consistency with adversarial training; (2) we demonstrate the efficacy of our method on various benchmark datasets, outperforming existing semi-supervised segmentation approaches; (3) we provide thorough ablation studies to investigate the role of each component in our framework, shedding light on the mechanism behind its success. The remainder of this paper is organized as follows: Section II reviews relevant works on semi-supervised medical image segmentation and dual-task learning. Section III describes our proposed method in detail, including network architectures, loss functions, and training strategies. Section IV presents experimental results and analysis, whileSection V concludes the paper and highlights future research directions.

text: In this paper, we propose an unsupervised domain adaptation network with a category-centric prototype aligner for biomedical image segmentation. The proposed method aims to address the challenge of segmenting images from unseen domains, which is a critical problem in many biomedical applications. Existing domain adaptation methods for image segmentation typically require annotated data from the target domain, which can be difficult and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our proposed method uses an unsupervised domain adaptation approach that does not require any annotated data from the target domain. The key innovation of our method is the use of a category-centric prototype aligner, which aligns the prototypes of the source domain categories with the prototypes of the target domain categories. This alignment is achieved by minimizing the distance between the prototypes of the corresponding categories across domains. The aligned prototypes are then used to adapt the feature extractor and classifier of the segmentation model to the target domain. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets, including a dataset of brain tumor images and a dataset of retinal fundus images. The results show that our method outperforms several state-of-the-art domain adaptation methods for image segmentation, including methods that require annotated data from the target domain. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose an unsupervised domain adaptation network with a category-centric prototype aligner for biomedical image segmentation. * We demonstrate that our method does not require annotated data from the target domain and still achieves better performance than state-of-the-art methods that require annotated data. * We show that our method is effective in adapting the segmentation model to new domains with different distributions, which is critical in many biomedical applications. Overall, our proposed method has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy of biomedical image segmentation in situations where annotated data from the target domain is not available.

text: This paper proposes an unsupervised domain adaptation approach for biomedical image segmentation, which leverages a category-centric prototype aligner to effectively adapt models across different domains. The proposed method consists of two main components: 1) a feature extractor that learns to extract domain-invariant features from input images, and 2) a prototype aligner that aligns prototypes from source and target domains using a categorization-based alignment strategy. The prototype aligner uses a novel loss function that encourages the model to learn a robust representation space where prototypes from different categories are well-separated and easily distinguishable. Moreover, the aligner integrates a spatial attention mechanism to focus on the most discriminative regions of the images, which improves the alignment process and reduces the impact of irrelevant information. Extensive experiments conducted on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method compared to state-of-the-art domain adaptation techniques for biomedical image segmentation tasks. Specifically, we achieve notable improvements in segmentation accuracy when adapting models trained on simulations or synthetic data to real-world data, indicating the effectiveness of our approach in addressing the challenging domain shift problem. Our work has significant implications for medical imaging applications, as it enables the development of robust machine learning models capable of accurately segmenting diverse types of biomedical images, even when only limited annotated data is available for a particular domain. With its ability to adapt models across different domains without requiring labeled target data, our technique paves the way toward more reliable, efficient, and cost-effective image segmentation solutions for various clinical settings.

text: Medical image segmentation is a fundamental task in healthcare, which involves identifying and isolating specific structures or features of interest in medical images. Deep learning techniques have revolutionized the field of medical image segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art performance in various tasks. However, the success of deep learning models relies heavily on the availability of large amounts of labeled data, which can be challenging and expensive to obtain in the medical domain. In this review paper, we explore the recent advances in deep network models for medical image segmentation with limited supervision. We discuss the challenges associated with medical image segmentation, the importance of limited supervision, and the different approaches to segmentation with limited supervision. We also provide a comprehensive overview of deep network models that have been proposed for medical image segmentation with limited supervision, including their strengths, weaknesses, and performance. Our review covers a range of deep network models, including fully convolutional networks (FCNs), U-Net, and its variants, as well as attention-based models. We also discuss the use of transfer learning, data augmentation, and semi-supervised learning techniques to improve the performance of deep network models in medical image segmentation tasks. Our analysis highlights the potential of deep network models for medical image segmentation with limited supervision, and the need for further research in this area to overcome the challenges associated with limited labeled data. We also identify future research directions, including the development of new architectures and techniques that can better handle limited supervision, and the integration of domain knowledge and prior information into deep learning models. Overall, this review provides a comprehensive overview of the current state of the art in medical image segmentation with limited supervision, and serves as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners working in this field.

Our review highlights the potential of these methods in various medical imaging modalities, such as computed tomography (CT), magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and positron emission tomography (PET). text: This paper proposes a novel approach to food image segmentation, which is a fundamental step in various applications such as nutrition analysis, meal planning, and food recognition. Existing methods for food image segmentation primarily rely on class-specific models that are trained on individual classes (e.g., rice, meat, vegetables) separately, resulting in limited generalization ability to unseen classes or variations in cooking styles. In this work, we introduce a saliency-aware class-agnostic food image segmentation method that simultaneously separates all present classes in an image without requiring explicit classification. Our approach leverages a self-paced learning strategy that gradually refines the model's sensitivity to different levels of salient features. The core idea is to iteratively identify and emphasize the most discriminative regions within each image while suppressing less relevant areas. By doing so, our model can efficiently capture intrinsic structures among diverse food categories and handle occlusions, clutters, and uneven illumination. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation using nonparametric clustering. Traditional image segmentation methods rely on parametric models, which can be limited by their assumptions about the underlying data distribution. Nonparametric clustering, on the other hand, does not make any assumptions about the data distribution and can capture more complex patterns and structures. We propose a nonparametric clustering algorithm that uses a combination of density estimation and graph-based clustering to segment images. The algorithm first estimates the density of the pixel intensities in the image using a nonparametric density estimation technique. The density estimate is then used to construct a graph, where each pixel is a node, and the edges represent the similarity between the pixel intensities. The graph is then partitioned into clusters using a graph-based clustering algorithm. We evaluate the proposed method on several benchmark datasets and compare it to traditional parametric segmentation methods. The results show that our nonparametric clustering approach outperforms the parametric methods in terms of segmentation accuracy and robustness to noise. We also demonstrate the versatility of our approach by applying it to various image segmentation tasks, including object recognition, medical image segmentation, and video segmentation. The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a nonparametric clustering approach to image segmentation, which does not make any assumptions about the data distribution and can capture complex patterns and structures. The proposed method has important implications for applications where accurate image segmentation is critical, such as object recognition, medical imaging, and video analysis.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation using nonparametric clustering. Traditional methods of image segmentation rely on parametric models, such as Gaussian mixture models or Bayesian inference, which can be limited by their assumptions about the underlying distribution of the data. Nonparametric clustering methods, on the other hand, do not make any prior assumptions about the number of clusters or the shape of the clusters, and have been shown to be effective in a variety of applications. We apply nonparametric clustering algorithms, specifically the K-means algorithm and the Gustafson-Kessel algorithm, to the task of image segmentation. We evaluate the performance of these algorithms on several benchmark datasets, including synthetic images with known clustering structures and real-world images from various domains. Our results show that nonparametric clustering methods outperform traditional parametric methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. In addition, we explore the effect of different distance metrics on the performance of nonparametric clustering algorithms. We demonstrate that choosing an appropriate distance metric can significantly improve the quality of the obtained clusters. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of varying the number of iterations and the initial placement of the cluster centers on the final segmentation result. Our findings suggest that nonparametric clustering is a promising approach to image segmentation, offering advantages in terms of flexibility, scalability, and robustness to noise. The proposed method has important implications for a wide range of applications, including object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding in computer vision.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for semantic segmentation of point clouds called boundary-aware geometric encoding (BAGE). Point clouds are 3D collections of points that can be difficult to segment due to their unstructured and sparse nature. Existing methods for point cloud segmentation often rely on learned features or hand-crafted heuristics, which can be limited in their ability to capture complex geometric relationships between points. Our approach, BAGE, addresses this limitation by introducing a new way of encoding point clouds that takes into account the geometric structure of the data. Specifically, we define a boundary-aware encoding function that captures the proximity of each point to the boundary of the object being segmented. This encoding function is combined with a learned neural network to produce a segmentation of the point cloud. We evaluate BAGE on several publicly available point cloud datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of our approach, demonstrating the importance of the boundary-aware encoding function and the benefit of combining it with a learned neural network. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach for semantic segmentation of point clouds called boundary-aware geometric encoding (BAGE) * A new way of encoding point clouds that takes into account the geometric structure of the data, including the proximity of each point to the boundary of the object being segmented * State-of-the-art segmentation performance on several publicly available point cloud datasets * A thorough analysis of the effectiveness of different components of our approach, demonstrating the importance of the boundary-aware encoding function and the benefit of combining it with a learned neural network. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for unsupervised domain adaptation on hip joint bone segmentation tasks, called Intra- and Cross-modality Semantic Consistency (ICMSC). Our approach aims to address the challenge of adapting a model trained on a source domain with a limited number of labeled images to a target domain with a different distribution, by leveraging the semantic consistency between the source and target domains. We first introduce a novel domain adaptation method that aligns the feature distributions of the source and target domains using a domain confusion technique. We then propose a novel consistency loss function that enforces the semantic consistency between the source and target domains, both within and across modalities. The loss function encourages the model to produce similar predictions for the same subject across different modalities, and to produce different predictions for different subjects. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of hip joint bone segmentation images, where the source domain consists of 300 images with manual annotations, and the target domain consists of 1000 images without annotations. Our results show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art domain adaptation methods, with a mean Dice similarity coefficient of 0.83 compared to 0.76 for the best performing baseline. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the different components of our approach. Our results show that the domain confusion technique and the consistency loss function both contribute significantly to the performance improvement, and that the combination of the two components leads to the best results. Our work demonstrates the potential of ICMSC for improving the performance of unsupervised domain adaptation on hip joint bone segmentation tasks, and has important implications for the development of domain adaptation methods for medical image segmentation tasks in general.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach called ICMSC (Intra- and Cross-modality Semantic Consistency) for unsupervised domain adaptation in hip joint bone segmentation. It utilizes a contrastive learning framework that encourages the model to learn features that are not only discriminative within a single modality but also consistent across different modalities. Moreover, an adversarial training strategy is employed to ensure that the learned feaures are robust against variations in image intensity and other nuisance variables. This research has important implications for medical imaging applications where diverse datasets may lack annotations or have variabilities in image acquisition protocols. By improving the ability of deep learning models to adapt across domains, this work can increase the reliability and efficiency of computer vision techniques used in clinical settings.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel technique called CAKES (Channel-wise Automatic KErnel Shrinking) for efficient 3D neural network design. CAKES aims to reduce the computational cost and memory usage of 3D neural networks by automatically shrinking the kernel sizes in a channel-wise manner. Existing methods for efficient 3D neural network design often rely on manual design or heuristics, which can be time-consuming and suboptimal. In contrast, CAKES leverages a novel formulation that jointly optimizes the kernel sizes and channel numbers in a 3D convolutional layer, leading to a more efficient use of computational resources. The key insight behind CAKES is that different channels in a 3D convolutional layer have varying importance, and thus can be assigned different kernel sizes. By shrinking the kernel sizes of less important channels, CAKES reduces the computational cost and memory usage of the network without sacrificing accuracy. We evaluate CAKES on several 3D datasets and compare it with state-of-the-art methods for efficient 3D neural network design. Our results show that CAKES achieves better accuracy and efficiency trade-offs than existing methods. Specifically, CAKES achieves a 1.23x to 2.46x reduction in computational cost and a 1.14x to 2.21x reduction in memory usage, while maintaining comparable accuracy to the baseline model. Our work demonstrates the effectiveness of CAKES in improving the efficiency of 3D neural networks, and highlights the importance of jointly optimizing kernel sizes and channel numbers in 3D convolutional layers. The proposed technique has important implications for a wide range of applications, including computer vision, robotics, and multimedia processing.

title: CAKES: Channel-wise Automatic KErnel Shrinking for Efficient 3D Networks

text: In this paper, we propose a novel method for efficient 3D neural network design called Channel-wise Automatic Kernel Shrinking (CAKES). This approach targets reducing computational cost and memory bandwidth requirements while maintaining comparable or improved accuracy compared to state-of-the-art models. Existing methods for efficient 3D neural networks mainly focus on pruning or quantization, which can result in increased computational complexity or reduced accuracy. CAKES addresses these limitations by introducing a channel-wise automated kernel shrinking technique that adaptively adjusts the size of each kernel along the channels of a 3D convolutional layer. This allows for significant reductions in parameters and computations without compromising performance. The proposed method is evaluated on several publicly available datasets and benchmarked against state-of-the-art models. Our results show that CAKES achieves better or comparable accuracy with significantly fewer parameters and computations. Specifically, we observe an average reduction of 52% in parameters and 46% in computations compared to the baseline model while maintaining similar accuracy. Additionally, we demonstrate the versatility of CAKES by applying it to various 3D deep learning architectures, including Voxel-based CNNs and PointNet-like models. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for few-shot medical image segmentation using a Global Correlation Network with Discriminative Embedding (GCN-DE). Medical image segmentation is a critical task in healthcare, but it can be challenging due to the complexity and variability of medical images. Few-shot learning, which enables models to learn from a small number of labeled examples, has the potential to improve the efficiency and accuracy of medical image segmentation. However, few-shot learning in medical image segmentation is particularly challenging due to the high dimensionality and variability of medical images. To address this challenge, we propose the GCN-DE model, which combines a Global Correlation Network (GCN) with a Discriminative Embedding (DE) module. The GCN captures long-range dependencies in medical images, while the DE module learns a compact and discriminative embedding space that can be used for few-shot segmentation. The GCN-DE model is trained on a large dataset of medical images and achieves state-of-the-art performance on a variety of segmentation tasks, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. We also conduct a series of ablation studies to evaluate the effectiveness of the GCN and DE modules, and demonstrate that the combination of these two modules leads to improved performance compared to using either module alone. Our main contributions are: * The proposal of a novel few-shot medical image segmentation approach using a Global Correlation Network with Discriminative Embedding (GCN-DE). * The development of a new network architecture that combines a Global Correlation Network (GCN) with a Discriminative Embedding (DE) module. * The achievement of state-of-the-art performance on a variety of medical image segmentation tasks using the GCN-DE model. * The demonstration of the effectiveness of the GCN and DE modules through ablation studies. Overall, our work has the potential to improve the efficiency and accuracy of medical image segmentation, and could potentially be applied to a wide range of medical image analysis tasks.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to few-shot medical image segmentation using a global correlation network with discriminative embedding (GCNDE). Our method is designed to address the challenge of segmenting rare or unseen structures in medical images, where annotated data is scarce. Existing few-shot learning methods rely on learned embeddings that are either task-agnostic or task-specific but require a large amount of training data. In contrast, our GCNDE model leverages the idea of global correlation to learn a shared embedding space that can be used across different tasks and datasets. This allows us to perform few-shot segmentation without requiring task-specific annotations. The key innovation of our approach is the use of a discriminative embedding space that captures the underlying structure of the data. We achieve this by introducing an adversarial loss term that encourages the model to produce similar embeddings for positively correlated samples and dissimilar embeddings for negatively correlated samples. We evaluate our method on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets, including brain tumor segmentation from MRI scans and lung tumor segmentation from CT scans. Our results show that GCNDE outperforms state-of-the-art few-shot learning methods, achieving high accuracy even when trained on a small number of labeled examples. Moreover, we conduct experiments to analyze the effectiveness of the various components of our method, providing insights into the role of each component in improving performance. Our findings demonstrate that thediscriminative embedding space and the global correlation constraint are crucial for achieving good few-shot performance. Our work has important implications for medical image segmentation applications where annotation resources are limited. With the ability to adapt to new tasks and datasets with only a handful of labeled examples, GCNDE offers a promising solution for automatic segmentation of rare or unseen structures in medical imaging.
label: ai
title: Few-shot Medical Image Segmentation using a Global Correlation Network with Discriminative Embedding

text: This paper proposes a novel active contour segmentation model that utilizes multiple features to address the issue of intensity inhomogeneity in infrared images. The proposed model combines the advantages of both intensity-based and texture-based segmentation methods, and is capable of accurately segmenting objects in scenes with complex backgrounds and varying illumination conditions. The proposed model employs a multi-feature representation that combines intensity, texture, and edge information to drive the segmentation process. The intensity feature is used to capture the overall brightness of the image, while the texture feature captures the spatial information and patterns in the image. The edge feature, on the other hand, provides information about the boundaries between different objects in the scene. The proposed model also incorporates a novel optimization algorithm that utilizes a combination of gradient descent and level set methods to optimize the segmentation results. The algorithm iteratively updates the contour curve to minimize an energy functional that incorporates the multi-feature representation and a regularization term. Experiments conducted on a dataset of infrared images demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model in segmenting objects in scenes with intensity inhomogeneity. The results show that the proposed model outperforms traditional active contour models and other state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * A novel active contour segmentation model that combines multiple features to address intensity inhomogeneity in infrared images. * A novel optimization algorithm that utilizes a combination of gradient descent and level set methods to optimize the segmentation results. * Experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed model in segmenting objects in scenes with intensity inhomogeneity. Overall, this paper provides a valuable contribution to the field of image segmentation, particularly in the context of infrared imaging, and demonstrates the potential of the proposed model for applications such as object detection, tracking, and recognition.
label: ai
title: Multi-feature driven active contour segmentation model for infrared image with intensity inhomogeneity

text: Abstract: This paper proposes a novel approach to infrared image segmentation using an Active Contour Model (ACM) that incorporates multiple features to handle intensity inhomogeneity. The proposed method, called Multi-Feature Driven Active Contour Segmentation (MF-ACS), combines the traditional ACM with a set of intensification functions that capture different aspects of the image information. The intensification functions are used to drive the evolution of the contour, allowing it to better adapt to the varying intensity characteristics of the image. Experimental results on several challenging infrared images demonstrate the superior performance of MF-ACS compared to traditional ACM and other state-of-the-art methods. The improved accuracy is achieved while maintaining the simplicity and efficiency of the ACM framework. Furthermore, the inclusion of multiple features provides greater robustness to noise and artifacts, ensuring more reliable segmentation. The presented approach has significant applications in various fields such as military surveillance, environmental monitoring, and medical imaging.
label: ai
title: Multi-feature driven active contour segmentation model for infrared image with intensity inhomogeneity

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for 3D medical image segmentation using bidirectional RNN-based few-shot learning. Our approach leverages the strengths of bidirectional recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to learn both spatial and temporal dependencies in 3D medical images, and few-shot learning to adapt to new classes with only a few labeled examples. Existing methods for 3D medical image segmentation often require large amounts of labeled data and computational resources, which can be a limiting factor in many clinical settings. In contrast, our proposed method uses a bidirectional RNN architecture to learn from both past and future frames in a video sequence, allowing it to capture complex spatial and temporal patterns in the data. Additionally, we incorporate a few-shot learning mechanism that enables the model to adapt to new classes with only a few labeled examples, reducing the need for large-scale annotated datasets. We evaluate our approach on several publicly available datasets of 3D medical images, including brain tumor segmentation and lung nodule segmentation. Our results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art few-shot learning methods for 3D medical image segmentation, while also being more efficient in terms of computational resources and annotation requirements. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach for 3D medical image segmentation using bidirectional RNN-based few-shot learning, which combines the strengths of bidirectional RNNs and few-shot learning to improve segmentation accuracy and efficiency. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several publicly available datasets of 3D medical images, outperforming state-of-the-art few-shot learning methods while requiring fewer annotations. * We show that our method has the potential to reduce the annotation burden and computational resources required for 3D medical image segmentation, making it more feasible for clinical applications. Overall, our work has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of 3D medical image segmentation, and could have a positive impact on a wide range of medical applications, including disease diagnosis,
label: ai
title: Bidirectional RNN-based Few Shot Learning for 3D Medical Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to few-shot learning for 3D medical image segmentation using bidirectional Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs). Our approach leverages the strengths of both CNNs and RNNs to learn a robust representation of 3D medical images that can be adapted to new classes with only a handful of labeled examples. Existing few-shot learning approaches for medical image segmentation are limited by their reliance on task-specific architectures or the use of complex meta-learning algorithms. In contrast, our proposed method uses a simple and efficient architecture that combines a CNN with a bidirectional RNN to learn a generalizable feature representation that can be fine-tuned for new tasks. The key innovation of our approach is the use of bidirectional RNNs, which allow us to capture both spatial and temporal information in 3D medical images. By processing the imaging sequence in both forward and backward directions, we can learn a rich set of features that encode both local and global patterns in the data. This allows us to achieve state-of-the-art performance on several challenging datasets without requiring a large amount of training data. Our experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach compared to various baselines and prior art methods. We show that our method outperforms others on three different 3D medical image segmentation benchmarks, achieving an average segmentation accuracy of 95% on unseen classes. Additionally, we perform an ablation study to analyze the contribution of individual components of our model, providing insights into the role of each component in improving performance. Our work has important implications for real-world applications where annotated data is scarce, such as neurosurgical planning and computer-aided diagnosis. With its strong performance and efficiency, our proposed method offers a promising solution for tackling the challenge of few-shot learning in 3D medical image segmentation.

title: Bidirectional RNN-based Few Shot Learning for 3D Medical Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel medical image segmentation model called w-Net, which combines the strengths of dual supervision and multi-dimensional attention with cascade multi-scale convolution. The proposed model aims to improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation tasks, particularly in cases where annotated data is limited. The w-Net model consists of two main components: a dual supervised encoder-decoder architecture and a multi-dimensional attention mechanism. The encoder-decoder architecture is designed to learn both pixel-level and spatial-level features of medical images, while the attention mechanism enables the model to focus on the most relevant features and scales. The cascade multi-scale convolution module is used to aggregate multi-scale contextual information and enhance the feature representation capability of the model. We evaluate the performance of w-Net on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. The results show that w-Net outperforms several state-of-the-art segmentation models, including U-Net, Attention U-Net, and Cascade U-Net, in terms of segmentation accuracy and efficiency. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel medical image segmentation model called w-Net, which combines dual supervision, multi-dimensional attention, and cascade multi-scale convolution. * The w-Net model improves the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation tasks, particularly in cases where annotated data is limited. * The proposed model has been evaluated on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets, and the results demonstrate its superior performance compared to several state-of-the-art segmentation models. Overall, the w-Net model has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation tasks, and it has important implications for a wide range of medical applications, including disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring.
label: ai
title: w-Net: Dual Supervised Medical Image Segmentation Model with Multi-Dimensional Attention and Cascade Multi-Scale Convolution

text: Abstract: Accurate segmentation of medical images is crucial for diagnosis and treatment planning in various medical disciplines. However, annotating large amounts of data for deep learning models poses significant challenges. In this work, we propose w-Net, a novel dual supervised medical image segmentation model that leverages both labeled and unlabeled data to improve performance while reducing annotation efforts. Our approach combines multi-dimensional attention and cascade multi-scale convolutions to effectively capture spatial hierarchical context and semantics across different scales. We evaluate our method on multiple datasets, including brain tumor, pancreatic cancer, and lung nodule detection, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Notably, when using only 20% of the labeled data, w-Net achieves comparable results to fully supervised models. Furthermore, we show that our model generalizes well to unseen data, outperforming other approaches under limited annotation conditions. This study introduces an effective way to leverage large amounts of unlabeled data for medical image segmentation tasks, paving the road for improving machine learning model performance in healthcare applications.

title: w-Net: Dual Supervised Medical Image Segmentation Model with Multi-Dimensional Attention and Cascade Multi-Scale Convolution

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation, which we term "Disentangle, Align, and Fuse" (DAF). Our method leverages the strengths of both multimodal and semi-supervised learning to improve the accuracy and robustness of image segmentation. First, we disentangle the input images into their underlying factors of variation using a variational autoencoder (VAE). This allows us to learn a set of modality-invariant features that capture the underlying structure of the data. Next, we align the features from different modalities using a novel contrastive loss function, which encourages the features to have similar distributions across modalities. After aligning the features, we fuse them using a new fusion strategy that combines the strengths of early and late fusion methods. Our fusion strategy allows us to leverage the complementary information present in different modalities, leading to improved segmentation performance. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and MS COCO. Our results show that DAF outperforms state-of-the-art methods in both fully supervised and semi-supervised settings. In particular, DAF achieves an mIoU of 81.2% on Cityscapes with only 10% annotated data, outperforming the next best method by 10.3%. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach for multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation, which disentangles, aligns, and fuses features from different modalities. * We introduce a new contrastive loss function for aligning features across modalities, which encourages modality-invariant feature learning. * We develop a novel fusion strategy that combines the strengths of early and late fusion methods, leading to improved segmentation performance. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on several benchmark datasets, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both fully supervised and semi-supervised settings. title: Disentangle, align and fuse for multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation, which leverages the strengths of both modalities and annotations to improve segmentation accuracy. Our method, called Disentangle, Align, and Fuse (DAF), consists of three main stages: 1) disentanglement, where we separate the input images into their independent factors of variation; 2) alignment, where we register the modalities and annotated labels across different domains; and 3) fusion, where we combine the aligned modalities and labels to produce accurate segmentations. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation performance and robustness to variations in modality and annotation quality. Additionally, we provide an extensive ablation study to analyze the effectiveness of each component of DAF and show that it can adapt to various scenarios with different levels of annotation scarcity and modality disparity. Our key contributions include the introduction of a new probabilistic framework for jointly modeling multimodal images and semi-supervised learning, and the development of an efficient optimization algorithm that enables iterative refinement of the proposed model. The DAF method has broad applications in medical imaging, object detection, and scene understanding, where multiple modalities are often available butannotated data is scarce or difficult to obtain.

title: Disentangle, align and fuse for multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation, which leverages both labeled and unlabeled data to improve segmentation accuracy. Our approach, called the Teacher-Student Framework for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation from Mixed Supervision (TSF-SMS), combines the strengths of two existing methods: (1) supervised learning using labeled data, and (2) self-supervised learning using unlabeled data. The TSF-SMS framework consists of two main components: a teacher model and a student model. The teacher model is trained on a small set of labeled data and is used to guide the training of the student model, which is trained on a larger set of unlabeled data. The teacher model provides high-level segmentation masks, which are then used as pseudo-labels for the student model. The student model refines the segmentation masks produced by the teacher model, resulting in improved segmentation accuracy. We evaluate the TSF-SMS framework on several medical image segmentation tasks, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. Our results show that the TSF-SMS framework outperforms traditional supervised learning methods, which use only labeled data, and also outperforms self-supervised learning methods, which use only unlabeled data. We also perform an ablation study to analyze the effectiveness of the teacher-student framework and demonstrate that the student model benefits from the guidance provided by the teacher model. Our main contributions are: (1) the proposal of a novel teacher-student framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation, (2) the development of a new self-supervised loss function that leverages the teacher model to improve the student model's performance, and (3) the demonstration of the effectiveness of the TSF-SMS framework on several medical image segmentation tasks. The TSF-SMS framework has the potential to improve the accuracy of medical image segmentation models, especially in situations where labeled data is scarce or difficult to obtain. title: A Teacher-Student Framework for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation From Mixed Supervision

text: In this paper, we propose a novel teacher-student framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation from mixed supervision. The proposed method leverages both labeled and unlabeled data to train a deep neural network for segmenting medical images, which can help alleviate the scarcity of annotated data in medical imaging. The proposed framework consists of two main components: a teacher model and a student model. The teacher model is trained on a small set of labeled data and serves as a guide to provide high-quality pseudo labels for the remaining unlabeled data. The student model is then trained on the combined dataset of labeled and pseudolabeled data, and learns to segment medical images under the guidance of the teacher model. We evaluate our framework on several publicly available datasets and show that it outperforms existing semi-supervised learning methods for medical image segmentation. Our experiments also demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed framework when dealing with different types of label noise and limited annotation rates. Our main contributions are threefold: (1) We introduce a novel teacher-student framework for semi-supervised medical image segmentation; (2) We demonstrate the efficacy of using a teacher model to provide high-quality pseudo labels for unlabeled data; (3) We show that our proposed framework outperforms existing methods in various experimental settings. This work has the potential to significantly impact the field of medical image analysis by providing an effective solution to the problem of scarce annotations, which is a major bottleneck in many medical image segmentation tasks.

title: A Teacher-Student Framework for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation From Mixed Supervision

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to improve the performance of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) by introducing a hierarchical-split block, called HS-ResNet. The proposed block is designed to address the problem of feature hierarchy and reduce the computational cost of traditional CNNs. The HS-ResNet block consists of three main components: a hierarchical-split convolutional layer, a spatial pyramid pooling layer, and a channel-wise attention layer. The hierarchical-split convolutional layer splits the input feature maps into multiple sub-bands, allowing the network to capture features at multiple scales. The spatial pyramid pooling layer reduces the spatial dimensions of the feature maps, while preserving the spatial information. The channel-wise attention layer adaptively weights the importance of each channel, enabling the network to focus on the most relevant features. We evaluate the performance of HS-ResNet on several benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet. The results show that HS-ResNet achieves better performance compared to state-of-the-art CNNs, including ResNet and DenseNet. Specifically, HS-ResNet achieves a 3.14% improvement in accuracy on CIFAR-10 and a 4.38% improvement on CIFAR-100, while reducing the number of parameters by 37.5% and 44.4%, respectively. On ImageNet, HS-ResNet achieves a 1.44% improvement in accuracy, while reducing the number of parameters by 28.6%. The proposed HS-ResNet also shows better robustness to adversarial attacks and generalizes well to unseen data. We analyze the effectiveness of the hierarchical-split block and demonstrate that it improves the feature representation and reduces the computational cost of the network. Our findings suggest that the hierarchical-split block is a promising approach to improve the performance of CNNs. title: HS-ResNet: Hierarchical-Split Block on Convolutional Neural Network

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to improve the performance of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) by introducing a hierarchical-split block called HS-ResNet. The proposed block is designed to address the limitations of traditional CNNs, which suffer from the vanishing gradient problem and limited representation capacity. The HS-ResNet block consists of three main components: a spatial pyramid pooling module, a feature fusion module, and a residual connection module. The spatial pyramid pooling module hierarchically splits the input feature maps into multiple scales, allowing the network to capture multiscale contextual information. The feature fusion module combines the split features through concatenation and channel-wise attention, enhancing the representational capacity of the network. Finally, the residual connection module ensures the stability of the training process and improves the overall performance by incorporating skip connections. We evaluate the efficacy of HS-ResNet on several benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet. Experimental results demonstrate that our proposed architecture achieves state-of-the-art performance compared to existing CNN models. Specifically, HS-ResNet50 achieves an error rate of 3.27% on CIFAR-10 and 19.48% on CIFAR-100, outperforming other CNN models with similar complexity. On ImageNet, HS-ResNet reaches a Top-5 accuracy of 86.1%, surpassing other ResNet variants. Our extensive ablation studies reveal that the hierarchical-split design and feature fusion module are crucial for the improved performance of HS-ResNet. We also analyze the effectiveness of different component combinations and provide insights into their roles in the network's behavior. In summary, this paper presents HS-ResNet, a novel CNN architecture that leverages hierarchical splitting and feature fusion to enhance the representational capacity and alleviate the vanishing gradient issue. Our experimental results prove its superiority over existing CNN models and provide valuable guidelines for future architectural advancements in computer vision tasks.

title: HS-ResNet: Hierarchical-Split Block on Convolutional Neural Network

text: In this paper, we propose a novel active learning method for semantic segmentation called MetaBox+, which leverages region-based priority maps to select the most informative samples for labeling. Unlike traditional active learning methods that rely on uncertainty or random sampling, MetaBox+ uses a priority map to identify regions of the image that are most likely to contain class boundaries, and thus, are most informative for learning. The priority map is generated using a novel combination of image features and class probabilities, which are computed using a pre-trained deep neural network. The features capture the spatial and spectral information of the image, while the class probabilities provide a measure of the model's confidence in its predictions. By combining these two types of information, the priority map can effectively identify regions of the image that are most likely to contain class boundaries, even in the presence of occlusions, clutter, or other challenges. We evaluate MetaBox+ on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and compare it to several state-of-the-art active learning methods. Our results show that MetaBox+ significantly outperforms these methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, achieving higher accuracy with fewer labeled samples. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of the MetaBox+ method, providing insights into the role of each component in improving performance. Overall, MetaBox+ offers a promising approach to active learning for semantic segmentation, with potential applications in a wide range of fields, including robotics, autonomous driving, and medical imaging. By selectively labeling the most informative samples, MetaBox+ can significantly reduce the labeling effort required for training accurate segmentation models, making it a valuable tool for tasks where labeling data is time-consuming or expensive.
label: ai
title: MetaBox+: A new Region Based Active Learning Method for Semantic Segmentation using Priority Maps

text: In this paper, we propose a novel region-based active learning method for semantic segmentation called MetaBox+, which leverages priority maps to efficiently select the most informative regions for annotation. Existing active learning methods for semantic segmentation often rely on uncertainty sampling or random sampling, which can lead to inefficient use of annotations and slow convergence. In contrast, MetaBox+ uses a priority map to identify regions of the image that are most likely to benefit from manual annotation, based on their semantic complexity and relevance to the current classifier's performance. The priority map is learned by a separate neural network, trained end-to-end with the segmentation model, and provides a continuous rating of each pixel's importance. We evaluate MetaBox+ on several public datasets and show that it significantly outperforms existing active learning methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, we demonstrate that MetaBox+ achieves state-of-the-art mIoU scores while requiring up to 50% fewer annotated examples than other methods. We also perform ablation studies to investigate the effectiveness of different components of the proposed approach. Our key contributions include: * Introducing MetaBox+, a novel region-based active learning method for semantic segmentation that utilizes priority maps to guide annotation effort. * Demonstrating superior performance and efficiency of MetaBox+ compared to existing active learning methods on multiple benchmark datasets. * Providing thorough analysis and ablation studies to understand the effectiveness of different components of the proposed approach. Overall, our work has important implications for improving the efficiency and efficacy of semantic segmentation tasks, particularly in scenarios where labeling data is expensive or time-consuming.
label: ai
title: MetaBox+: A new Region Based Active Learning Method for Semantic Segmentation using Priority Maps

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for interactive segmentation of patterned images, which we call content-based propagation of user markings (CPUM). Our method leverages the user's initial annotations to propagate segmentation markers throughout the image, using a content-based similarity measure to ensure that the markers are placed in regions with similar visual characteristics. We evaluate the effectiveness of CPUM on a variety of patterned images and compare it to traditional interactive segmentation methods, demonstrating that our approach is more efficient and accurate. We also show that CPUM can be used to segment complex images with multiple patterns and objects, and that it can handle noisy or missing user annotations. Our approach has applications in various fields, including image and video analysis, computer vision, and human-computer interaction.
label: ai
title: Content-based Propagation of User Markings for Interactive Segmentation of Patterned Images

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for interactive image segmentation that leverages content-based propagation of user markings to accurately separate objects from their backgrounds. Our method is designed to handle patterned images with complex shapes and structures, where traditional segmentation techniques often struggle. We propose a two-stage framework that first involves generating an initial segmentation mask using user annotations, and then propagating these annotations content-wise throughout the image to refine the segmentation mask. The proposed propagation technique takes into account both local and global contextual information, ensuring accurate boundary detection and minimization of fragmentation. We also introduce a novel optimization model that incorporates user feedback to improve the accuracy of the segmentationmask over time. Extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the superiority of our method compared to state-of-the art interactive segmentation algorithms. Specifically, we show significant improvements in segmentation accuracy, efficiency, and robustness, especially when dealing with large and/or occluded objects. Moreover, we evaluate the usability of our method throughuser studies, which confirm its effectiveness in terms of annotation effort and user satisfaction. Our main contribution lies in introducing a powerful content-based propagation mechanism that adaptively absorbs user markings and produces high-quality segmentation masks, even when the object boundaries are intricate or unclear. By combining this mechanism with optimized user interaction,we provide a more efficient, robust, and reliable interactive segmentation method that can be applied to various applications such as medical imaging, industrial inspection, and multimedia processing.
label: ai
title: Content-based Propagation of User Markings for Interactive Segmentation of Patterned Images

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to segmenting microscopy images using lazy labels. Traditional segmentation methods rely on manually annotating images with precise boundaries between different regions of interest, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our approach leverages the concept of lazy labels, which are rough annotations that can be quickly and easily obtained, to train a deep learning model to segment microscopy images. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several datasets of microscopy images, showing that our model is able to accurately segment images with high precision and recall, even when the lazy labels are noisy or incomplete. We also show that our approach is more efficient and scalable than traditional segmentation methods, as it requires significantly less annotated data to achieve the same level of performance. Our main contribution is the development of a deep learning model that can effectively learn to segment microscopy images from lazy labels. We propose a novel architecture that combines a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a conditional random field (CRF) to refine the segmentation results. The CNN is trained to learn features that are relevant for segmenting microscopy images, while the CRF is used to incorporate the lazy labels and enforce spatial consistency in the segmentation results. We evaluate our approach using several metrics, including precision, recall, and F1-score, and show that it outperforms traditional segmentation methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. We also perform a user study to evaluate the usability of our approach and show that it can be easily adopted by non-experts. Overall, our paper demonstrates the potential of using lazy labels for segmenting microscopy images, and highlights the advantages of our approach in terms of efficiency, scalability, and accuracy. We believe that our method has the potential to significantly improve the throughput and accuracy of microscopy image analysis, and could enable new applications in biology and medicine.
label: ai
title: Learning to segment microscopy images with lazy labels

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to train deep neural networks for image segmentation tasks on data with limited annotated examples. Our method leverages the concept of "lazy labels," which enables us to utilize large amounts of unannotated data during training, while still maintaining accuracy and efficiency. Existing methods for image segmentation typically rely on fully-supervised learning approaches that require abundant high-quality labeled data. However, obtaining such annotations can be time-consuming and expensive, particularly for microscopy images where expertise in both imaging techniques and domain knowledge is required. In contrast, our proposed method exploits the idea of lazily labeling the data, where only a small portion of the dataset is manually annotated, while the remaining majority remains unaltered. Our key insight is that, by appropriately augmenting the partially labeled data, we can learn a robust segmentation model without relying solely on fully annotated samples. To achieve this, we introduce a regularization term into the loss function that encourages the model to produce smooth predictions on the unlabeled data. Theoretical analysis and experiments show that our Lazy Labels Segmentation (LLS) framework outperforms traditional semi-supervised learning strategies and rivals performance of fully-supervised models trained on larger fully annotated datasets. We evaluate LLS on several challenging microscopy image segmentation benchmarks. Compared to state-of-the-art methods, our approach achieves higher IoU scores using fewer annotations, demonstrating its potential to reduce annotation efforts and accelerate research progress in biomedical sciences. Furthermore, we demonstrate generalizability of LLS across various segmentation architectures and provide insights into the working mechanisms behind it. In summary, this work offers an alternative paradigm for segmenting microscopy images by introducing the concept of lazy labels. By harnessing the power of large-scale unannotated data, our proposed method has the potential to markedly improve the efficiency and scalability of image segmentation practices in life science research applications.
label: ai
title: Learning to segment microscopy images with lazy labels

text: Semi-supervised instance segmentation is a computer vision task that aims to segment objects within an image, where only a limited number of objects are labeled. Mask-guided sample selection is a novel approach that leverages the power of masks to select the most informative samples for training. By using masks to guide the sampling process, the proposed method can significantly reduce the number of labeled samples needed for training, while still achieving high segmentation accuracy. In this paper, we propose a mask-guided sample selection method for semi-supervised instance segmentation, which consists of two main components: (1) mask generation and (2) sample selection. First, we generate high-quality masks for each object category using a instance segmentation model. Then, we use the masks to select a subset of the unlabeled data that is most likely to contain objects from the target category. We evaluate the effectiveness of our method on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and COCO. The main contributions of this paper are: * A novel mask-guided sample selection method for semi-supervised instance segmentation, which significantly reduces the number of labeled samples needed for training. * A thorough evaluation of the proposed method on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving segmentation accuracy. * An analysis of the impact of different components of the proposed method on its performance, providing insights into the best practices for implementing the method. The proposed method has important implications for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce or expensive to obtain. By leveraging the power of masks to select the most informative samples, we can significantly reduce the amount of labeled data needed for training, while still achieving high segmentation accuracy. This can help to improve the efficiency and scalability of semi-supervised instance segmentation systems, and enable their deployment in a wider range of applications.
label: ai
title: Mask-guided sample selection for Semi-Supervised Instance Segmentation

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to semi-supervised instance segmentation, entitled Mask-Guided Sample Selection (MGSS). Existing methods often rely on random sampling or heuristics to select instances for training, which can lead to suboptimal performance. In contrast, MGSS leverages the information provided by masks to strategically select the most informative samples for training. The proposed method consists of two main stages: (1) generating high-quality candidate masks using a state-of-the-art instance segmentation model and (2) selecting a subset of instances from the dataset that maximizes the expected accuracy improvement for a given amount of annotation effort. The second stage is achieved through a reinforcement learning agent designed to learn an optimal policy for selecting the most informative instances based on their potential contribution to the model's accuracy. Extensive experiments conducted on several benchmark datasets demonstrate that MGSS significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of segmentation accuracy while requiring fewer annotated examples. This work has crucial implications for real-world applications where labeling data can be expensive and time-consuming. By employing MGSS, one can leverage large amounts of unlabeled data and reduce annotation efforts required for achieving high segmentation accuracy. Additionally, this study provides new insights into how different factors affect the efficiency of semi-supervised instance segmentation techniques, shedding light on previously overlooked aspects of the problem.
label: ai
title: Mask-guided sample selection for Semi-Supervised Instance Segmentation

text: PC-U Net: Learning to Jointly Reconstruct and Segment the Cardiac Walls in 3D from CT Data Abstract: Accurate segmentation and reconstruction of the cardiac walls from computed tomography (CT) data is crucial for various cardiovascular applications, such as diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring of cardiac diseases. However, the complexity and variability of the cardiac anatomy, as well as the limited resolution and quality of CT images, make this task challenging. In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning approach, called PC-U Net, which jointly reconstructs and segments the cardiac walls in 3D from CT data. PC-U Net is built upon a U-Net architecture and incorporates a novel partitioned convolutional branch (PCB) to simultaneously learn the cardiac wall segmentation and 3D reconstruction. The PCB divides the input image into four sub-images, each of which is processed by a separate convolutional neural network (CNN) to predict the corresponding cardiac wall. text: Abstract: Accurate segmentation and reconstruction of cardiac walls from computed tomography (CT) data are crucial steps in various clinical applications, such as diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring of heart diseases. However, the task is challenging due to factors like image quality, motion artifacts, and complex anatomy. In this work, we propose PC-U Net, a deep learning framework that jointly learns to reconstruct and segment cardiac walls in 3D from raw CT data. Our approach leverages a combination of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with physics constraints to improve accuracy, efficiency, and robustness. The proposed method consists of two parts: 1) PC-Net, which generates initial wall boundaries using a fully convolutional network, and 2) U-Net, which refines the borders via skip connections and incorporates user interactions. We evaluate our method on a dataset containing 40 diverse patients using both qualitative and quantitative metrics. Experimental results show superior performance when compared to state-of-the-art methods regarding Dice similarity coefficient, average surface distance, and processing time. Additionally, we demonstrate the feasibility of integrating our method into real-time scenarios through synchronization with commercial software. By providing accurate and efficient reconstruction and segmentation, PC-U Net can significantly aid interventionalists and researchers in their endeavors involving cardiac care.

This process iteratively refines the segmentation, gradually isolating objects from the surrounding pixels. We also introduce a new criterion for stopping the iteration, which ensures that the segmentation is accurate and robust. text: In recent years, deep learning techniques have gained significant attention in the field of image segmentation, with many researchers and practitioners touting their ability to achieve state-of-the-art performance. However, the veracity of these claims has not been thoroughly examined, and the extent to which deep learning methods truly excel in image segmentation remains unclear. This paper provides a comprehensive evaluation of deep learning techniques for image segmentation, assessing their strengths and weaknesses, and examining the factors that contribute to their performance. Our analysis reveals that, while deep learning methods can indeed achieve high accuracy in certain scenarios, their performance is not uniformly superior across all datasets and task settings. We identify several factors that influence the effectiveness of deep learning for image segmentation, including dataset size, annotation quality, and the choice of architecture and training parameters. Our findings suggest that, in some cases, traditional computer vision techniques can perform equally well or even better than deep learning methods, particularly when combined with domain-specific knowledge and careful parameter tuning. Our study also investigates the impact of overfitting and generalization in deep learning-based image segmentation, and proposes strategies to mitigate these issues. We demonstrate that with appropriate regularization techniques and careful model selection, deep learning models can generalize well to unseen data and provide robust performance. In conclusion, our paper provides a balanced perspective on the use of deep learning for image segmentation, highlighting both its potential and limitations. Our findings have important implications for the development of image segmentation systems, and suggest that a combination of traditional computer vision techniques and deep learning methods may offer the best approach in many cases.
