title: Deep learning for image segmentation: veritable or overhyped?

text: In recent years, deep learning techniques have gained widespread attention in the field of image segmentation, with many researchers and practitioners touting their ability to achieve state-of-the-art performance. However, the question remains as to whether these techniques are truly effective or simply overhyped. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive review of deep learning methods for image segmentation, examining both their strengths and limitations. Our analysis reveals that deep learning algorithms, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and fully convolutional networks (FCNs), have indeed shown impressive performance on various image segmentation tasks. They are able to learn rich feature representations from large datasets, leading to improved accuracy compared to traditional computer vision approaches. Moreover, they offer greater flexibility and generalization capabilities, allowing them to be applied to a wide range of images and scenarios. However, our investigation also highlights several challenges and open issues in deep learning-based image segmentation. Firstly, we note that deep learning models require copious amounts of labeled training data, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. Additionally, they can suffer from overfitting, especially when dealing with small datasets or minor variations in input images. Furthermore, interpretability and explainability remain significant concerns, making it difficult to understand why certain segmentation decisions are made. To address these limitations, we propose several avenues for future research. One promising direction is to explore alternative architectures and regularization techniques that enhance generalizability and robustness. Another approach is to develop novel transfer learning strategies, enabling deep learning models to adapt effectively to new domains or tasks with minimal additional training data. Finally, improving interpretability through visualizations and attention mechanisms may help build trust in deep learning-based image segmentation systems. In conclusion, while deep learning techniques have undoubtedly revolutionized image segmentation, it is crucial to recognize both their merits and drawbacks. By acknowledging the current limitations and pursuing targeted advancements, we can ensure that deep learning continues to serve as a powerful tool for image segmentation and other computer vision applications.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for 3D semantic segmentation of medical images, which combines the strengths of shape-aware and semi-supervised learning. Our proposed method leverages the rich spatial and semantic information present in medical images to improve segmentation accuracy, while minimizing the need for annotated data. Existing 3D semantic segmentation methods rely heavily on fully-supervised learning, which requires a large amount of annotated data to achieve high accuracy. However, annotating medical images is a time-consuming and expensive process, and the lack of annotated data can limit the performance of these methods. In contrast, our proposed method exploits the shape information present in medical images to guide the segmentation process, even in the absence of annotated data. Our approach consists of two main components: a shape-aware loss function and a semi-supervised learning framework. The shape-aware loss function encourages the segmentation model to produce smooth and consistent predictions that align with the underlying anatomy, while the semi-supervised learning framework leverages both labeled and unlabeled data to improve segmentation accuracy. We evaluate our method on several publicly available medical image datasets, including brain MRI and CT scans. Our results show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art semi-supervised and fully-supervised 3D semantic segmentation methods, while requiring significantly less annotated data. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in segmenting various medical structures, such as tumors, organs, and vessels, and show that it can handle complex cases with multiple structures and low contrast. Our work has important implications for medical image analysis, as it provides a more efficient and accurate approach to 3D semantic segmentation, which can be used for a variety of applications, such as disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring. Additionally, our method has the potential to be adapted to other medical imaging modalities and tasks, such as 2D image segmentation and image registration. In summary, our paper presents a novel approach to 3D semantic segmentation of medical images that combines the strengths of shape-aware and semi-supervised learning.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to semi-supervised 3D semantic segmentation of medical images, which leverages shape information to improve accuracy. Our proposed method, called Shape-Aware Semi-Supervised 3D Semantic Segmentation (SASS), incorporates shape prior knowledge into the segmentation process through a novel graph convolutional network architecture. SASS takes advantage of both sparse annotated data and abundant unannotated data to learn a robust representation of the underlying anatomy, leading to improved performance compared to traditional fully supervised or semi-supervised segmentation approaches. We evaluate our method on several public medical datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in handling variations in image quality, modality, and annotation density. The results show that SASS outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in terms of segmentation accuracy and demonstrates superior generalization ability to new subjects and imaging protocols. Notably, as few as 10% labeled examples are required to achieve high performance on some tasks, making it particularly useful for applications where manual annotations are difficult and expensive to obtain. The main contributions of this work are twofold: first, the introduction of a novel graph convolutional network architecture that fuses instance-wise shape information with image features; second, the development of an effective training strategy that combines adversarial and self-supervised learning objectives to optimize the segmentation model using limited annotated data. This study has important implications for future research in medical image analysis, and more broadly, for developing AI algorithms that can effectively leverage large amounts of weakly labeled data for complex task automation.

text: This paper proposes a novel human-in-the-loop system for industrial tool wear analysis that incorporates uncertainty into the decision-making process. The system uses a combination of machine learning algorithms and human expertise to accurately detect and classify tool wear in industrial settings. The uncertainty-based approach allows the system to adapt to changing conditions and handle variability in the data, leading to improved accuracy and reliability compared to traditional machine learning approaches. The proposed system consists of three main components: (1) a machine learning model that predicts tool wear based on sensor data, (2) a human-in-the-loop module that allows an expert to correct and refine the predictions, and (3) an uncertainty quantification module that estimates the uncertainty of the predictions. The system uses a feedback loop to continuously update the machine learning model and improve its accuracy over time. The paper presents the results of a case study in which the proposed system was implemented in a manufacturing setting to detect tool wear in a machining process. The results show that the uncertainty-based approach significantly outperformed traditional machine learning approaches in terms of accuracy and reliability. The system was able to accurately detect tool wear in real-time, and the human-in-the-loop module allowed an expert to correct errors and improve the system's performance. The paper also discusses the limitations and challenges of implementing an uncertainty-based human-in-the-loop system in industrial settings, and provides insights into the potential applications of such systems in other domains. The authors conclude that the proposed system has the potential to revolutionize the field of tool wear analysis and pave the way for more accurate and reliable monitoring of industrial processes.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for annotation-efficient cardiac segmentation, called Dual-Teacher, which integrates both intra-domain and inter-domain teachers to improve the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation model. Cardiac segmentation is a critical task in medical imaging, but annotating the large amount of data required for deep learning models can be time-consuming and expensive. To address this challenge, we propose a dual-teacher framework that combines the strengths of both intra-domain and inter-domain teachers. Intra-domain teachers are trained on the same dataset as the target model and can provide accurate annotations for the target domain, while inter-domain teachers are trained on different datasets and can provide diverse annotations from other domains. Our proposed Dual-Teacher framework consists of two main components: (1) a intra-domain teacher that uses a self-supervised learning approach to annotate the target dataset, and (2) an inter-domain teacher that uses a contrastive learning approach to annotate datasets from different domains. We evaluate the effectiveness of our approach using a dataset of cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) images. The results show that our Dual-Teacher framework outperforms traditional annotation methods, achieving a dice similarity coefficient of 0.832 and a mean absolute error of 0.063. In addition, we demonstrate that our approach can reduce the amount of manual annotation required by 60% while maintaining the same level of accuracy. Our study demonstrates the potential of using a dual-teacher framework for annotation-efficient cardiac segmentation, and highlights the importance of integrating both intra-domain and inter-domain knowledge for improving the accuracy and efficiency of deep learning models in medical image segmentation tasks.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that leverages the mathematical properties of hyperbolic space to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of image classification tasks. Traditional CNNs rely on Euclidean space and are limited by the "curse of dimensionality," which makes it difficult to process high-dimensional data. By contrast, hyperbolic space has a more favorable dimensionality scaling, which allows for more efficient processing of high-dimensional data. We propose a fully hyperbolic CNN (FH-CNN) that uses hyperbolic space to represent images and perform convolutional operations. The FH-CNN is designed to take advantage of the properties of hyperbolic space, such as its non-Euclidean geometry and the existence of a natural hierarchy of scales. We develop a new convolution operation that is tailored to the hyperbolic space and allows for efficient feature extraction and representation. We evaluate the performance of the FH-CNN on several image classification benchmarks and compare it to traditional CNNs. Our results show that the FH-CNN achieves better performance than traditional CNNs on several benchmarks, while also requiring fewer parameters and computations. We also perform an ablation study to analyze the contributions of the different components of the FH-CNN and provide insights into the working mechanism of the model. Our work demonstrates the potential of hyperbolic space for image classification tasks and paves the way for further research in this area. The FH-CNN presents a promising direction for improving the efficiency and effectiveness of CNNs, and we believe that it has important implications for a wide range of applications, including computer vision, machine learning, and data analysis.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to convolutional neural networks (CNNs) called Fully Hyperbolic Convolutional Neural Networks (FHCN). Unlike traditional CNNs that operate on Euclidean spaces, FHCNs are designed to work directly on hyperbolic spaces, which are non-Euclidean and have negative curvature. This allows FHCNs to capture complex patterns and relationships in data that would be difficult or impossible to model using traditional CNNs. We demonstrate the effectiveness of FHCNs on several challenging tasks, including image classification, object detection, and natural language processing. Our results show that FHCNs significantly outperform state-of-the-art CNNs on these tasks, while also providing more robust and generalizable performance. We also provide an analysis of the theoretical properties of FHCNs, showing that they possess a number of desirable qualities such as improved ability to capture long-range dependencies and resistance to overfitting. The key innovation of FHCNs is the use of hyperbolic activation functions, which map the output of each neuron to a point in a high-dimensional hyperbolic space. These functions allow FHCNs to learn hierarchical representations of data that take into account both local and global structure, leading to improved performance on complex tasks. Additionally, we introduce a new training algorithm specifically designed for FHCNs, which combines elements of gradient descent and iterative linearization to optimize network parameters efficiently. Our work marks the first time that fully hyperbolic neural networks have been proposed and demonstrates their potential for advancing the field of artificial intelligence. The success of FHCNs suggests that non-Euclidean methods may play an important role in future research and development of deep learning algorithms, especially those aimed at tackling complex problems with large amounts of data.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to self-supervised learning of deep neural networks for image inpainting, called Region-of-interest guided Supervoxel Inpainting (RSI). Our method leverages the concept of supervoxels, which are small, meaningful regions of an image that can be used to learn robust features without explicit annotation. By combining supervoxels with a region-of-interest (RoI) guidance mechanism, we enable the network to focus on the most relevant areas of the image, resulting in improved performance and efficiency. Existing self-supervised inpainting methods often rely on randomly masked patches or pixels, which can lead to the network learning trivial solutions or suffering from overfitting. In contrast, our RSI method uses a learnable RoI module to identify the most informative regions of the image, which are then used to guide the inpainting process. This approach allows the network to learn more robust and meaningful features, as it is forced to infer the missing information based on the context of the image. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10 and ImageNet. The results show that RSI outperforms state-of-the-art self-supervised inpainting methods, achieving better performance on both image inpainting and downstream tasks such as image classification. text: This paper proposes a novel approach to self-supervised learning, called Region-of-Interest (RoI) Guided Supervoxel Inpainting, which leverages the power of unlabelled data to train deep neural networks. The proposed method is based on the idea that regions of interest in an image can be identified and used as a guidance signal to drive the training process. By combining this signal with a supervoxel-based inpainting strategy, we are able to learn rich and robust feature representations without requiring manual annotation. The proposed RoI Guided Supervoxel Inpainting method consists of two main components: (1) a region proposal network (RPN) that generates a set of regions of interest (RoIs) within an input image, and (2) a supervoxel inpainting network (SIN) that learns to fill in missing information within each RoI using surrounding context. During training, the RPN and SIN are trained simultaneously, where the RPN provides guidance to the SIN by identifying relevant RoIs, and the SIN refines its predictions based on the observed data. Experiments conducted on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach. Compared to state-of-the-art self-supervised learning methods, RoI Guided Supervoxel Inpainting achieves superior performance on downstream tasks such as object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. Our analysis reveals that the use of RoIs improves the quality of learned features by providing focused supervision, while the supervoxel inpainting module enables the model to capture long-range dependencies and produce more accurate predictions. Overall, this work makes an important contribution to the field of self-supervised learning by introducing a simple yet effective technique that leverages the wealth of unlabelled data available today to improve the accuracy of various computer vision tasks.

title: Region-of-interest guided Supervoxel Inpainting for Self-supervision

title: Cross-denoising Network against Corrupted Labels in Medical Image Segmentation with Domain Shift

text: In medical image segmentation, accurate labeling is crucial to ensure reliable diagnosis and treatment. However, obtaining clean labels can be challenging due to various factors such as noise or domain shift. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach called Cross-Denoising Network (CDN) that leverages multiple degraded versions of images to improve segmentation performance in the presence of corrupted labels. CDN consists of two main components: (1) Denosing modules which remove noise from the input images through residual learning, and (2) segmentation modules which predict masks using both the denoised images and their corresponding noisy counterparts. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods under different types of noise and domain shifts. Our results indicate that CDN not only improves segmentation accuracy but also enhances robustness to varying degrees of corruption in labels. This work has important implications for developing more reliable medical image segmentation models that can adapt to diverse real-world scenarios.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for 3D medical image segmentation using elastic boundary projection. Our method leverages the concept of elasticity in image processing to accurately segment objects in 3D medical images. We propose a new algorithm that projects the image intensity values onto an elastic boundary, which is a surface that deforms to match the object boundary in the image. The resulting boundary is then used to segment the object from the rest of the image. We evaluate our method on several 3D medical image datasets, including MRI and CT scans, and compare it to traditional segmentation techniques. Our results show that elastic boundary projection outperforms traditional methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. We also demonstrate the versatility of our method by applying it to various medical applications, such as tumor segmentation, organ segmentation, and vessel segmentation. Our main contribution is the introduction of elastic boundary projection as a novel approach to 3D medical image segmentation. Our method has the advantage of being robust to noise and irregularities in the image data, and it can handle complex object boundaries with ease. Additionally, it is computationally efficient and can be used in real-time applications. Overall, our paper provides a significant contribution to the field of medical image segmentation, and it has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of various medical applications that rely on image segmentation.

title: Elastic Boundary Projection for 3D Medical Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to 3D medical image segmentation called Elastic Boundary Projection (EBP). EBP is designed to address the challenge of accurately separating complex anatomical structures in 3D medical images, where traditional methods often struggle due to the complexity and variability of boundary shapes. Our approach leverages the concept of elasticity to create a dynamic boundary that adapts to the underlying anatomy, enabling accurate segmenation even in the presence of large deformations and irregularities. The key idea behind EBP is to establish a continuous boundary that snugly fits around the object of interest, much like a rubber sheet fitting around a irregularly-shaped object. The proposed method consists of two main components: (1) a deep neural network that learns to project 2D image intensities onto an elastic boundary, and (2) an optimization algorithm that adjusts the boundary's shape and position to minimize an energy functional. We evaluate EBP on several publicly available datasets containing various types of 3D medical images, including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), and positron emission tomography (PET). Experimental results demonstrate that EBP outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Our approach has important implications for applications such as tumor tracking, organ segmentation, and surgical planning, where accurate separation of anatomical structures is crucial. Moreover, the ability of EBP to handle complex and varying boundary shapes makes it particularly well-suited for cases where traditional methods may struggle or fail. In summary, our paper introduces a novel, physically informed approach to 3D medical image segmentation that leverages elasticity to achieve accurate and robust separation of anatomical structures. With its ability to adapt to complex boundaries and deformations, EBP holds great promise for improving the accuracy and efficacy of various medical image analysis tasks.

title: Elastic Boundary Projection for 3D Medical Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation called Super-BPD, which stands for Super Boundary-to-Pixel Direction. This method is designed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of image segmentation by leveraging the concept of boundary direction and pixel-level information. Existing image segmentation methods often rely on boundary detection techniques that are computationally expensive and may produce incomplete or inaccurate results. In contrast, Super-BPD uses a novel approach that combines boundary direction and pixel-level information to quickly and accurately segment images. The proposed method consists of three stages: (1) boundary direction estimation, (2) boundary-to-pixel direction mapping, and (3) pixel labeling. In the first stage, we estimate the boundary direction of the image using a convolutional neural network (CNN). In the second stage, we map the boundary direction to pixel-level directions, which enables us to identify the pixels that are likely to belong to the object boundary. Finally, in the third stage, we use a pixel-wise classification algorithm to label each pixel with its corresponding class label. We evaluate the performance of Super-BPD on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes. The results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, Super-BPD achieves a higher mean Intersection over Union (mIoU) score than other methods while requiring fewer computational resources. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach to image segmentation called Super-BPD, which combines boundary direction and pixel-level information to improve efficiency and accuracy. * We introduce a new boundary direction estimation method that uses a CNN to estimate the boundary direction of an image. * We develop a boundary-to-pixel direction mapping technique that enables us to identify pixels that are likely to belong to the object boundary. * We demonstrate the superior performance of Super-BPD compared to state-of-the-art segmentation methods on several benchmark datasets. Overall, Super-BPD offers a significant improvement in image segmentation

title: Super-BPD: Super Boundary-to-Pixel Direction for Fast Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation called Super-BPD (Super Boundary-to-Pixel Direction), which significantly improves upon traditional boundary-based methods. Our approach uses a combination of boundary detection and pixel-level features to efficiently segment images into regions of similar texture or color. Existing boundary-based segmentation methods suffer from two main limitations: they are computationally expensive and produce results that are sensitive to the choice of parameters. Super-BPD addresses these issues by introducing a new directional feature that captures the similarity between pixels along different directions. This feature allows us to effectively partition an image into regions of coherent texture or color, without relying on explicit boundary detection. The key contribution of our work is the introduction of a superpixel-based framework that leverages both boundary information and pixel-level features to perform efficient and accurate image segmentation. We demonstrate the effectiveness of Super-BPD through extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets, showing that it outperforms state-of-the-art boundary-based methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Additionally, we provide a thorough analysis of the proposed method's computational complexity, proving that it scales linearly with the number of pixels in the image. Our experimental results show that Super-BPD achieves better performance compared to traditional boundary-based methods, while also being more robust to parameter variations. Furthermore, we applied Super-BPD to real-world applications such as object recognition, scene understanding, and medical image segmentation, where it demonstrated superior performance compared to existing approaches. In summary, Super-BPD offers a significant improvement over traditional boundary-based image segmentation methods, providing a faster, more accurate, and more robust solution for a wide range of computer vision tasks.

title: Super-BPD: Super Boundary-to-Pixel Direction for Fast Image Segmentation

text: Edge detection is a fundamental task in image processing and computer vision, and has been extensively studied in the past few decades. Recently, deep learning-based methods have gained popularity for their ability to learn complex features and achieve state-of-the-art performance. However, these methods often require large amounts of labeled data and computational resources, which can be limiting for real-world applications. In this paper, we propose a novel deep neural network architecture inspired by traditional edge detection methods, which can be efficiently deployed on resource-constrained devices at the edge of the network. Our approach leverages the strengths of traditional methods, such as the Sobel operator and Canny edge detector, to extract robust features that are less sensitive to noise and artifacts. We combine these features with a deep neural network to learn a hierarchical representation of the image, which enables accurate edge detection and segmentation. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art edge detection methods. Our results show that our proposed method outperforms traditional methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, while also providing better interpretability and robustness. We also demonstrate the feasibility of deploying our method on resource-constrained devices, such as smartphones and smart cameras, which is not feasible with existing deep learning-based methods. Our work has important implications for edge computing and IoT applications, where real-time processing and low latency are critical. By leveraging traditional methods and deep learning techniques, we can develop efficient and accurate edge detection systems that can be deployed in a variety of real-world scenarios.

title: Traditional Method Inspired Deep Neural Network for Edge Detection

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to edge detection using deep neural networks, inspired by traditional methods in image processing. The proposed method leverages the strengths of both worlds to improve the accuracy and efficiency of edge detection at low computational cost. Firstly, we review and analyze traditional methods for edge detection, such as Canny edge detection and Sobel operators, and identify their key components and limitations. We then employ these components to design a novel architecture for deep neural networks that can learn and adapt to various types of edges in images. Specifically, we propose a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture with custom-designed filters and activation functions that mimic the behavior of traditional edge detectors. The resulting model is able to effectively detect edges with high precision while reducing computational complexity compared to state-of-the-art CNN models. Furthermore, our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms traditional edge detection methods in terms of accuracy and robustness across different datasets and scenarios. text: This paper proposes a novel approach to eye fundus images segmentation using deep neural convolution networks with a convex shape prior. Eye fundus images are complex and contain various structures, including the optic disc, macula, and retinal vessels, which can be challenging to segment accurately. Existing methods often rely on hand-crafted features or shallow neural networks, which may not capture the underlying structure of the images effectively. In this paper, we propose a deep neural convolution network (CNN) that incorporates a convex shape prior to improve the segmentation accuracy. The convex shape prior is used to regularize the CNN's output, encouraging it to produce a smooth and convex segmentation mask. We also introduce a novel loss function that combines the traditional cross-entropy loss with a term that enforces the convexity of the segmentation mask. Our experiments on a publicly available dataset show that the proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods, including those that use hand-crafted features or shallow CNNs. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the convex shape prior and the novel loss function. The results demonstrate that the convex shape prior helps to improve the smoothness and accuracy of the segmentation mask, while the novel loss function helps to refine the boundaries of the segmented structures. The main contributions of this paper are: * A deep CNN with a convex shape prior for eye fundus images segmentation * A novel loss function that combines cross-entropy loss with a convexity regularization term * Experimental results that demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method and its superiority over state-of-the-art methods This paper has important implications for the diagnosis and treatment of eye diseases, as accurate segmentation of eye fundus images can help to identify diseases such as diabetic retinopathy and age-related macular degeneration. The proposed method has the potential to be used in clinical settings to improve the accuracy and efficiency of eye disease diagnosis.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation that leverages the power of meta-learning to learn effective initializations for segmentation models. Our approach, called Meta-Learning Initializations for Image Segmentation (MLIS), is designed to improve the efficiency and accuracy of image segmentation tasks by learning a good initialization for the segmentation model, which can be fine-tuned for a specific task with only a few training examples. Existing image segmentation methods typically require a large amount of labeled training data to achieve good performance. However, obtaining high-quality labels for medical images can be time-consuming and expensive. In contrast, our MLIS approach learns a good initialization for the segmentation model from a set of related tasks, which can be easily obtained from publicly available datasets. This enables the segmentation model to learn quickly and accurately for a specific task with only a few training examples. We evaluate the performance of MLIS on several medical image segmentation tasks, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. The results show that MLIS outperforms traditional image segmentation methods, including fully supervised models and transfer learning-based methods, in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Specifically, MLIS achieves an average segmentation accuracy of 95.2% on the brain tumor segmentation task with only 10 training examples, outperforming the next best method by 12.8%. Our analysis shows that the learned initialization from MLIS captures important features that are useful for image segmentation, such as edges, textures, and shapes. We also demonstrate that the learned initialization can be used to improve the performance of other segmentation models, such as U-Net and FCN. Our work has important implications for medical image segmentation, where obtaining large amounts of labeled data can be challenging. By leveraging the power of meta-learning, MLIS provides a promising solution to improve the efficiency and accuracy of image segmentation tasks, which can be applied to a wide range of medical applications.

title: Meta-Learning Initializations for Image Segmentation

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation using meta-learning. Traditional methods of image segmentation involve training a model on a specific dataset and require careful hyperparameter tuning for optimal performance. However, these models are not easily adaptable to new datasets or tasks. Meta-learning offers a promising solution by enabling learning of how to learn from a set of related tasks, allowing for more efficient adaptation to new tasks with only a few training examples. We focus specifically on the problem of initialization in image segmentation, where the choice of initialization can significantly impact the optimization process. By applying meta-learning to learn an optimal initialization for image segmentation models, we show that our proposed method, called Meta-Learning Initializations for Image Segmentation (MLI), outperforms traditional random initialization methods. MLI leverages knowledge gained from a set of pretext tasks to quickly adapt to new segmentation tasks, achieving state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks. Our main contributions include: 1. The proposal of a meta-learning framework for image segmentation initializations; 2. The development of a novel optimization algorithm that integrates meta-learning into popular deep learning architectures for segmentation; 3. An extensive evaluation demonstrating the superiority of MLI over conventional initialization strategies across various publicly available datasets. The significance of our work lies in the potential to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of image segmentation models, which has far-reaching implications in computer vision applications such as autonomous driving, medical imaging, and robotics. Our research paves the way for further exploration of meta-learning techniques in the field, ultimately leading to better generalization capabilities and improved utilization of computational resources.

text: Segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves dividing an image into its constituent parts or objects. Continual learning, which involves learning new tasks while preserving the knowledge gained from previous tasks, has become increasingly important in segmentation tasks, especially when dealing with multiple domains. In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Importance Driven Continual Learning (IDCL) for segmentation across domains. IDCL addresses the problem of catastrophic forgetting, which occurs when a model forgets previously learned knowledge when learning a new task, by assigning importance scores to the samples in the new domain. The importance scores are used to select the most informative samples for training, which helps to preserve the knowledge gained from previous tasks. In addition, IDCL uses a domain adaptation technique to align the features from the new domain with the features from the source domain, which helps to improve the performance of the model on the new domain. We evaluate IDCL on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and demonstrate its effectiveness in comparison to state-of-the-art continual learning methods. Our results show that IDCL achieves better performance on the new domain while preserving the performance on the source domain. We also perform an ablation study to analyze the effectiveness of the importance scores and the domain adaptation technique, and show that both components are crucial for the success of IDCL. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach called Importance Driven Continual Learning (IDCL) for segmentation across domains, which assigns importance scores to samples in the new domain and uses domain adaptation to align features from the new domain with features from the source domain. * An effective way to preserve knowledge gained from previous tasks while learning new tasks, which addresses the problem of catastrophic forgetting. * State-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of IDCL in comparison to other continual learning methods. IDCL has important implications for real-world applications, such as autonomous driving, where a model needs to learn new tasks while preserving the knowledge gained from previous tasks. It can also be applied to other computer vision tasks,

IDCL addresses this challenge by incorporating domain importance into the learning process, allowing the model to prioritize learning from the most relevant domains and adapt effectively to new environments. The proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating superior performance compared to state-of-the-art continual learning and domain adaptation approaches. Additionally, IDCL is shown to be robust against forgetting, preserving previous knowledge while adapting to new domains. The significance of importance-driven learning is further validated through ablation studies and analysis of learner behavior. Key contributions of this work include: 1. text: This paper proposes a novel approach for color image segmentation using adaptive particle swarm optimization and fuzzy C-means (APSO-FCM) algorithm. The proposed method combines the strengths of particle swarm optimization (PSO) and fuzzy C-means (FCM) algorithms to achieve efficient and accurate segmentation of color images. In the proposed method, the PSO algorithm is used to optimize the parameters of the FCM algorithm, which are the membership values of each pixel to different classes. The PSO algorithm is a population-based optimization technique that is inspired by the social behavior of bird flocking and fish schooling. It uses a swarm of particles to search for the optimal solution in the search space. The APSO-FCM algorithm is tested on several benchmark color image datasets, and the results are compared with those obtained using traditional FCM and PSO algorithms. The results show that the proposed method outperforms the traditional methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. The main contributions of this paper are: 1. A novel approach for color image segmentation using adaptive particle swarm optimization and fuzzy C-means algorithm. 2. An effective combination of PSO and FCM algorithms to achieve efficient and accurate segmentation of color images. 3. A comprehensive evaluation of the proposed method on several benchmark color image datasets. The paper is organized as follows: The next section provides a brief review of the related work on image segmentation, PSO, and FCM algorithms. The following section describes the proposed APSO-FCM algorithm in detail. The experimental results and analysis are presented in the fourth section. Finally, the conclusion and future work are discussed in the last section. Overall, this paper provides a novel approach for color image segmentation using adaptive particle swarm optimization and fuzzy C-means algorithm, which can be useful for various applications such as object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding.

In this work, an adaptive PSO algorithm is proposed that dynamically adjusts the population size based on the convergence progress of the optimization process. The adaptive PSO algorithm is combined with FCM clustering to separate the image into its constituent parts. The proposed method was tested on several benchmark images and compared to existing methods, including traditional PSO and FCM. The main contribution of this paper lies in the development of an adaptive PSO algorithm that can effectively optimize the objective function in color image segmentation problems. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to medical image segmentation using semi-supervised few-shot learning. To address this challenge, we leverage the large amount of unannotated data available in medical imaging datasets and utilize semi-supervised learning techniques to improve the performance of few-shot learning models. Our proposed approach combines a self-supervised loss function with a few-shot learning algorithm, allowing the model to learn from both labeled and unlabeled data. text: This paper proposes a novel approach to document image segmentation using a fast fully octave convolutional neural network (CNN). text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to unified perceptual parsing, which is a fundamental problem in computer vision that involves assigning a single label to each pixel in an image, indicating its belonging to a specific object or material category. Our approach, called the Deep Grouping Model (DGM), leverages the strengths of both deep learning and grouping-based methods to achieve state-of-the-art performance on this task. Existing methods for unified perceptual parsing often rely on hand-crafted features and heuristics, which are limited in their ability to capture the complexity and variability of real-world scenes. In contrast, DGM uses a deep neural network to learn a rich representation of the input image, which is then grouped into a small set of clusters using a novel grouping algorithm. In contrast, DGM leverages recent advances in deep learning to learn hierarchical representations of images and jointly perform multiple tasks related to perceptual parsing, such as object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. Our key innovation is the use of a grouping module that iteratively refines the output of a backbone network, progressively partitioning the image into more coherent regions. This allows the model to capture complex contextual relationships between objects, leading to improved performance across all tasks. We demonstrate the effectiveness of DGM on several benchmark datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results with significantly fewer parameters than existing methods. Moreover, we show that DGM can be easily adapted to different dataset settings and outperform task-specific baselines. The main contributions of this work are: 1. State-of-the-art performance on various benchmarks while using fewer parameters compared to current state-of-the-art methods. 4. Adaptability to diverse dataset settings, validating the versatility and robustness of our proposed approach. Our findings have important implications for computer vision research, showing that jointly training models for multiple perceptual parsing tasks can lead to better performance and more efficient use of resources. text: In this paper, we address the problem of training machine learning models on noisy structured labels, which can significantly impact the accuracy and reliability of the model's predictions. Existing approaches to handle noisy labels often rely on simplistic heuristics or assumptions that may not always hold, particularly in complex real-world scenarios. Our proposed method, called Improved Training on Noisy Structured Labels (ITNSL), leverages recent advances in deep learning and structured prediction to improve the robustness of machine learning models to noisy labels. ITNSL consists of two main components: (1) a noise-aware loss function that can handle a variety of noise models and (2) a regularization term that encourages the model to produce smooth predictions. We evaluate ITNSL on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art methods for handling noisy labels. Our results show that ITNSL significantly outperforms existing approaches in terms of accuracy and robustness to noise. We also demonstrate the versatility of ITNSL by applying it to various machine learning tasks, including image classification, sentiment analysis, and named entity recognition. Our work has important implications for real-world applications where labeled data is often noisy, incomplete, or corrupted. By developing methods that can effectively handle noisy structured labels, we can improve the reliability and accuracy of machine learning models in a wide range of domains, from healthcare to finance. In summary, this paper presents a novel approach to improving training on noisy structured labels, which can significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of machine learning models in various applications. text: This paper proposes a novel variational image segmentation model that incorporates normalized cuts with adaptive similarity and spatial regularization. The proposed model aims to overcome the limitations of traditional segmentation techniques that rely on hand-crafted features and fixed parameters, which can lead to suboptimal performance in certain applications. The proposed model formulates image segmentation as a variational problem, where the goal is to minimize an energy functional that captures the underlying structure of the image. The energy functional combines a data term, which encourages the segmentation to be consistent with the image data, and a regularization term, which promotes spatial smoothness and adaptive similarity between neighboring pixels. The key innovation of the proposed model is the use of adaptive similarity, which allows the model to adapt to the local image structure and capture finer details in the segmentation. The adaptive similarity is achieved by learning a set of similarity weights that are spatially varying and take into account the local image features. The proposed model also incorporates spatial regularization, which helps to smooth the segmentation boundaries and reduce artifacts. The spatial regularization term is designed to be flexible and can be adjusted based on the complexity of the image structure. The model is optimized using a efficient optimization algorithm, which enables fast and accurate segmentation of large images. The proposed model is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, and the results demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * A novel variational image segmentation model that incorporates normalized cuts with adaptive similarity and spatial regularization. * An efficient optimization algorithm that enables fast and accurate segmentation of large images. * Superior performance compared to state-of-the-art segmentation techniques on several benchmark datasets. The proposed model has important applications in various fields, including object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding, and has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation tasks.

text: This paper proposes a novel image segmentation model that combines the normalized cut algorithm with adaptive similarity and spatial regularization. The proposed method, called variational image segmentation (VIS), is designed to improve the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation tasks, particularly in situations where the images have complex structures or low contrast between objects. The VIS model formulates image segmentation as a variational problem, where the goal is to minimize an energy function that incorporates both data fidelity and regularization terms. The energy function includes a normalized cut term that encourages the segmentation to be consistent with the image gradient information, an adaptive similarity term that captures the similarity between pixels in the same class, and a spatial regularization term that enforces smoothness of the segmentation boundary. To solve the variational problem, we propose an efficient optimization algorithm that iteratively updates the segmentation mask and the auxiliary variables using a alternate direction method of multipliers. The algorithm ensures that the resulting segmentation masks are not only accurate but also consistent with the given image data. Extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance of VIS compared to state-of-the-art image segmentation methods, including traditional algorithms such as thresholding, edge detection, and clustering, as well as deep learning-based approaches such as fully convolutional networks (FCN) and U-Net. Additionally, we show that VIS can handle real-world applications, such as medical imaging and object tracking, effectively. Overall, this paper makes significant contributions to the field of image segmentation by introducing a new approach that leverages normalized cut, adaptive similarity, and spatial regularization to produce high-quality segmentation results efficiently. The proposed method has important implications for various applications where image segmentation plays a key role.

text: In this paper, we address the problem of computing valid p-values for image segmentation, a fundamental task in computer vision. Existing methods for computing p-values for image segmentation often rely on simplistic models and assumptions, which can lead to invalid p-values and incorrect conclusions. We propose a new method called Selective Inference for computing valid p-values for image segmentation. Our method is based on a careful consideration of the inference process and the selection of relevant features for each image. We demonstrate that Selective Inference can significantly improve the accuracy of p-values compared to existing methods. We evaluate the performance of Selective Inference on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art methods. Our results show that Selective Inference achieves higher accuracy and robustness in computing p-values, leading to more reliable and accurate segmentation results. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding. We demonstrate the practicality and effectiveness of Selective Inference through experiments on real-world images. Overall, this paper makes a significant contribution to the field of image segmentation by providing a reliable and accurate method for computing p-values, which can be used to evaluate and validate segmentation results.

text: The paper "Computing Valid p-values for Image Segmentation by Selective Inference" presents a novel approach to computing valid p-values for image segmentation techniques. Traditional methods of evaluating segmentation algorithms rely on arbitrarily defined thresholds or subjective visual inspection, which can lead to inconsistent and unreliable results. In this work, we propose a method based on selective inference that allows for the computation of valid p-values for image segmentation. Our approach leverages recent advances in statistical inference and machine learning to provide a principled way of evaluating the significance of segmentation results. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method through experiments on several benchmark datasets, where we show that it provides more accurate and robust assessments of segmentation performance compared to traditional methods. The key contribution of this paper is the introduction of a new evaluation framework for image segmentation that is grounded in statistical hypothesis testing. By treating segmentation as a hypothesis test, we can compute p-values that indicate the probability that the observed segmentation result occurred by chance. This allows us to quantify the uncertainty in segmentation results and provides a meaningful way to compare the performance of different algorithms. Our proposed method has important implications for the field of image segmentation, as it enables researchers to evaluate and compare segmentation algorithms in a more objective and reliable manner. It also opens up new avenues for future research, such as investigating the relationship between segmentation performance and underlying factors like image quality or object size. Overall, this work represents an important step towards developing a more rigorous understanding of image segmentation and its limitations.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for generating image collages that combines the strengths of guided and unguided methods. Existing methods for image collage generation can be broadly classified into two categories: guided and unguided. Guided methods use explicit instructions or templates to generate collages, resulting in predictable outcomes but limited creativity. On the other hand, unguided methods rely solely on algorithmic combinations of images, often leading to visually appealing but lacking contextual relevance. Our TTS method addresses these limitations by introducing a memory attentive module that learns to attend to the input images and their relationships during the generative process. Demonstration of the versatility of TTS in different application scenarios, including artistic collages, fashion design, and data visualization. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for identifying cells' nuclei using deep learning neural networks. The accurate detection of cell nuclei is crucial in various biomedical applications, including cancer diagnosis, cell counting, and cell classification. Traditional methods for cell nuclei detection rely on manual segmentation, which is time-consuming and prone to errors. To address this challenge, we propose a deep learning-based method that utilizes a convolutional neural network (CNN) to automatically detect cell nuclei in microscopic images. Our approach involves training the CNN on a large dataset of labeled microscopic images, where the cell nuclei are annotated with bounding boxes. We evaluate the performance of our method using a set of test images and compare it with traditional manual segmentation. Our results show that the CNN-based method outperforms manual segmentation in terms of accuracy, efficiency, and robustness. We also demonstrate the versatility of our approach by applying it to various types of microscopic images, including brightfield, phase contrast, and fluorescence microscopy. The main contribution of this paper is the development of a deep learning-based method for accurate and efficient detection of cell nuclei in microscopic images. Our approach has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and speed of various biomedical applications that rely on cell nuclei detection.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for CNN-based segmentation in radiotherapy treatment planning, which exploits clinically available delineations to improve the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation process. Traditional segmentation methods rely on manual contouring by experts, which can be time-consuming and subjective. Our approach leverages the wealth of clinical knowledge embedded in existing delineations, such as tumor volumes, organs-at-risk, and treatment margins, to guide the segmentation process. We propose a two-stage framework, consisting of a pre-processing step and a segmentation step. In the pre-processing step, we extract a set of features from the clinically available delineations, such as shape, size, and location. These features are then used to create a set of regions of interest (ROIs) that are likely to contain the target structures. In the segmentation step, we apply a CNN to each ROI to refine the segmentation and accurately delineate the target structures. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of 30 patients with various types of tumors and treatment sites. The results show that our approach significantly improves the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation process compared to traditional methods. Specifically, we achieve an average Dice similarity coefficient of 0.85 and a mean absolute error of 1.2 mm, while reducing the segmentation time by 75%. Our approach has the potential to revolutionize the field of radiotherapy treatment planning by providing a fast, accurate, and robust segmentation method that can be widely adopted in clinical practice. By exploiting clinically available delineations, we can reduce the burden on experts and improve the consistency and quality of treatment plans. This work paves the way for further research in using AI techniques to improve radiotherapy treatment planning and ultimately enhance patient outcomes.

text: Medical image segmentation is a fundamental task in healthcare, with the aim of accurately separating the pixels of a medical image into distinct regions of interest. Evaluating the performance of segmentation algorithms is crucial to ensure that the resulting segments are reliable and useful for clinical decision-making. Two commonly used metrics for evaluating segmentation performance are the Dice score and Jaccard index. However, optimizing these metrics can be challenging, especially when dealing with complex medical images. This paper presents a comprehensive review of the Dice score and Jaccard index, including their theoretical foundations and practical applications in medical image segmentation. We discuss the limitations and drawbacks of these metrics, as well as strategies for optimizing them. We also explore the use of these metrics in deep learning-based segmentation methods, such as convolutional neural networks (CNNs). Our review covers a range of topics, including: 1. Introduction to medical image segmentation and its importance in healthcare. 2. Overview of the Dice score and Jaccard index, including their mathematical formulations and interpretation. 3. Limitations and drawbacks of the Dice score and Jaccard index, including their sensitivity to class imbalance and spatial overlap. 4. Strategies for optimizing the Dice score and Jaccard index, including data augmentation, ensemble learning, and post-processing techniques. 5. Application of the Dice score and Jaccard index in deep learning-based segmentation methods, including CNNs. 6. Future directions for research and development in medical image segmentation evaluation. This paper serves as a valuable resource for researchers, practitioners, and students working in the field of medical image segmentation. By providing a comprehensive understanding of the Dice score and Jaccard index, as well as practical strategies for optimizing these metrics, we hope to improve the accuracy and reliability of medical image segmentation and ultimately contribute to better patient outcomes.

text: In this paper, we present a comprehensive approach to optimizing the Dice score and Jaccard index for medical image segmentation. These two metrics are widely used in the field to evaluate the performance of segmentation algorithms, with the Dice score measuring overlap between the predicted mask and ground truth, and the Jaccard index assessing their similarity. Despite their importance, existing methods for maximizing these metrics often rely on heuristics or intuition rather than a principled optimization framework. In contrast, our proposed method takes a theoretical approach, leveraging tools from optimization theory to directly optimize the Dice score and Jaccard index. We first derive the optimal transport formulation of the segmentation problem, which enables us to cast the task as minimization of a linear program (LP). By solving this LP, we obtain theoptimal parameters that maximize the Dice score and Jaccard index simultaneously. To ensure computational efficiency, we also propose an approximate solution based on gradient descent with provable guarantees. Our experimental evaluation on several benchmark datasets demonstrates significant improvements over state-of-the-art segmentation techniques. Moreover, we perform ablation studies to dissect the contributions of various components in our methodology, providing valuable insights into the role of each component in enhancing performance. Our results not only establish new baselines for medical image segmentation tasks but also shed light on the inherent tradeoffs between different objectives, paving the way for more informed design choices in future research. The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows: 1. A theoretically grounded approach to optimizing the Dice score and Jaccard index via optimal transport and linear programming. 2. An efficient algorithm that scales up to large datasets while maintaining competitive performance. 3. Empirical validation through extensive experiments on diverse medical imaging modalities. 4. Insightful ablation studies elucidating the interplay between various factors influencing segmentation quality. 5. Establishment of a new benchmark for segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for scene parsing, called Deep Multiphase Level Set (DMLS), which leverages the strengths of deep learning and level set methods to accurately segment objects in complex scenes. Scene parsing is the task of assigning a semantic label to each pixel in an image, and it has numerous applications in computer vision, robotics, and autonomous driving. Existing scene parsing methods suffer from several limitations, such as their inability to handle complex scenes with multiple objects, their sensitivity to initialization, and their reliance on hand-crafted features. To address these limitations, we propose a deep learning-based approach that uses a multiphase level set formulation to segment objects in a scene. The DMLS method consists of three main components: (1) a deep neural network that learns to predict the level set function for each object in the scene, (2) a multiphase level set formulation that uses the predicted level set functions to segment the objects, and (3) a optimization-based post-processing step that refines the segmentation results. We evaluate the DMLS method on several challenging datasets, including the PASCAL VOC dataset, the Cityscapes dataset, and the BSDS500 dataset. Our experiments show that DMLS outperforms state-of-the-art scene parsing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. We also demonstrate the versatility of DMLS by applying it to various computer vision tasks, such as object proposal generation, instance segmentation, and semantic segmentation. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel deep learning-based approach for scene parsing, called Deep Multiphase Level Set (DMLS), which combines the strengths of deep learning and level set methods. * We introduce a multiphase level set formulation that uses the predicted level set functions to segment objects in a scene. * We develop an optimization-based post-processing step that refines the segmentation results and improves the accuracy of the method. * We evaluate the DMLS method on several challenging datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art scene parsing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency.

text: NeurReg: Neural Registration and Its Application to Image Segmentation Abstract: Image registration is a fundamental task in image processing that involves aligning multiple images of the same scene or object taken at different times or from different viewpoints. Traditional image registration methods rely on hand-crafted features and optimization techniques, which can be time-consuming and limited in their ability to handle complex deformations. In this paper, we propose a deep learning-based approach called NeurReg, which leverages neural networks to learn a mapping between images and their corresponding transformations. NeurReg consists of two main components: a registration network and a segmentation network. The registration network takes two images as input and outputs a transformation matrix that aligns the images. The segmentation network takes the aligned images as input and outputs a segmentation mask that separates the objects of interest from the background. We train NeurReg using a large dataset of images with manual segmentation masks, and evaluate its performance on several benchmark datasets. Our experiments show that NeurReg outperforms traditional image registration methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. It also demonstrates superior performance in handling complex deformations and occlusions, which are challenging for traditional methods. Additionally, NeurReg can be used for a variety of applications, including image-guided surgery, object tracking, and 3D reconstruction. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of NeurReg. The results show that the use of a segmentation network and the integration of multi-scale features are crucial for its performance. Furthermore, we demonstrate the generalization ability of NeurReg by applying it to unseen data, including images with different modalities and resolutions. In conclusion, NeurReg presents a novel deep learning-based approach to image registration and segmentation, which has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of image registration tasks in various applications.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for synthetic segmentation without target modality ground truth, which we term SynSeg-Net. We evaluate the performance of SynSeg-Net on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in segmenting images from different modalities, including medical imaging and remote sensing. Our results show that SynSeg-Net outperforms traditional segmentation methods that rely on manually annotated target modalities, and achieves high accuracy on par with fully supervised methods. The key contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel loss function that enables the training of a segmentation network without target modality ground truth. Our loss function combines a traditional segmentation loss with an adversarial term that encourages the network to produce segmentation masks that are consistent across different modalities. This allows the network to learn a shared representation across modalities, which improves segmentation performance and reduces the need for annotated target modality data. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including medical imaging, remote sensing, and computer vision. With the ability to perform synthetic segmentation without target modality ground truth, we can reduce the time and cost associated with manual annotation, and improve the accuracy and robustness of segmentation models. In summary, SynSeg-Net is a novel approach for synthetic segmentation that leverages the power of deep learning to segment images from multiple modalities without the need for manually annotated target modality ground truth. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image-to-image translation for semantic segmentation, which we term "extremely weak supervised image-to-image translation" (EWSIT). This approach leverages a small number of labeled images to train a deep neural network to translate images from a source domain to a target domain, while also predicting the semantic segmentation of the target image. Unlike traditional image-to-image translation methods that require a large number of labeled images, our EWSIT approach uses a combination of adversarial training and self-supervised learning to learn the translation process with a minimal number of labeled images. Specifically, we use a adversarial loss function to ensure that the translated images are visually similar to the target domain, while also using a self-supervised loss function to ensure that the translated images have the same semantic meaning as the source image. We evaluate our EWSIT approach on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO. Our results show that our method outperforms traditional image-to-image translation methods that require a large number of labeled images, while also achieving state-of-the-art performance on semantic segmentation tasks. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach to image-to-image translation for semantic segmentation that requires a minimal number of labeled images * A combination of adversarial training and self-supervised learning to learn the translation process * State-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets for semantic segmentation Our work has important implications for applications where labeled data is scarce, such as medical imaging and autonomous driving. With the ability to train deep neural networks with a minimal number of labeled images, we can reduce the cost and time required for training, while still achieving high accuracy performance.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image-to-image translation for semantic segmentation tasks, which we term "extremely weak supervision." This method leverages a small number of labeled images, typically in the order of tens or hundreds, to train a deep neural network to translate between two different imaging modalities. The translated images are then used to obtain dense and accurate semantic segmentations without requiring any additional annotation efforts. Our proposed approach is based on a combination of adversarial training and self-supervised learning techniques. Specifically, we use a CycleGAN architecture to perform image-to-image translations, while incorporating an additional discriminator branch that enforces the generated images to follow the statistical properties of the target modality. Moreover, we introduce a new regularization term that encourages the preservation of semantic information during the translation process. We evaluate our method on several publicly available datasets including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and Mapillary Vistas, where it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art weakly supervised segmentation methods. Our experiments demonstrate that with only a few dozen labeled images, our approach can produce high-quality segmentations that accurately capture the desired semantics. Furthermore, by gradually increasing the amount of annotated data, we show that our method adapts well to improved supervision conditions and consistently achieves better performance. The main contributions of this work include: (1) introducing extremely weak supervision for image-to-image translation-based semantic segmentation, (2) developing a robust and effective training strategy combining adversarial and self-supervised learning, and (3) demonstrating superior performance compared to existing weakly supervised segmentation approaches. This research has broad implications for applications where labeled data is scarce or difficult to acquire, such as medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics. With its ability to generate highly accurate semantic segmentations from minuscule annotation effort, our technique paves the way for more efficient and scalable deployments of machine vision systems in real-world scenarios.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to image segmentation using deep learning, specifically the Mumford-Shah loss functional. The Mumford-Shah loss functional is a regularization term that encourages the segmentation mask to have a piecewise constant structure, which is well-suited for images with complex structures. In this paper, we incorporate the Mumford-Shah loss functional into a deep learning framework for image segmentation, and demonstrate its effectiveness in segmenting images with varying levels of complexity. Experiments conducted on several benchmark datasets show that the proposed method outperforms traditional deep learning-based segmentation methods, such as fully convolutional networks (FCNs) and U-Net, in terms of segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency. We also show that the Mumford-Shah loss functional is able to capture the underlying structure of the images, leading to more accurate and interpretable segmentation results. The main contributions of this paper are: * The introduction of the Mumford-Shah loss functional for image segmentation with deep learning * The development of an efficient optimization algorithm for minimizing the Mumford-Shah loss functional in a deep learning framework * An extensive evaluation of the proposed method on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its superior performance compared to traditional methods Overall, this paper presents a novel approach to image segmentation using the Mumford-Shah loss functional, which can be useful for applications where accurate and interpretable segmentation results are crucial, such as medical imaging and object detection.

title: Mumford-Shah Loss Functional for Image Segmentation with Deep Learning

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to image segmentation using deep learning and the Mumford-Shah loss functional. The Mumford-Shah functional is a popular tool in image processing and computer vision that measures the similarity between two images based on their gradient information. However, its use in deep learning frameworks has been limited due to its non-differentiable nature. To overcome this limitation, we introduce a differentiable variant of the Mumford-Shah functional that can be seamlessly integrated into deep neural networks. Our proposed method, called Mumford-Shah loss functional for image segmentation (MSLF), combines the strengths of traditional image segmentation techniques with the power of deep learning algorithms. The MSLF objective function consists of two main components: a data fidelity term that ensures accurate prediction of the output label maps, and a regularization term that encourages smoothness and consistency in the predicted labels. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and CamVid. Experiments show that MSLF outperforms state-of-the-art image segmentation methods, including fully convolutional network (FCN) and U-Net, in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Additionally, we perform an ablation study to analyze the contribution of different components of the MSLF functional, providing insights into the role of each component in improving segmentation performance. Our work marks the first successful integration of the Mumford-Shah functional into deep learning frameworks for image segmentation tasks. We believe that MSLF has great potential for applications in various fields such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging, where accurate image segmentation is critical.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for semi-supervised video segmentation that leverages the temporal information present in video data. Our approach, called Temporal-based Semi-Supervised Video Segmentation (TSSVS), exploits the temporal relationships between frames in a video sequence to improve the accuracy of segmentation. Existing semi-supervised video segmentation methods rely heavily on the spatial information present in individual frames, which can lead to limited performance due to the lack of labeled data. In contrast, TSSVS incorporates temporal information into the segmentation process, enabling the model to learn from the temporal relationships between frames and improve segmentation accuracy. The proposed approach consists of two main components: (1) a temporal attention module that learns to weight the importance of different frames in the video sequence based on their relevance to the current frame, and (2) a segmentation module that uses the weighted frames to perform segmentation. We evaluate TSSVS on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art semi-supervised video segmentation methods. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach for semi-supervised video segmentation that leverages temporal information * A temporal attention module that learns to weight the importance of different frames in the video sequence * A segmentation module that uses the weighted frames to improve segmentation accuracy * Improved performance on several benchmark datasets compared to state-of-the-art semi-supervised video segmentation methods. The proposed approach has important implications for video analysis tasks, as it can improve the accuracy of segmentation in scenarios where labeled data is scarce.
