text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for semi-supervised video segmentation that leverages temporal information to improve segmentation accuracy. Existing methods for semi-supervised video segmentation typically rely on spatial annotations, which are often limited in quantity and quality. By incorporating temporal information into the segmentation process, our approach can effectively exploit the redundancy and consistency of objects across frames, leading to improved performance. We introduce a new algorithm that integrates a temporally-aware graph convolutional network (T-GCN) with a spatial graph convolutional network (S-GCN) to simultaneously leverage spatial and temporal information. The T-GCN captures long-term dependencies between frames, while the S-GCN refines the segmentation results by considering local spatial cues. Our proposed method is evaluated on three benchmark datasets, and experimental results demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods. The key contributions of our work are: 1. We propose a novel semi-supervised video segmentation framework that integrates temporal and spatial information to improve segmentation accuracy. 2. We develop a temporally-aware graph convolutional network (T-GCN) that captures long-term dependencies between frames, enhancing the representation capacity of the model. 3. We design a hybrid segmentation pipeline that combines the strengths of both T-GCN and S-GCN, generating more accurate and robust segmentation masks. 4. Extensive experiments conducted on three datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach and its advantage over existing methods. Our work opens up new directions for future research in semi-supervised video segmentation, highlighting the importance of exploring temporal information to enhance segmentation performance.

To address these limitations, ACE-Net introduces three key components: (1) augmented contracting paths, (2) expansive paths, and (3) feature pyramid networks. Feature pyramid networks are employed to fuse low-level, middle-level, and high-level features, improving the network's ability to capture both local and global information. ACE-Net is evaluated on several publicly available biomedical image segmentation datasets, including brain tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and lung segmentation. The experimental results demonstrate that ACE-Net outperforms several state-of-the-art segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and robustness. text: ACE-Net: Biomedical Image Segmentation with Augmented Contracting and Expansive Paths In this paper, we present a novel deep learning architecture called ACE-Net (Augmented Contracting and Expansive Network), designed specifically for biomedical image segmentation tasks. The proposed network leverages both contracting and expansive paths to effectively captured spatial information and contextual details in medical images. The contracting path of ACE-Net consists of convolutional layers that gradually reduce the spatial resolution while increasing the number of feature channels, allowing it to capture high-level semantic features from the input image. In contrast, the expansive path utilizes dilated convolutions and upsampling operations to increase the spatial resolution, enabling the network to recover fine details and precise boundaries. Furthermore, we introduce an augmentation module that strategically duplicates and jitteres the input images during training, fostering the network's ability to generalize across various imaging protocols and artifacts. Additionally, we implement a skip connection mechanism between the contracting and expansive paths, facilitating the integration of multi-scale information and improving segmentation performance. We evaluate ACE-Net on several publicly available datasets comprising diverse modalities, including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), computed tomography (CT), and microscopy images. Our experiments show that ACE-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods, demonstrating its superiority in accurately delineating complex structures and objects. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to long-term visual localization that leverages fine-grained segmentation networks to improve the accuracy and robustness of the localization system. Existing localization methods rely on coarse segmentation methods that are prone to errors, especially in dynamic environments. In contrast, our proposed method uses self-supervised fine-grained segmentation networks to learn a rich and detailed representation of the environment, leading to improved localization performance. We evaluate our method on several challenging datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art localization methods in terms of accuracy and robustness. In addition, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in real-world scenarios, including scenarios with significant changes in lighting, viewpoint, and occlusion. Our key contribution is the development of a self-supervised fine-grained segmentation network that can learn to segment the environment into small, meaningful regions without the need for manual annotation. This allows us to train a localization system that is not only more accurate but also more robust to changes in the environment. The main idea behind our approach is to use a self-supervised loss function that encourages the network to predict a consistent segmentation across different frames. This loss function is combined with a localization loss function that encourages the network to predict the correct location of the agent in the environment. The combination of these two loss functions allows the network to learn a rich and detailed representation of the environment that is useful for localization. We evaluate the performance of our method using several metrics, including mean absolute error and frequency of correct localization. Our results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art localization methods in terms of accuracy and robustness. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the contribution of different components of our method, providing insights into the effectiveness of our approach. Overall, our paper makes a significant contribution to the field of long-term visual localization by introducing a novel approach that leverages fine-grained segmentation networks to improve the accuracy and robustness of the localization system. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to long-term visual localization that leverages fine-grained segmentation networks (FGSN) for improved performance. Existing methods for visual localization rely on coarse segmentation techniques that often result in reduced accuracy and robustness. FGSN addresses this limitation by introducing a self-supervised segmenation framework that learns to segment images at multiple scales and levels of detail. This allows for more accurate and robust feature extraction, resulting in improved localization performance. Our experimental results show that FGSN outperforms state-of-the-art methods on several challenging benchmark datasets, including those with varying illumination conditions, viewpoints, and occlusions. We also demonstrate the generalizability of our approach by evaluating it on real-world scenarios, where FGSN shows superior performance compared to traditional localization methods. The key contribution of this work is the introduction of a novel architecture that integrates a self-supervised segmentation network with a convolutional neural network (CNN) for visual localization. The proposed architecture enables the learning of fine-grained features that are essential for reliable long-term localization. Our extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of FGSN and establish it as a new baseline for visual localization tasks.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel, plug-and-play multi-task module for medical image segmentation called Conv-MCD. Our approach leverages the strengths of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and multi-task learning to improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation. Existing medical image segmentation methods often rely on task-specific models that are trained on individual datasets, leading to limited performance and scalability. In contrast, Conv-MCD adopts a multi-task learning framework that enables the sharing of knowledge across different segmentation tasks, resulting in improved performance and reduced training time. The key innovation of Conv-MCD is the use of a novel module that integrates convolutional layers with a multi-task classification layer. This module, called the Multi-task Convolutional Dense (MCD) module, is designed to learn a shared representation across multiple segmentation tasks, enabling the model to adapt to new tasks with minimal additional training data. We evaluate Conv-MCD on several publicly available medical image segmentation datasets, including brain tumor segmentation, lung segmentation, and liver segmentation. Our results show that Conv-MCD outperforms state-of-the-art single-task and multi-task segmentation models, while also demonstrating improved scalability and adaptability to new tasks. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel, plug-and-play multi-task module called Conv-MCD that leverages the strengths of CNNs and multi-task learning for medical image segmentation. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of Conv-MCD on several medical image segmentation datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art single-task and multi-task models. * We show that Conv-MCD exhibits improved scalability and adaptability to new tasks, reducing the need for task-specific models and large-scale retraining. Overall, Conv-MCD has the potential to significantly improve the accuracy and efficiency of medical image segmentation, and we envision its application in a variety of medical imaging domains.

Conv-MCD combines the strengths of multi-task learning and dilated convolutions to improve the efficiency and accuracy of medical image segmentation. By sharing features across different tasks, multi-task learning enables the model to learn more robust representations and generalize better to unseen data. Dilated convolutions, on the other hand, increase the receptive field of the network without increasing the number of parameters, allowing it to capture larger contextual information in medical images. We evaluate Conv-MCD on several publicly available medical image datasets, including lung tumor segmentation, liver segmentation, and brain tumor segmentation. Experimental results show that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art single-task and multi-task learning methods in terms of both segmentation accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically, Conv-MCD achieves an average dice similarity coefficient of 0.832 on the lung tumor dataset, 0.945 on the liver dataset, and 0.817 on the brain tumor dataset, while reducing the number of parameters and computations required compared to existing methods. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to improve the performance of fully convolutional networks (FCNs) by learning what to attend to in images. Our approach, called AttentionBoost, leverages the strengths of recent attention mechanisms to boost the performance of FCNs in various computer vision tasks. Existing attention mechanisms have shown promising results in natural language processing and image captioning, but they have limitations when applied to image classification and object detection tasks. Specifically, these mechanisms are designed to focus on specific parts of an image, but they often struggle to identify the most relevant regions when dealing with complex images or multiple objects. AttentionBoost addresses this issue by introducing a novel attention module that learns to attend to the most discriminative regions of an image. Our module is designed to be lightweight and can be easily integrated into existing FCN architectures. We evaluate AttentionBoost on several benchmark datasets, including CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and PASCAL VOC. The key contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel attention mechanism that learns to attend to the most discriminative regions of an image, leading to improved performance in image classification and object detection tasks. Our approach outperforms existing attention mechanisms and achieves state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks. The paper is organized as follows: In section 2, we review related work on attention mechanisms and their applications in computer vision. In section 3, we introduce the AttentionBoost module and its architecture. In section 4, we describe the experimental setup and the datasets used for evaluation. In section 5, we present the results of our experiments and compare AttentionBoost with existing attention mechanisms. Finally, in section 6, we conclude the paper and discuss future directions for improving AttentionBoost. Overall, AttentionBoost offers a promising approach to improving the performance of FCNs in various computer vision tasks, and we believe it has the potential to be widely adopted in the field.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for improving the performance of fully convolutional networks (FCNs) in image classification tasks. Our method, called AttentionBoost, leverages the concept of attention mechanisms to learn an effective way of attending to relevant regions in images, which in turn enhances the accuracy and efficiency of FCNs. Existing approaches for improving FCNs have focused on enhancing the network architecture or using transfer learning techniques. However, these methods often require significant modifications to the original network structure or large amounts of labeled data, which can be limiting. In contrast, AttentionBoost is a lightweight and flexible technique that can be easily integrated into existing FCN architectures without requiring extensive changes or additional training data. The key idea behind AttentionBoost is to use boosting algorithms to learn a set of attention weights that highlight the most important regions in an image. These attention weights are then used to modify the input to the FCN, effectively guiding the network's focus towards the most informative areas. We evaluate AttentionBoost on several benchmark datasets and demonstrate its superiority compared to traditional FCNs and state-of-the-art attention-based models. Our main contributions are: * The introduction of AttentionBoost, a simple yet powerful technique for improving the performance of FCNs using attention mechanisms. * An efficient algorithm for learning attention weights that can be seamlessly integrated into existing FCN architectures. * Experimental evidence demonstrating the effectiveness of AttentionBoost in enhancing the accuracy and efficiency of FCNs on various image classification benchmarks. Overall, our work presents a valuable tool for improving the performance of FCNs, and sheds new light on the role of attention mechanisms in image classification tasks.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for automatic quality evaluation of noisy-labeled image segmentation, called Pick-and-Learn. Noisy-labeled data, which contains incorrect or inconsistent labels, is a common problem in image segmentation tasks and can significantly degrade the performance of deep learning models. Existing methods for quality evaluation rely on manual annotation of a small subset of the data, which is time-consuming and expensive. Our proposed method, Pick-and-Learn, leverages a small set of manually annotated images to learn a quality evaluation metric that can be used to evaluate the quality of the entire dataset. The key idea is to iteratively select a subset of images from the dataset, annotate them manually, and use these annotations to learn a quality evaluation metric. The metric is then used to evaluate the quality of the remaining images in the dataset. We evaluate the effectiveness of Pick-and-Learn on several benchmark datasets and compare it to existing methods. Our results show that Pick-and-Learn achieves state-of-the-art performance in terms of accuracy and efficiency. We also demonstrate the versatility of our approach by applying it to different segmentation tasks, including object detection and instance segmentation. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach for automatic quality evaluation of noisy-labeled image segmentation, called Pick-and-Learn. * An efficient and effective method for learning a quality evaluation metric using a small set of manually annotated images. * State-of-the-art performance on several benchmark datasets, outperforming existing methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. The paper is organized as follows: In section 2, we review related work on quality evaluation for image segmentation. In section 3, we describe the proposed Pick-and-Learn method in detail. In section 4, we present the experimental results and comparison to existing methods. Finally, we conclude the paper in section 5 with a discussion of the results and future work. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach called Pick-and-Learn for automatic quality evaluation of noisy-labeled image segmentation. Existing methods rely heavily on manually annotated datasets, which are time-consuming and expensive to create, or use indirect metrics that may not correlates well with human perception. Our method addresses these limitations by learning to recognize high-quality segments from noisy labels through a simple, pick-and-choose style interaction. We introduce a deep neural network architecture that learns to predict the probability distribution over the possible segmentation masks given an image and its corresponding noisy label. During training, our model is trained to select the best segmentation mask from multiple choices, leveraging both accurate and erroneous annotations in the noisy dataset. At test time, the model can evaluate the quality of a given segmentation result based on the similarity between the predicted mask and the ground truth. Our experiments demonstrate that Pick-and-Learn outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on several benchmark datasets, achieving high accuracy while being robust to various types of noise in the labels. We also show that our method can be used to improve the performance of downstream tasks such as object detection and instance segmentation. This work has important implications for real-world applications where labeled data is scarce or difficult to obtain. By enabling the usage of large quantities of noisy data, Pick-and-Learn paves the way for more efficient and cost-effective development of image segmentation models.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning architecture called ET-Net, which stands for Edge-aTtention Guidance Network, for medical image segmentation. ET-Net is designed to address the problem of poor boundary detection in medical images, which is a critical task in various medical applications. Existing deep learning models for medical image segmentation rely heavily on fully convolutional networks (FCNs) or U-Net-like architectures, which often produce blurry or incomplete boundaries. This is because these models are designed to focus on the feature extraction process rather than the boundary detection process. ET-Net addresses this limitation by introducing an edge-aTtention mechanism that explicitly guides the network to learn the boundary information. The edge-aTtention mechanism is based on a novel attention module that learns to weight the importance of different image regions based on their relevance to the boundary. This allows the network to selectively focus on the boundary regions and produce more accurate segmentation results. ET-Net is a generic architecture that can be easily adapted to different medical image segmentation tasks, such as tumor segmentation, organ segmentation, and vessel segmentation. We evaluate ET-Net on several publicly available datasets and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our main contributions are: * A novel edge-aTtention mechanism that selectively focuses on boundary regions to improve boundary detection in medical image segmentation. * A generic Edge-aTtention Guidance Network (ET-Net) that can be adapted to different medical image segmentation tasks. * Extensive experiments demonstrating the superior performance of ET-Net compared to state-of-the-art methods. Overall, ET-Net represents a significant improvement in medical image segmentation, and we believe it has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of various medical applications.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning architecture called ET-Net, which stands for Edge-aTtention Guidance Network, for medical image segmentation tasks. The proposed network leverages both edge information and attention mechanisms to improve the accuracy and efficiency of the segmentation process. Existing segmentation methods often rely on heavily designed hand-crafted features or multi-scale feature pyramids, which can be suboptimal and computationally expensive. In contrast, our ET-Net utilizes a simple and effective approach that combines edge detection and attention mechanisms to selectively focus on the most relevant regions of the input images. This allows the network to learn richer representations at multiple scales and produce accurate segmentations with less computational overhead. The key innovation of ET-Net lies in its ability to incorporate edge information into the attention mechanism. Specifically, we introduce an edge transformation module that converts the input image into a set of edge maps, each capturing different levels of edges. These edge maps are then used to compute attention weights, which are combined with the original image features to form a new set of hybrid features. This process not only enhances the representation capacity of the network but also reduces the risk of overfitting due to the regularizing effect of edge information. Extensive experiments conducted on several publicly available datasets demonstrate the superiority of our ET-Net compared to state-of-the-art segmentation models. In particular, we achieve better performance than existing methods using fewer parameters and computations, highlighting the efficiency and scalability of our approach. Additionally, we provide thorough ablation studies and visualizations to analyze the contributions of various components in ET-Net, offering insights into the working mechanisms of the model. In summary, our paper introduces ET-Net, a generic edge-attention guidance network for medical image segmentation, which effectively integrates edge information and attention mechanisms to improve accuracy and efficiency. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications in computer vision and machine learning, especially in scenarios where object boundaries and edges play crucial roles.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to action/object segmentation in video using 3D convolutional neural networks (CNNs). The key contribution of our work is the development of an efficient 3D CNN architecture that effectively captures both spatial and temporal information in videos. Our network design combines convolutional and recurrent layers to encode both local and global context, enabling accurate segmentation of actions and objects in complex scenes. We also introduce a new technique for Temporal Spatial Pyramid Pooling (TSPP) that enables our network to focus on different time scales, further improving performance. This makes our approach particularly suitable for real-world applications where computation resources are limited. In summary, our paper presents a novel 3D CNN architecture for efficient action/object segmentation in video, which advances the state-of-the-art in terms of performance, efficiency, and robustness. text: This paper presents a novel approach for stereo-based terrain traversability analysis, which combines normal-based segmentation and superpixel surface analysis. The proposed method is designed to effectively evaluate the traversability of rough terrain, which is a crucial task in various applications such as robotics, autonomous vehicles, and environmental monitoring. The proposed method consists of two main stages. The first stage involves normal-based segmentation, where the terrain is segmented into small patches based on the normal vectors of the surface. This stage aims to capture the local surface orientation and texture information, which are essential for traversability analysis. The second stage involves superpixel surface analysis, where the segmented patches are further analyzed to extract meaningful features that describe the surface properties. The superpixel surface analysis stage involves the calculation of various features such as surface roughness, slope, and orientation. These features are then used to train a machine learning model that predicts the traversability of the terrain. The model is trained using a large dataset of labeled terrain samples, and it is able to accurately predict the traversability of unknown terrain. The proposed method is evaluated using a variety of experiments, including simulations and real-world experiments using a robotic platform. The results show that the method is effective in predicting the traversability of rough terrain, and it outperforms traditional methods that rely solely on slope angle or texture information. The method is also robust to various environmental conditions such as lighting and shadows. The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel approach for stereo-based terrain traversability analysis that combines normal-based segmentation and superpixel surface analysis. The proposed method provides a more accurate and robust way of evaluating the traversability of rough terrain, which is essential for various applications. The paper also provides a detailed evaluation of the method's performance and limitations, and it highlights future research directions in this area.

title: Stereo-based terrain traversability analysis using normal-based segmentation and superpixel surface analysis

text: This paper presents a novel approach to stereo-based terrain traversability analysis, which combines normal-based segmentation and superpixel surface analysis to assess the traversability of rough terrain. The proposed method utilizes stereo vision to estimate the 3D structure of the environment and partition the terrain into smaller regions based on their normals. Each region is then analyzed using superpixels to extract meaningful features that describe its surface characteristics. A set of traversability metrics are computed from these features, including slope angle, orientation, and surface roughness. These metrics are then combined to generate a traverseability map that highlights areas of the terrain that can be safely navigated by a robot or vehicle. The effectiveness of the proposed method is demonstrated through experiments conducted in both simulated and real-world environments. Results show that the approach is able to accurately identify traversable and non-traversable regions, even in the presence of complex geometry and deformable surfaces. Furthermore, the method's ability to handle varying illumination conditions and sensor noise makes it suitable for deployment in real-world scenarios. Overall, this research contributes a robust and efficient technique for stereo-based terrain traversability analysis that has applications in various domains such as autonomous driving, robotics, and mapping.

text: Image segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision that involves dividing an image into its constituent parts or objects. Deep learning techniques have revolutionized the field of image segmentation by providing accurate and efficient solutions. In this paper, we provide an overview of deep learning techniques for image segmentation, focusing on the most popular architectures and their applications. We begin by discussing the basics of deep learning and its relevance to image segmentation. We then explore the different deep learning architectures that have been used for image segmentation, including fully convolutional networks (FCNs), U-Net, and SegNet. We also discuss the concept of transfer learning and its role in improving the performance of deep learning models for image segmentation. Next, we delve into the details of the most popular deep learning techniques for image segmentation, including semantic segmentation, instance segmentation, and object detection. We discuss the differences between these techniques and their applications in various fields. We also provide a comprehensive analysis of the strengths and limitations of each technique. In addition, we discuss some of the challenges associated with deep learning for image segmentation, such as the need for large amounts of labeled data, the problem of overfitting, and the difficulty in interpreting the learned features. We also discuss some of the solutions to these challenges, such as data augmentation, regularization techniques, and attention mechanisms. Finally, we conclude the paper by discussing some of the future directions for deep learning research in image segmentation, including the use of multi-modal data, the integration of domain knowledge, and the development of explainable AI models. We also provide some suggestions for further research in this field. Overall, this paper provides a comprehensive overview of deep learning techniques for image segmentation, and is intended to serve as a resource for researchers and practitioners who are interested in applying deep learning techniques to image segmentation tasks.

Finally, we highlight future research directions and potential applications of deep learning techniques in image segmentation, including medical imaging, autonomous driving, and robotics. This paper is intended for both beginners and experts in the field of deep learning, providing a comprehensive resource for those looking to gain a deeper understanding of image segmentation techniques using deep learning.

text: In this paper, we propose a general framework for image segmentation based on complex networks. Our approach leverages the rich topological structure of complex networks to segment images into regions of similar characteristics. We first represent an image as a complex network, where pixels are nodes and edges connect pixels with similar features. We then apply community detection algorithms to identify clusters of densely connected nodes, which correspond to regions of similar characteristics in the image. We propose a novel algorithm that combines community detection and boundary detection techniques to optimize the segmentation process. Our framework does not require any prior knowledge about the number of regions or the characteristics of the image, making it a fully unsupervised approach. We evaluate our framework on several benchmark images and compare the results with state-of-the-art segmentation methods. The results show that our approach outperforms traditional image segmentation methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. We also demonstrate the versatility of our framework by applying it to various types of images, including natural images, medical images, and textures. Our work makes several contributions to the field of image segmentation. First, we introduce a novel framework that leverages complex networks to segment images. Second, we propose a new algorithm that combines community detection and boundary detection techniques to optimize the segmentation process. Finally, we demonstrate the effectiveness and versatility of our framework through extensive experiments. Overall, our paper provides a new perspective on image segmentation and offers a powerful tool for segmenting images into regions of similar characteristics. The proposed framework has important applications in various fields, including computer vision, image processing, and machine learning.

text: In this paper, we present a general framework for complex network-based image segmentation, which leverages the mathematical notion of complex networks to model the structure and relationships between pixels in an image. Our approach enables the integration of diverse image features and prior knowledge into a unified framework, leading to improved segmentation accuracy and robustness. We first represent an image as a graph, where pixels are nodes and their similarities are edges. We then apply community detection algorithms to identify clusters of highly connected pixels, which correspond to regions of homogeneous texture or color. These clusters serve as the basis for our segmentation framework, which combines them with other features such as edge information, smoothness priors, and boundary detection. Our proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark images with varied characteristics, including textured scenes, natural images, and medical imaging data. The results demonstrate that our framework outperforms traditional image segmentation methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically, it achieves higher segmentation quality while requiring fewer computational resources, making it suitable for real-world applications where speed and accuracy are crucial. The main contributions of this work can be summarized as follows: (1) we introduce a novel image segmentation framework based on complex networks, which effectively captures the structural information and relationships among pixels; (2) we develop an efficient algorithm that integrates various image features and priors into the network-based framework, ensuring accurate and robust segmentation performance; and (3) we conduct extensive experiments demonstrating the superiority of our approach over state-of-the-art methods. This paper provides a significant step forward in image segmentation research by introducing a powerful framework that harnesses the power of complex networks. It has important implications for a wide range of applications, including object recognition, tracking, and scene understanding, and paves the way for further advancements in computer vision and machine learning.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach for training image dehazing models that can be used for segmentation tasks. Existing methods for image dehazing typically focus on improving the visual quality of the image, but they often neglect the impact of haze on downstream tasks such as segmentation. In this work, we explore the use of dehazing models as a preprocessing step for segmentation tasks, and demonstrate that dehazing can significantly improve segmentation accuracy. We propose a new training method for dehazing models that incorporates segmentation information into the dehazing process. Our approach uses a combination of image-to-image translation and segmentation loss functions to train a deep neural network to remove haze while preserving the underlying segmentation information. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art dehazing methods in terms of both visual quality and segmentation accuracy. Our main contributions are: * A novel approach for training image dehazing models that incorporates segmentation information into the dehazing process. * A new training method that uses a combination of image-to-image translation and segmentation loss functions to train a deep neural network for dehazing. * An evaluation of our method on several benchmark datasets, demonstrating its effectiveness in improving segmentation accuracy. Overall, this paper demonstrates the importance of considering downstream tasks when designing image dehazing models, and provides a new approach for training dehazing models that can be used for segmentation tasks.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to image segmentation that leverages the power of deep learning-based image dehazing models. Existing methods for image segmentation often rely on handcrafted features or shallow neural networks, which can be limited in their ability to handle complex and diverse real-world images. In contrast, recent advances in image dehazing research have led to the development of sophisticated deep neural networks capable of removing haze from images while preserving important details. In this work, we investigate whether these powerful image dehazing models can be repurposed for segmentation tasks. We propose a simple yet effective method for adapting existing image dehazing models to perform image segmentation, without requiring significant modifications to the original network architecture. Our approach involves fine-tuning the pre-trained dehazing model on a small set of labeled images for the target segmentation task, followed by fusion with a coarse segmentation mask generated using traditional computer vision techniques. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO. The results show that our adaptation approach outperforms state-of-the-art segmentation methods, achieving an average IoU increase of 5% across all datasets. Furthermore, we demonstrate the superiority of our method in handling various challenges such as semantic ambiguity, object occlusion, and cluttered backgrounds. Our study highlights the versatility and transferability of learned representations in deep neural networks, and opens up new possibilities for addressing long-standing problems in computer vision. The proposed method has far-reaching implications for applications such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging, where accurate segmentation is critical.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach for image segmentation using fully convolutional networks (FCNs), with a focus on retinal images. The proposed method, called Patch-FCN, leverages the strengths of FCNs in processing data with variable-sized filters and applies them to image segmentation tasks. Existing FCN-based segmentation methods typically require a fixed-size input image, which can be a limitation when dealing with large images or images with varying sizes. Patch-FCN addresses this limitation by introducing a patch-based approach, where the input image is divided into smaller patches, and each patch is processed independently using an FCN. This allows for efficient processing of large images and enables the use of FCNs for image segmentation tasks. The proposed method was evaluated on a dataset of retinal images and showed superior performance compared to traditional image segmentation methods. The patch-based approach allowed for faster processing times and improved accuracy, demonstrating the effectiveness of Patch-FCN for retinal image segmentation. The main contributions of this paper are: 1. A novel approach for image segmentation using fully convolutional networks, which leverages the strengths of FCNs in processing data with variable-sized filters. 2. A patch-based approach for image segmentation, which enables the use of FCNs for large images and improves processing times and accuracy. 3. An application of Patch-FCN to retinal image segmentation, demonstrating its effectiveness in this domain. The proposed method has important implications for medical image analysis, particularly in the field of ophthalmology, where accurate segmentation of retinal images is crucial for diagnosis and treatment of eye diseases. The patch-based approach allows for faster and more accurate segmentation, which can be used to support clinical decision-making and improve patient outcomes.

text: Abstract: Skin lesion segmentation is a crucial step in the diagnosis and treatment of skin diseases. While automated segmentation methods have shown promising results, they are not immune to errors, particularly when dealing with varied annotator interpretations. In this paper, we address the issue of inter-annotator agreement in automated skin lesion segmentation. We propose a novel approach that incorporates a consensus-based algorithm, leveraging multiple annotators' inputs to improve segmentation accuracy. Our method is designed to handle diverse annotation styles and inconsistencies, ensuring that the final segmentation mask accurately reflects the collective understanding of the annotators. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of 100 images, each annotated by four expert dermatologists. Our results show a significant improvement in segmentation accuracy, with a 23% reduction in mean absolute error compared to the baseline method. Additionally, we perform a user study to assess the efficacy of our approach in real-world scenarios. The results indicate that our method is effective in reducing annotation time and improving segmentation accuracy, with an average time savings of 17% and a 19% improvement in accuracy. Our work has important implications for the development of automated skin lesion segmentation systems, demonstrating the value of incorporating inter-annotator agreement in the segmentation process. The proposed method has the potential to increase the accuracy and efficiency of skin lesion segmentation, ultimately improving the diagnosis and treatment of skin diseases.

text: In the field of medical image analysis, inter-annotator agreement is a crucial aspect in ensuring the accuracy and reliability of automated skin lesion segmentation systems. This paper proposes a novel approach to handling inter-annotator agreement for automated skin lesion segmentation, which combines human annotations with deep learning techniques. Our proposed method utilizes a dataset of manually annotated images of skin lesions, where multiple annotators have labeled each image with bounding boxes around the lesion areas. We employ a combination of overlap measures and uncertainty-based methods to quantify the level of agreement among annotators and identify instances of disagreement. By leveraging these metrics, we introduce an algorithm that selects the most reliable annotator for each instance and generates a corrected annotation mask that better reflects the true extent of the lesion. The performance of our approach was evaluated using a test set of images, demonstrating significant improvement in segmentation accuracy compared to using a single annotator's labels or a simple majority voting strategy. Additionally, we investigate the impact of different factors on inter-annotator agreement, such as lesion size, complexity, and location, providing valuable insights into the limitations and strengths of current annotation practices. This work has important implications for improving the quality and consistency of skin lesion segmentation, which can boost the accuracy of downstream tasks like disease diagnosis and monitoring. Our findings also highlight the potential benefits of incorporating multiple annotator perspectives into machine learning models, particularly in situations where ground truth data are limited or subjective.

text: Remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation is a crucial task in various applications such as land cover classification, crop type mapping, and object detection. Deep learning techniques have shown promising results in this field, but they are prone to suffer from boundary loss, which occurs when the model fails to accurately predict the class labels near the boundaries of objects or regions. In this paper, we investigate the problem of boundary loss in remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation and propose a novel approach to address it. Our approach is based on the idea of incorporating boundary information into the segmentation model. We introduce a new loss function that takes into account the boundary information and encourages the model to produce smooth and accurate predictions near the boundaries. The proposed loss function is combined with a deep neural network architecture that is specifically designed for remote sensing imagery segmentation. We evaluate our approach on several publicly available remote sensing datasets and compare it with state-of-the-art methods. The results show that our approach outperforms the existing methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, especially near the boundaries. We also perform a series of experiments to analyze the effectiveness of the proposed loss function and demonstrate its superiority over traditional loss functions. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * We investigate the problem of boundary loss in remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation and propose a novel approach to address it. * We introduce a new loss function that incorporates boundary information and encourages the model to produce smooth and accurate predictions near the boundaries. * We evaluate our approach on several publicly available remote sensing datasets and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods. * We perform a series of experiments to analyze the effectiveness of the proposed loss function and demonstrate its superiority over traditional loss functions. The proposed approach has important implications for remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation, and it can be applied to various applications such as land cover classification, crop type mapping, and object detection.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation, called boundary loss. The proposed method is designed to improve the accuracy of semantic segmentation models by leveraging the information from object boundaries in remote sensing images. Existing methods for remote sensing imagery semantic segmentation mainly focus on using fully convolutional networks (FCNs) or encoder-decoder architectures, which often produce blurry or incomplete segmentation results due to the lack of explicit boundary information. In contrast, this work introduces a new boundary loss function that incorporates the spatial and spectral information of object boundaries into the training process. The proposed boundary loss function consists of two components: a boundary detection term and a boundary consistency term. The boundary detection term encourages the model to predict accurate boundaries between different objects or land cover classes while considering both local and global contextual information. Meanwhile, the boundary consistency term enforces the model to generate consistent boundary predictions across multiple scales by aggregating multi-scale features with a specially designed hierarchical fusion module. The effectiveness of the proposed boundary loss was evaluated using several benchmark datasets including PASCAL VOC dataset, Cityscapes dataset, and a large-scale high-resolution remote sensing image dataset. Compared to state-of-the art baselines, our method achieved significant improvements in terms of overall segmentation accuracy as well as boundary quality measured by various evaluation metrics such as intersection over union (IoU), pixel accuracy (PA), and boundary F1-score (BF1). Moreover, visualization analysis demonstrated that our boundary loss could effectively preserve sharp object boundaries, especially in cases where there are complex shape variations or abrupt changes in texture. We also investigated several variants of the proposed boundary loss and presented an in-depth discussion about their effects on performance. text: Abstract: Machine learning has revolutionized numerous fields, including computer vision, natural language processing, and predictive analytics. However, a critical examination of the field reveals a lack of diversity in various aspects, including the data used for training, the algorithms employed, and the individuals involved in the development process. This homogeneity can result in biases, discrimination, and limited representation of marginalized groups, which can have severe consequences in areas such as healthcare, finance, and criminal justice. This paper explores the concept of diversity in machine learning and its importance in ensuring fairness, equity, and inclusivity. We discuss the various dimensions of diversity, including demographic, cognitive, and cultural diversity, and their impact on the development and deployment of machine learning models. We also examine the challenges and barriers that prevent diverse groups from participating in the machine learning ecosystem and propose strategies to address these issues. Furthermore, we discuss the role of diversity in mitigating bias and ensuring ethical considerations in machine learning. We explore techniques such as data augmentation, debiasing, and inclusive data collection, which can help reduce bias and improve the representativeness of machine learning models. We also discuss the importance of transparency and accountability in ensuring that machine learning models are fair and unbiased. Finally, we highlight the need for a multidisciplinary approach to address the issue of diversity in machine learning. We argue that diversity should be integrated into the development process, and that individuals from diverse backgrounds should be involved in every stage of the machine learning lifecycle. We also emphasize the importance of creating inclusive environments that foster diversity and encourage diverse perspectives. In conclusion, this paper emphasizes the urgent need for diversity in machine learning to ensure fairness, equity, and inclusivity. We propose strategies to address the challenges and barriers that prevent diverse groups from participating in the machine learning ecosystem and highlight the importance of a multidisciplinary approach to address this issue. By promoting diversity in machine learning, we can create models that are more representative, fair, and beneficial to society as a whole.

title: Diversity in Machine Learning

text: Abstract: Machine learning has become a cornerstone of modern technology, with applications in a wide range of fields, including computer vision, natural language processing, and predictive modeling. However, despite its widespread adoption, machine learning remains plagued by a lack of diversity, both in terms of the data used to train models and the individuals who create and interpret them. This homogeneity can lead to biased models that perpetuate existing social inequalities and limit the potential impact of machine learning on diverse populations. In this paper, we explore the concept of diversity in machine learning and its importance for creating more inclusive and equitable AI systems. We first examine the various sources of bias in machine learning, including data bias, algorithmic bias, and demographic bias, and discuss their effects on model performance and fairness. We then propose strategies for improving diversity in machine learning, such as data curation techniques, debiasing methods, and diversification of the workforce. Finally, we demonstrate the benefits of diversity in machine learning through case studies on facial recognition, sentiment analysis, and customer service chatbots. Our results show that increasing diversity in machine learning leads to improved model performance, reduced bias, and increased applicability across diverse populations. Our findings have important implications for the development and deployment of machine learning systems, highlighting the need for greater investment in diversity-focused research and practice. By promoting diversity in machine learning, we can create AI systems that are not only more effective but also more ethical and responsible, ultimately leading to better outcomes for society as a whole.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for semantic segmentation of video sequences using Convolutional LSTMs (ConvLSTMs). Semantic segmentation is the task of assigning a class label to each pixel in an image, indicating the object or material it belongs to. In the context of video sequences, this task is particularly challenging due to the temporal dynamics of the visual content and the need to capture long-term dependencies between frames. ConvLSTMs are a type of deep neural network that combine the strengths of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks. They are well-suited for processing sequential data, such as video, by learning both short-term and long-term dependencies between frames. Our proposed approach leverages ConvLSTMs to learn a representation of the video sequence that captures both spatial and temporal information. We introduce a novel architecture that combines convolutional and recurrent layers to effectively segment objects in each frame while also modeling their temporal evolution. We evaluate our approach on several challenging datasets, including the Cityscapes and CamVid datasets, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our experiments show that ConvLSTMs outperform traditional CNNs and LSTMs in terms of segmentation accuracy, while also being more efficient in terms of computational resources. We also perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the contributions of different components of our approach, providing insights into the effectiveness of ConvLSTMs for semantic segmentation of video sequences. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including autonomous driving, robotics, and video surveillance, where the ability to accurately segment objects in real-time is crucial. We believe that our approach represents a significant step forward in the field of video understanding and paves the way for further research in this area.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to cardiac cine magnetic resonance (MR) image segmentation using a distance map regularized convolutional neural network (CNN). Accurate segmentation of cardiac structures from cine MR images is a crucial step in various cardiovascular applications, but it remains a challenging task due to the complexity of the heart's anatomy and the variability of image quality. Existing CNN-based methods for cardiac cine MR image segmentation have limitations in handling the large variations in cardiac structures and motion patterns, leading to suboptimal performance. To address these limitations, we propose a distance map regularized CNN that incorporates a distance map loss function into the network's optimization process. The distance map loss encourages the network to produce a smooth and accurate distance map, which in turn improves the segmentation accuracy. Our proposed method was evaluated on a dataset of 160 cardiac cine MR images and compared with state-of-the-art segmentation methods. The results show that our method outperforms the compared methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, robustness to variations in cardiac motion, and computational efficiency. Specifically, our method achieved an average Dice similarity coefficient of 0.86 ± 0.06, which is a 10% improvement over the best compared method. The main contributions of this paper are: 1. A novel distance map regularized CNN for cardiac cine MR image segmentation, which incorporates a distance map loss function into the network's optimization process to improve segmentation accuracy and robustness. 2. An extensive evaluation of the proposed method on a large dataset of cardiac cine MR images, demonstrating its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. 3. A detailed analysis of the effectiveness of the distance map regularization term, providing insights into the role of distance map information in improving segmentation accuracy. This paper has important implications for the development of accurate and robust cardiac cine MR image segmentation methods, which can be used for various cardiovascular applications such as disease diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring.

This term encourages the CNN to learn a smooth and continuous representation of the LV boundary, leading to improved segmentation performance. Additionally, we perform thorough ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the distance map regularization term and provide insights into its contribution to the overall performance. In conclusion, this paper presents a distance map regularized CNN for cardiac cine MR image segmentation, which offers improved accuracy and efficiency over existing methods. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic segmentation in pathology using an adaptive weighting multi-field-of-view convolutional neural network (CNN). Current state-of-the-art methods for semantic segmentation in pathology are limited by their inability to effectively incorporate multi-scale contextual information and handle varying field-of-view (FOV) sizes. Our proposed method addresses these limitations by introducing an adaptive weighting mechanism that learns to assign appropriate weights to different FOVs based on their relevance to the current image. The proposed method consists of three main components: 1) a multi-FOV CNN that extracts features from different FOVs, 2) an adaptive weighting module that learns to weight the importance of each FOV, and 3) a fusion module that combines the weighted features to produce the final segmentation mask. We evaluate our method on a large dataset of pathology images and demonstrate its superiority compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our key contributions include: * The introduction of an adaptive weighting mechanism to learn the importance of different FOVs for semantic segmentation in pathology. * The development of a multi-FOV CNN that effectively incorporates contextual information from different scales. * The proposal of a novel fusion module that combines weighted features from different FOVs to produce accurate segmentation masks. Our experimental results show that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of segmentation accuracy and efficiency. We also perform a series of ablation studies to demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed adaptive weighting mechanism and the contribution of each component to the overall performance. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for efficient and accurate compression of 3D medical images using machine vision guidance for efficient transmission and segmentation in the clouds. Our proposed method leverages the latest advancements in machine learning and computer vision to significantly reduce the size of 3D medical images while preserving their diagnostic accuracy. The proposed method consists of three stages: (1) image preprocessing, (2) machine vision-guided compression, and (3) accurate segmentation. In the image preprocessing stage, we apply a combination of image filtering and normalization techniques to enhance the quality of the input images. In the machine vision-guided compression stage, we utilize a deep learning-based model to identify and compress the most relevant features of the images, resulting in a significant reduction in data size. Finally, in the accurate segmentation stage, we employ a hybrid segmentation approach that combines machine learning and traditional image processing techniques to accurately separate the different structures within the compressed images. We evaluate the performance of our proposed method using a dataset of 3D medical images and compare it with state-of-the-art compression and segmentation methods. Our results show that our method achieves a higher level of compression while maintaining diagnostic accuracy, and outperforms traditional methods in terms of computational efficiency and accuracy. The main contributions of this paper can be summarized as follows: * A novel approach for machine vision-guided 3D medical image compression that leverages the latest advancements in machine learning and computer vision. * A hybrid segmentation approach that combines machine learning and traditional image processing techniques for accurate segmentation of compressed images. * An extensive evaluation of the proposed method using a dataset of 3D medical images, demonstrating its superior performance in terms of compression ratio, computational efficiency, and diagnostic accuracy. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to machine vision-guided 3D medical image compression for efficient transmission and accurate segmentation in the clouds. utilizing cutting-edge techniques in machine learning and computer vision, our proposed method enables the reduction of data transfer rates and enhances the accuracy of segmentation in medical imaging applications. Our proposed method addresses these limitations by exploiting the capabilities of cloud computing and recent advancements in convolutional neural networks (CNN). Specifically, we develop a two-stage framework that first employs a lightweight CNN model to rapidly preview and compress the input data followed by a deeper CNN model refinement stage to accurately segment the compressed image volumes. Our approach enables real-time performance while maintaining high-quality segmentation standards, as demonstrated through extensive evaluation using various publicly available datasets. text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic image segmentation called Auto-DeepLab, which leverages hierarchical neural architecture search (HNAS) to optimize the design of deep neural networks (DNNs) for this task. Unlike traditional methods that rely on hand-crafted architectures or brute-force search, Auto-DeepLab uses a hierarchical search strategy to efficiently explore the space of possible architectures and identify the most effective ones for semantic image segmentation. Our approach consists of three main components: (1) a hierarchical search algorithm that iteratively refines the architecture of a DNN, (2) a set of evaluation metrics that guide the search process, and (3) a robust optimization method that enables efficient training of the identified architectures. We evaluate Auto-DeepLab on several benchmark datasets, including Cityscapes, PASCAL VOC, and COCO, and compare its performance to state-of-the-art methods. The results show that Auto-DeepLab significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving an average IoU of 81.7% on Cityscapes and 85.1% on PASCAL VOC. Moreover, we perform ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of the hierarchical search strategy and demonstrate that it is crucial for achieving better performance. Our work has important implications for the field of computer vision and deep learning. First, it demonstrates the potential of HNAS for solving complex tasks like semantic image segmentation. Second, it provides a new way to design high-performance DNNs that do not rely on manual design or brute-force search. Finally, it highlights the importance of considering the hierarchical structure of images when designing DNNs for semantic segmentation. In summary, Auto-DeepLab represents a significant step forward in the field of semantic image segmentation, and we believe that its hierarchical search strategy and evaluation metrics can be applied to other computer vision tasks, leading to further advances in the field.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to semantic image segmentation called Auto-DeepLab, which leverages hierarchical neural architecture search (HNAS) to optimize both the encoder and decoder architectures of a deep learning model. Unlike traditional methods that rely on hand-designed architectures or brute force searches, HNAS uses reinforcement learning to efficiently explore the space of possible architectures and identify optimal designs that balance accuracy and computational cost. Our proposed method is evaluated on several benchmark datasets, including PASCAL VOC and Cityscapes, and achieves state-of-the-art performance while significantly reducing the number of parameters and computations required compared to existing models. The key contribution of this work is the development of an iterative HNAS algorithm that jointly optimizes the encoder and decoder architectures of a semantic image segmentation model. The encoder architecture is represented as a set of nested blocks that encode features at multiple scales, while the decoder architecture is parameterized by a set of upsampling and convolutional layers that refine the output resolution. We introduce a new search space that allows us to efficiently explore a large space of possible architectures and define a reward function that balances accuracy and efficiency. Our experimental results show that Auto-DeepLab outperforms manually designed models and other state-of-the-art NAS methods, achieve an mIoU of 81.9% on PASCAL VOC and 76.3% on Cityscapes, while having fewer parameters and requiring less computation than existing models. We also perform ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of different components of our method and demonstrate its generalizability to other tasks such as object detection and instance segmentation. This work demonstrates the potential of using HNAS for semantic image segmentation and contributes to the growing interest in using AI to automate the design of machine learning models. The proposed Auto-DeepLab model provides a more efficient and effective way to perform semantic image segmentation, which can be applied to various computer vision applications such as autonomous driving, robotics, and medical imaging analysis.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for generating realistic vehicle images that seamlessly blend with their surroundings. Our method leverages advanced computer vision and machine learning techniques to accurately capture the context of the vehicle's environment, including the road, other vehicles, pedestrians, and buildings. We then use this information to generate a photorealistic image of the vehicle that appears naturally integrated with its surroundings. Our approach consists of three main stages: surroundings analysis, vehicle segmentation, and image synthesis. In the first stage, we use a deep neural network to analyze the surroundings of the vehicle and extract relevant features such as road texture, lighting, and object detection. In the second stage, we segment the vehicle from the rest of the image using a instance segmentation algorithm, allowing us to focus on the vehicle and its immediate surroundings. Finally, in the third stage, we use a generative adversarial network (GAN) to synthesize a realistic image of the vehicle that takes into account the extracted surroundings features and the vehicle's segmentation mask. We evaluate our method on a large dataset of real-world images and demonstrate its effectiveness in generating high-quality vehicle images that accurately reflect the surrounding environment. Our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of image quality and computational efficiency, making it a promising solution for a wide range of applications such as virtual try-on, vehicle rendering, and autonomous driving. Our main contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach for generating realistic vehicle images that seamlessly blend with their surroundings. * We introduce a three-stage pipeline that combines surroundings analysis, vehicle segmentation, and image synthesis to generate high-quality vehicle images. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on a large dataset of real-world images and show its superiority over state-of-the-art methods. Overall, this paper provides a significant step forward in the field of vehicle image generation, and we believe that our approach has the potential to significantly improve the quality and efficiency of various applications that require realistic vehicle images.

text: This paper proposes a novel application of the cumulative sum (CUSUM) filter for brain segmentation on diffusion-weighted magnetic resonance (DSC) perfusion head scans with abnormal brain anatomy. The CUSUM filter is a statistical method that detects small shifts in the mean of a signal, making it suitable for identifying subtle changes in brain tissue perfusion. In this study, we adapt the CUSUM filter for brain segmentation, where it is used to identify regions of abnormal perfusion in DSC perfusion MR head scans. We evaluate the performance of the CUSUM filter on a dataset of DSC perfusion MR head scans with abnormal brain anatomy, including tumors, cerebral vasospasm, and stroke. Our results show that the CUSUM filter is effective in identifying regions of abnormal perfusion, with high sensitivity and specificity compared to manual segmentation. We also compare the performance of the CUSUM filter to traditional machine learning approaches, demonstrating its superiority in terms of accuracy and computational efficiency. The CUSUM filter offers several advantages for brain segmentation on DSC perfusion MR head scans. First, it does not require a priori knowledge of the normal perfusion pattern, making it suitable for cases with abnormal brain anatomy. Second, it is robust to noise and artifacts, which are common in DSC perfusion MR scans. Third, it is computationally efficient, allowing for real-time segmentation. Our findings suggest that the CUSUM filter is a valuable tool for brain segmentation on DSC perfusion MR head scans with abnormal brain anatomy. Its accuracy, robustness, and efficiency make it a promising approach for clinical applications, such as tumor diagnosis and treatment monitoring. Future studies can explore the integration of the CUSUM filter with other machine learning techniques to further improve segmentation accuracy and robustness.

We evaluate our method using a dataset of 10 DSC-MR head scans with abnormal brain anatomy, and compare the results to those obtained using traditional machine learning approaches. The results show that the CUSUM filter outperforms traditional methods in terms of both accuracy and computational efficiency. Specifically, it achieves higher accuracy in segmenting brains with abnormal anatomy, while also being computationally faster than other methods. Additionally, the CUSUM filter provides more consistent performance across different subjects and scan sessions, indicating its potential for robust clinical application. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for interactive segmentation of medical images using fully convolutional neural networks (FCNs). The user interacts with the system by providing rough annotations of the object boundaries, which are then used to fine-tune the FCN's predictions. text: Left atrium segmentation is a crucial step in the diagnosis and treatment of various cardiac conditions. However, LA segmentation remains a challenging task due to the complexity of the heart's anatomy and the variability of the LA's shape and appearance across individuals. In this paper, we propose a novel approach to LA segmentation that combines global shape priors and autoencoding local intensity priors using a mixture modeling framework. Our approach leverages the strengths of both global and local prior knowledge to improve the accuracy and robustness of LA segmentation. We first establish a global shape prior using a statistical shape model, which captures the average shape and variability of the LA across a population. We then combine the global shape prior with a local intensity prior, which is learned from a set of training images using an autoencoder network. The autoencoder network is trained to reconstruct the input images, and the learned local intensity prior is used to capture the spatial patterns and variability of the LA's intensity values. We propose a mixture modeling framework that combines the global shape prior and local intensity prior to segment the LA. The mixture modeling framework allows us to integrate the global and local priors in a probabilistic manner, enabling the model to adapt to the complexity and variability of the LA's shape and appearance. We evaluate our approach on a dataset of cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) images and compare it with state-of-the-art methods. Global shape priors have been widely used in medical image segmentation tasks, as they can effectively capture the overall shape and structure of organs. To address these limitations, we propose a mixture model that combines global shape priors and local intensity priors through an autoencoder-based framework. The proposed approach uses a variational inference scheme to learn a shared representation between the two types of priors, which enables the sharing of information across them. This allows our method to leverage the complementary information present in both types of priors to improve segmentation performance. We evaluate our method on a dataset of CMR images and demonstrate its superiority over state-of-the-art methods that rely solely on either global shape priors or local intensity priors. We also conduct experiments to analyze the effectiveness of each prior and show that our method provides better segmentation results by utilizing both priors. Additionally, we perform a series of ablation studies to validate the design choices and provide insights into the functioning of our proposed approach. Our work contributes to the development of robust and accurate left atrium segmentation algorithms, which are essential for various clinical applications such as atrial fibrillation diagnosis and treatment planning. The proposed method has the potential to be applied to other medical image segmentation tasks where multiple types of priors are available or can be derived.

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for dense 3D visual mapping, which we term "semantic simplification." This method leverages the power of semantic segmentation to reduce the complexity of 3D point clouds, while preserving the essential information required for accurate mapping. Existing methods for dense 3D visual mapping often suffer from the "curse of dimensionality," where the complexity of the data grows exponentially with the number of dimensions. This makes it difficult to efficiently process and analyze large 3D datasets. Semantic simplification addresses this challenge by separating the point cloud into semantically meaningful regions, allowing for a significant reduction in the number of points while preserving the essential features of the environment. Our approach is based on a deep neural network that learns to segment 3D point clouds into meaningful regions, such as walls, floors, and objects. This segmentation enables the creation of a compact representation of the environment, which can be used for efficient mapping, object recognition, and scene understanding. We evaluate our method on several large-scale 3D datasets and demonstrate its effectiveness in reducing the complexity of the data while preserving the accuracy of the map. Our experiments show that semantic simplification can reduce the number of points in a point cloud by up to 90% while maintaining the same level of accuracy as the original data. The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a novel approach to dense 3D visual mapping that leverages semantic segmentation to reduce the complexity of 3D point clouds. Our method has numerous applications in various fields, including robotics, autonomous vehicles, and virtual reality. In summary, semantic simplification is a powerful tool for dense 3D visual mapping that enables the efficient processing and analysis of large 3D datasets. Our approach has the potential to significantly improve the performance of various applications that rely on 3D data, and we believe it will play a crucial role in the development of future 3D data processing technologies.

text: This paper proposes a novel approach to brain MRI segmentation using a rule-based hybrid method that combines the strengths of traditional machine learning techniques with the interpretability of rule-based systems. text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for neuron segmentation in electron microscopy images using learning metric graphs. Electron microscopy imaging is a powerful tool for visualizing the morphology of neurons, but segmenting neurons from the background and identifying their boundaries can be a challenging task. Existing methods for neuron segmentation often rely on hand-crafted features and manual annotation, which can be time-consuming and limited in accuracy. In contrast, our approach uses a learning metric graph to model the similarity between pixels in the image and learn a representation that captures the structural information of the neurons. The learning metric graph is a flexible and efficient way to incorporate prior knowledge about the structure of neurons and the image acquisition process. We show that our method outperforms traditional segmentation methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency, and provides a more robust and reliable way to segment neurons in electron microscopy images. Our approach consists of two main components: a graph construction step, where we build a graph that encodes the similarity between pixels in the image, and a graph-based segmentation step, where we use the graph to segment the neurons. We evaluate our method on a dataset of electron microscopy images and show that it produces high-quality segmentations that are consistent with manual annotation. We also demonstrate the versatility of our approach by applying it to a variety of different imaging conditions and neuron types. The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a learning metric graph for neuron segmentation in electron microscopy images. text: Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for neuron segmentation in electron microscopy images using learning metric graphs. Our method leverages the rich spatial information present in these images to learn a metric graph, which is then used to partitions the image into distinct regions of interest.
