Electron microscopy imaging is an important tool for understanding the structure and function of biological systems at the nanoscale. However, analyzing these images remains challenging due to their large size and complex structures. In particular, identifying and isolating individual neurons within these images is a critical step in neuroscience research, but current methods are often time-consuming and prone to errors. Our proposed approach addresses these limitations by combining the strengths of both unsupervised and supervised learning methods. Specifically, we first apply clustering algorithms to identify rough partitions of the image, and then refine these partitions using a supervised learning framework. The key insight behind our approach is the use of a learning metric graph, which encodes spatial relationships between image pixels in a flexible and efficient manner. This allows us to capture subtle patterns and features that would be difficult or impossible to detect using traditional image processing techniques. We evaluate our approach on several publicly available datasets and compare its performance against state-of-the art segmentation methods. Our results demonstrate significant improvements in terms of accuracy, computational efficiency, and robustness to noise and variations in input data. Additionally, we perform thorough analysis of the learned metric graphs and provide insights into their structural properties and capabilities. The main contribution of this work is thus a scalable and accurate algorithm for neuron segmentation in electron microscopy images, which has important implications for a wide range of applications including neural connectomics, drug discovery, and material science. Future directions include further improving the computational efficiency of the algorithm, exploring new applications of the learned metric graphs, and integrating additional information such as annotated labels or domain knowledge into the segmentation process.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel deep learning approach for joint shape learning and segmentation of medical images. Our method utilizes a minimalistic deep network that combines the strengths of both shape learning and segmentation techniques to accurately identify and separate different structures within medical images. Existing methods for medical image segmentation often rely on complex and computationally expensive deep learning architectures, which can be a limiting factor for clinical applications. In contrast, our proposed method uses a simple and efficient network design that can be trained quickly and accurately, making it suitable for real-time applications. The key innovation of our approach is the use of a shared encoder-decoder architecture that simultaneously learns shape representations and performs image segmentation. This is achieved by introducing a novel loss function that combines shape-based and segmentation-based losses, allowing the network to learn both tasks simultaneously. We evaluate our method on several publicly available medical image datasets, including MRI and CT scans, and demonstrate its superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. Our experiments show that our method can accurately segment medical images while also learning meaningful shape representations that can be used for a variety of downstream tasks, such as image registration and landmark detection. Our work has important implications for medical image analysis, as it provides a fast, accurate, and efficient method for joint shape learning and segmentation. This can enable a wide range of clinical applications, including image-guided surgery, disease diagnosis, and treatment planning.

The hierarchy encoder extracts multi-scale features from the input image using a CNN, while the cascade decoder refines the segmentation results through a series of upsampling and convolution operations. Unlike traditional decoding methods that use a single upsample operation, our cascade decoder employs multiple upsamples with different dilation rates, allowing it to capture both local and global contextual information. text: This paper proposes a novel approach for iris recognition using image segmentation and retraining off-the-shelf deep neural networks. Existing iris recognition systems rely heavily on hand-crafted features and domain-specific algorithms, which can be time-consuming and costly to develop. In contrast, our approach leverages pre-trained deep neural networks to learn discriminative features from the iris images, resulting in improved recognition performance and reduced computational complexity. We begin by segmenting the iris images using a fully convolutional network (FCN), which produces a binary mask that isolates the iris region from the rest of the eye. We then apply data augmentation techniques to increase the size of the segmented iris images and retrain a pre-trained ResNet-50 network to learn iris-specific features. Our experimental results on the CASIA-IrisV4 dataset show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance in iris recognition, with an accuracy of 98.52%. Additionally, we perform a series of ablation studies to analyze the effectiveness of our approach, demonstrating that both image segmentation and feature learning contribute significantly to the improved recognition performance. Our work has important implications for the development of efficient and accurate iris recognition systems. By leveraging pre-trained deep neural networks and image segmentation techniques, we can reduce the computational complexity and development time of iris recognition systems, making them more accessible and cost-effective for a wider range of applications.

title: Iris Recognition with Image Segmentation Employing Retrained Off-the-Shelf Deep Neural Networks

text: This paper explores the use of retrainined off-the-shelf deep neural networks (DNNs) for iris recognition with image segmentation. Iris recognition is a widely used biometric modality that offers high accuracy and resistance to fraud. However, accurate iris recognition requires precise image segmentation to extract the iris region from the eye image. Traditional methods rely on hand-crafted features and manual feature extraction, which can be time-consuming and achieve limited performance. In this work, we propose a novel approach using DNNs to perform iris segmentation and recognition. We employ a pre-trained convolutional neural network (CNN) to learn a robust representation of the iris region from a large dataset of images. The CNN is fine-tuned on a smaller dataset of eye images, and the learned features are used to train a support vector machine (SVM) classifier for iris recognition. Our approach does not require any manual feature engineering or domain-specific knowledge, making it fast and efficient. We evaluate our method on several datasets of eye images and compare its performance against state-of-the-art approaches. Experimental results show that our method achieves high accuracy in both still image and video sequences, outperforming traditional methods in terms of speed and recognition rate. Furthermore, we analyze the effects of different parameters and provide insights into the working mechanism of our approach. Our findings demonstrate that off-the-shelf DNNs can be effectively employed for iris recognition, offering an alternative to laborious feature extraction and paving the way for real-time biometric applications. Future work includes further improving the accuracy by incorporating additional modalities and addressing challenges related to variability in lighting conditions and occlusions.

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for semantic segmentation of 3D magnetic resonance (MR) images, titled Holistic Decomposition Convolution (HDC). Existing methods for 3D MR image segmentation are limited by their reliance on voxel-wise processing, which neglects the inherent spatial and intensity relationships between voxels. HDC addresses this limitation by introducing a holistic decomposition of the 3D image, which captures the spatial and intensity correlations between voxels. The HDC approach consists of two stages: (1) holistic decomposition, and (2) convolutional neural network (CNN) processing. In the first stage, we decompose the 3D MR image into a set of spatial and intensity basis functions, which are generated using a novel optimization algorithm. These basis functions capture the underlying structure of the image and are used to represent the image in a more compact and informative way. In the second stage, we apply a 3D CNN to the decomposed image representation, leveraging the spatial and intensity information captured by the basis functions. The CNN is designed to learn the mapping between the decomposed image representation and the corresponding semantic labels. We evaluate the performance of HDC on a dataset of 3D MR images and compare it to state-of-the-art methods. Our results show that HDC outperforms existing methods in terms of segmentation accuracy, demonstrating the effectiveness of capturing holistic spatial and intensity relationships in 3D MR image segmentation. The main contributions of this paper are: * The introduction of a novel approach for 3D MR image segmentation based on holistic decomposition and CNN processing. * The development of a novel optimization algorithm for generating spatial and intensity basis functions that capture the underlying structure of 3D MR images. * The demonstration of the effectiveness of HDC in capturing holistic spatial and intensity relationships in 3D MR images, leading to improved segmentation accuracy. The proposed method has the potential to improve the accuracy and efficiency of 3D MR image segmentation, and could have a significant impact on various medical applications, such as tumor detection, lesion characterization

title: Holistic Decomposition Convolution for Effective Semantic Segmentation of 3D MR Images

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach to semantic segmentation of 3D magnetic resonance (MR) images called holistic decomposition convolution (HDC). This method combines the strengths of traditional convolutional neural networks (CNNs) with the representational power of dimensionality reduction techniques to improve the accuracy and efficiency of semantic segmentation in medical imaging. Existing methods for semantic segmentation of 3D MR images often rely on voxel-wise classification, which can be computationally expensive and may not fully capture the spatial relationships between different structures within the image. HDC addresses these limitations by decomposing the input volume into a set of lower-dimensional, non-overlapping feature maps that encode spatial information at multiple scales. These feature maps are then fed into a hierarchical CNN to learn high-level representations of the input data. The key innovation of HDC is the use of a novel activation function that allows the network to selectively emphasize or suppress certain features based on their relevance to the task at hand. This results in improved performance compared to traditional CNNs, as well as reduced computational cost due to the fewer number of parameters required to represent the input data. We evaluate HDC using several experiments on publicly available datasets of 3D MR images. Our results demonstrate significant improvements in segmentation accuracy compared to state-of-the-art baselines, as well as faster training times due to the reduced number of parameters. We also perform an ablation study to analyze the effectiveness of the proposed activation function and provide insights into its behavior. Our main contributions include: * The introduction of HDC, a novel method for efficient and accurate semantic segmentation of 3D MR images * A new activation function that selectively emphasizes relevant features and reduces the computational cost of the network * An extensive evaluation of HDC using several experiments on publicly available datasets, demonstrating its superior performance and efficiency compared to existing methods Overall, our work has important implications for improving the diagnosis and treatment of various neurological disorders, where accurate segmentation of brain structures from MR images is critical.

title: Holistic Decomposition Convolution for Effective Semantic Segmentation of 3D MR Images

text: In this paper, we present SwipeCut, a novel interactive segmentation method that leverages diversified seed proposals to improve the accuracy and efficiency of segmenting objects in images. Existing interactive segmentation methods rely on a single seed point or a small set of seed points to initiate the segmentation process, which can lead to suboptimal segmentation results, especially in cases where the object boundaries are complex or unclear. SwipeCut addresses this limitation by introducing a diversified seed proposal generation step that produces a large set of candidate seed points, each with a different orientation and location. These seed proposals are then used to initialize a novel optimization framework that refines the segmentation mask through a series of iterative updates. The optimization framework incorporates both image-level and user-level feedback to ensure that the resulting segmentation mask accurately reflects the user's intent. We evaluate SwipeCut on several benchmark datasets and compare it to state-of-the-art interactive segmentation methods. Our results show that SwipeCut achieves significantly better segmentation accuracy and faster convergence rates than existing methods, while also providing a more intuitive and user-friendly experience. We also demonstrate the versatility of SwipeCut by applying it to a variety of applications, including object segmentation, instance segmentation, and semantic segmentation. Our key contributions can be summarized as follows: * We introduce a novel interactive segmentation method called SwipeCut that leverages diversified seed proposals to improve segmentation accuracy and efficiency. * We propose a novel optimization framework that incorporates both image-level and user-level feedback to refine the segmentation mask. * We demonstrate the effectiveness and versatility of SwipeCut through extensive experiments on several benchmark datasets and various applications. Overall, SwipeCut represents a significant advancement in interactive segmentation technology, and we believe it has the potential to significantly improve the efficiency and accuracy of a wide range of image and video analysis tasks.

title: SwipeCut: Interactive Segmentation with Diversified Seed Proposals

text: In this paper, we present SwipeCut, an interactive segmentation method that leverages diversified seed proposals to improve the accuracy and efficiency of image segmentation. Existing interactive segmentation methods rely on a single initial guess or user input, which can be time-consuming and may not capture the desired details. SwipeCut addresses these limitations by generating multiple diverse seed proposals that cover various regions of interest in an image. We propose a novel algorithm that combines region proposal networks (RPNs) and density-based clustering to generate high-quality seed proposals. The RPN generates a set of regions of interest, which are then used as seeds for density-based clustering. By iteratively adding clusters and refining their boundaries, our method produces a diverse set of seed proposals that cover different parts of the image. We evaluate our method on several benchmark datasets and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art interactive segmentation techniques in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce a new post-processing technique called boundary smoothness optimization, which enhances the quality of the final segmentation masks produced by SwipeCut. This technique enables us to further improve the segmentation results while maintaining the same level of interactivity. Our experiments demonstrate that SwipeCut achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks while providing a more intuitive and efficient interaction experience for users.

title: SwipeCut: Interactive Segmentation with Diversified Seed Proposals

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for accelerating matrix-vector products involving the graph Laplacian of fully connected networks using a combination of the non-uniform fast Fourier transform (NFFT) and Krylov subspace methods. The graph Laplacian is a fundamental matrix in graph theory and plays a central role in various applications, including graph partitioning, clustering, and solving linear systems. However, computing matrix-vector products with the graph Laplacian can be computationally expensive, especially for large graphs. Our proposed method leverages the NFFT to efficiently compute the matrix-vector products by exploiting the spectral properties of the graph Laplacian. Specifically, we use the NFFT to approximate the inverse of the graph Laplacian, which enables us to compute matrix-vector products using a series of fast Fourier transforms. We also employ Krylov subspace methods to further accelerate the computation by exploiting the structure of the graph Laplacian. Our experimental results demonstrate that our proposed method significantly outperforms existing methods in terms of computational efficiency and scalability. We show that our method can handle large-scale graphs with millions of nodes and edges in a matter of seconds, while maintaining high accuracy. We also demonstrate the versatility of our method by applying it to various graph-related tasks, such as graph partitioning, clustering, and solving linear systems. Our work has important implications for a wide range of applications that involve computing matrix-vector products with the graph Laplacian. Our method enables faster and more efficient processing of large-scale graph data, which can lead to breakthroughs in various fields, including social network analysis, recommendation systems, and machine learning.
label: ai
title: NFFT meets Krylov methods: Fast matrix-vector products for the graph Laplacian of fully connected networks

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for accelerating matrix-vector products involving the graph Laplacian of fully connected networks using the combination of Near Field Fourier Transform (NFFT) and Krylov subspace methods. The graph Laplacian is a critical component in various network analysis tasks such as partitioning, clustering, and centrality measures. However, its large size and sparse structure make it challenging to compute matrix-vector products efficiently. Our proposed method leverages the advantages of NFFT, which enables fast computation of long-range interactions in spatial domains, and Krylov subspace methods, which provide efficient solutions to linear systems. We first apply NFFT to transform the graph Laplacian into a compact representation, reducing the number of nonzero elements by exploiting the decay of similarity between nodes with distance. We then employ a Krylov subspace method to solve the system associated with the transformed matrix, achieving faster convergence than traditional methods. The proposed approach significantly reduces computational complexity and memory usage compared to state-of-the-art methods while maintaining comparable accuracy. Our experiments on several real-world networks demonstrate that our method outperforms existing techniques in terms of time efficiency and scalability, making it an attractive choice for large-scale network analysis applications. Additionally, we prove the theoretical correctness and convergence properties of our algorithm, ensuring its reliability and robustness in practice. Overall, this work introduces the concept of combining NFFT and Krylov methods for fast matrix-vector products in graph computing, paving the way for more efficient and accurate analyses of complex networks.
label: ai
title: NFFT meets Krylov methods: Fast matrix-vector products for the graph Laplacian of fully connected networks

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for exclusive independent probability estimation using deep 3D fully convolutional dense networks (FCNs) for isointense infant brain MRI segmentation. Traditional methods for brain MRI segmentation rely on manual annotation of training data, which can be time-consuming and expensive to obtain. In contrast, our approach leverages the concept of exclusive independence to learn a probability distribution over the different tissue classes in the brain, without requiring annotated data. We propose a deep 3D FCN architecture that combines convolutional and dense connectivity patterns to learn both local and global features from the input data. The network is trained using a novel loss function that encourages the model to produce exclusive and independent probability estimates for each tissue class. We evaluate our approach on a large dataset of isointense infant brain MRIs and show that it outperforms traditional supervised learning methods, as well as other state-of-the-art unsupervised segmentation techniques. Our results demonstrate the effectiveness of exclusive independent probability estimation using deep 3D FCNs for brain MRI segmentation, and highlight the potential of this approach for other applications in medical imaging where annotated data is scarce.
label: ai
title: Exclusive Independent Probability Estimation using Deep 3D Fully Convolutional DenseNets: Application to IsoIntense Infant Brain MRI Segmentation

text: Abstract Accurate segmentation of brain structures from Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) scans is crucial for studying brain development and identifying neurological disorders in infants. However, manual segmentation is time-consuming and subjective, leading to variability and inconsistency across different observers. To address this challenge, we propose an automated segmentation method based on deep learning techniques called Exclusive Independent Probability Estimation using Deep 3D Fully Convolutional DenseNets (EIPE-FCD). Our proposed method combines a deep neural network architecture with a novel probability estimation technique that enables exclusive and independent probability estimation for each class label. This allows for accurate segmentation of multiple classes, including the challenging task of separating the cortical white matter, cerebrospinal fluid, and blood vessels in infant brains. We evaluate our method on a large dataset of isointense infant brain MRIs and show that it outperforms state-of-the art methods in terms of accuracy and efficiency. Additionally, we investigate the effectiveness of different architectures and provide insights into the contribution of various design choices to the performance of the model. Our results demonstrate that EIPE-FCD achieves high accuracy and robustness in segmenting brain structures from isointense infant brain MRIs, which has important implications for future studies investigating early brain development and neurological disorders. Moreover, the proposed method can be easily extended to other applications like image segmentation tasks in medical imaging or computer vision fields.

text: Brain tumors are complex and aggressive cancers that pose significant challenges for clinicians and researchers. Accurate segmentation of brain tumors is essential for surgical planning, radiation therapy, and monitoring treatment response. Similarly, predicting overall survival is crucial for clinical decision-making and personalized patient care. In this paper, we propose an ensemble of 3D U-Nets for brain tumor segmentation and overall survival prediction using radiomic features. Our proposed method utilizes a combination of three 3D U-Nets, each with a different architecture, to segment brain tumors from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans. The ensemble approach allows for improved accuracy and robustness compared to a single model. We evaluate the performance of our method using a dataset of 100 brain tumor patients and demonstrate superior segmentation accuracy compared to existing methods. In addition to tumor segmentation, we extract radiomic features from the segmented tumors and use them to predict overall survival. Radiomic features are quantitative imaging biomarkers that capture the spatial and intensity patterns of the tumor. We use a machine learning algorithm to identify the most relevant radiomic features that are associated with overall survival. Our results show that the combination of radiomic features and clinical variables can significantly improve the prediction of overall survival compared to clinical variables alone. Our findings have important implications for the diagnosis and treatment of brain tumors. Accurate segmentation of brain tumors can help clinicians identify the most effective treatment strategies and monitor treatment response. Predicting overall survival can help clinicians make informed decisions about patient care and tailor treatment plans to individual patients. Our ensemble of 3D U-Nets and radiomic feature analysis provides a promising approach for improving brain tumor diagnosis and treatment.

title: Brain Tumor Segmentation using an Ensemble of 3D U-Nets and Overall Survival Prediction using Radiomic Features

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach for brain tumor segmentation using an ensemble of 3D U-Nets and overall survival prediction using radiomic features. Brain tumor segmentation is a crucial step in diagnosis and treatment planning, but it remains a challenging task due to the complexity and variability of brain tumors. To address this challenge, we utilize an ensemble of 3D U-Nets, which are deep learning models that have shown promising results in image segmentation tasks. Our proposed method consists of two main components: (1) brain tumor segmentation using an ensemble of 3D U-Nets, and (2) overall survival prediction using radiomic features extracted from the segmented tumor regions. The ensemble of 3D U-Nets is trained on a large dataset of magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scans to accurately segment brain tumors. The segments generated by each model are combined using a novel fusion strategy to produce the final segmentation mask. To predict overall survival, we extract a set of radiomic features from the segmented tumor regions, including intensity, texture, and shape features. These features are then used to train a machine learning model that can predict patient survival outcomes. We evaluate our method on a dataset of MRI scans collected from patients with glioblastoma, the most common and aggressive type of primary brain cancer. The results show that our proposed method outperforms traditional tumor segmentation methods and achieves high accuracy in terms of Dice similarity coefficient and Hausdorff distance. Furthermore, the survival prediction model based on radiomic features shows strong predictive power, with a concordance index of 0.75. Our findings demonstrate the potential of using an ensemble of 3D U-Nets and radiomic features for accurate brain tumor segmentation and overall survival prediction. This approach could help improve clinical decision-making and personalized treatment strategies for brain cancer patients.

title: Brain Tumor Segmentation using an Ensemble of 3D U-Nets and Overall Survival Prediction using Radiomic Features

text: In this paper, we present a novel approach for joint segmentation and localization in videos and images, which we term "foreground clustering." Our method leverages the strengths of both clustering and contour-based segmentation techniques to simultaneously segment and localize objects in the foreground of an image or video sequence. Existing approaches for joint segmentation and localization often rely on complex models that require careful parameter tuning and may not perform well in the presence of occlusions, clutter, or varying illumination conditions. In contrast, our foreground clustering method is robust, efficient, and easy to implement. We begin by applying a background subtraction algorithm to the video or image sequence to obtain a sequence of foreground masks. We then apply a clustering algorithm to the foreground masks to group pixels into distinct clusters, which correspond to individual objects in the scene. Next, we use a contour-based segmentation algorithm to extract the boundaries of each object, which provides a precise segmentation mask for each object. To localize the objects in the scene, we propose a novel feature called the "spatial layout map," which encodes the spatial relationships between objects in the scene. We use this feature to train a machine learning model to predict the location of each object in the scene. We evaluate our method on several challenging datasets, including videos and images with complex scenes, occlusions, and varying illumination conditions. Our experiments demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art methods for joint segmentation and localization in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Our contributions can be summarized as follows: * We propose a novel approach for joint segmentation and localization in videos and images based on foreground clustering. * We introduce a new feature called the spatial layout map, which encodes the spatial relationships between objects in the scene. * We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on several challenging datasets, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in terms of both accuracy and efficiency. Overall, our work has important implications for a wide range of applications, including object tracking, human-computer interaction, and video surveillance.
label: ai
title: Foreground Clustering for Joint Segmentation and Localization in Videos and Images

text: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to joint segmentation and localization in videos and images called Foreground Clustering. The proposed method leverages the strengths of both spectral clustering and graph-based algorithms to simultaneously segment objects from their backgrounds and estimate their positions within the scene. Existing methods often treat these tasks separately, requiring manual parameter tuning and resulting in suboptimal performance when dealing with complex scenes or varying illumination conditions. In contrast, our method treats the problems as interrelated and uses them to mutually improve each other. By iteratively refining object boundaries and motion estimates through careful cluster assignments, Foreground Clustering achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on several benchmark datasets while providing real-time performance. The key idea is to construct an affinity graph that encodes appearance, spatial, and temporal information across frames and then applies spectral clustering to partition it into coherent foreground structures. These are then used to seed dense trajectory optimization, which revises object boundaries based on motion cues and enforces temporal consistency. This process alternates between clustering and tracking until convergence, where the final output consists of separated objects and their associated motion fields. Extensive experiments show that Foreground Clustering outperforms existing methods under various scenarios, such as occlusions, cluttered scenes, shadows, and low light conditions. Additionally, its ability to handle irregular object shapes and non-rigid deformations makes it particularly suitable for applications like multi-object tracking, activity recognition, and video surveillance. Overall, the proposed method significantly advances the field by introducing a robust solution that can effectively address challenging scenarios in computer vision.
label: ai
title: Foreground Clustering for Joint Segmentation and Localization in Videos and Images

text: \begin{quote} \textbf{FUTURE-AI: Guiding Principles and Consensus Recommendations for Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence in Future Medical Imaging} \textbf{Abstract} Artificial intelligence (AI) is poised to transform medical imaging, with the potential to improve the quality of care, reduce costs, and increase access to care. However, the widespread adoption of AI in medical imaging is contingent on the development of trustworthy AI systems. In this paper, we present the consensus recommendations of the Future of Artificial Intelligence in Medical Imaging (FUTURE-AI) working group, a multidisciplinary group of experts in medical imaging, AI, and ethics. The FUTURE-AI working group was convened by the American College of Radiology (ACR) and the Radiological Society of North America (RSNA) to develop a set of guiding principles and consensus recommendations for trustworthy AI in medical imaging. The FUTURE-AI working group identified four guiding principles for trustworthy AI in medical imaging: (1) transparency, (2) explainability, (3) fairness, and (4) accountability. The FUTURE-AI working group also identified a set of consensus recommendations for trustworthy AI in medical imaging, including recommendations for the development, validation, and implementation of AI systems. The FUTURE-AI working group's recommendations are intended to guide the development of trustworthy AI systems in medical imaging and to promote the safe and effective use of AI in clinical practice. \textbf{Keywords} Artificial intelligence, medical imaging, radiology, ethics, transparency, explainability, fairness, accountability \textbf{MSC} 68T05, 68T10, 68T15, 68T20, 68T30, 68T40, 68T50, 68T60, 68T70, 68T80, 68T90, 68T99, 68W05, 68W10, 68W20, 68W30, 68W40, 68W50, 68W60, 68W70, 68W80, 68W90, 68W99, 68Z05, 68Z10, 68Z20, 68Z30, 68Z40, 68Z50, 68Z60, 68Z

text: > **Abstract:** > > Semantic image segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision. Recent years have witnessed the rapid development of deep learning-based methods, which have achieved remarkable performance. However, the performance of these methods is still far from satisfactory, especially for the challenging scenarios, such as the images with complex backgrounds and low resolutions. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, named Double Similarity Distillation (DSD), to improve the performance of semantic image segmentation. Specifically, we first introduce a similarity distillation module to learn the similarity between the feature maps of the teacher and student networks. Then, we propose a double similarity distillation module to learn the similarity between the feature maps of the teacher network and the ground truth. The proposed DSD framework can be easily integrated into existing semantic image segmentation networks. text: > Polyp detection and localisation is a challenging task in colonoscopy. The main challenge is the large variation in the appearance of polyps. In this paper, we propose a novel ensemble learning method based on classifier prediction confidence and comprehensive learning particle swarm optimisation (CLPSO) for polyp localisation. The proposed method consists of two stages. In the first stage, a set of classifiers are trained to detect polyps. In the second stage, the prediction confidence of each classifier is calculated and used to generate a set of candidate regions. Then, a comprehensive learning particle swarm optimisation (CLPSO) algorithm is used to select the best candidate region. The proposed method is evaluated on the Kvasir-SEG dataset. The experimental results show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. text: Pneumothorax is a condition in which air enters the pleural space, causing the lung to collapse. It is a common complication of chest trauma and can be life-threatening if not treated promptly. Pneumothorax is often diagnosed using chest radiographs, which are readily available and relatively inexpensive. However, the diagnosis of pneumothorax can be challenging, especially in cases with subtle findings. Deep learning image segmentation is a promising approach for the automated detection of pneumothorax. In this study, we developed and evaluated a deep learning model for the segmentation of pneumothorax on chest radiographs. Our model was trained on a large dataset of chest radiographs and achieved high accuracy in the segmentation of pneumothorax. We also evaluated the performance of our model in the detection of pneumothorax in a separate dataset of chest radiographs. Our results show that deep learning image segmentation can be an effective tool for the detection of pneumothorax on chest radiographs. text: > We propose a novel self-guided and cross-guided learning framework for few-shot segmentation. The proposed framework consists of two stages: self-guided learning and cross-guided learning. In the self-guided learning stage, we first train a segmentation model with a large number of labeled images. Then, we use the trained model to generate pseudo labels for unlabeled images. In the cross-guided learning stage, we train a segmentation model with the pseudo labels generated in the self-guided learning stage and a small number of labeled images. The proposed framework can be applied to both the one-shot and the few-shot segmentation tasks. We conduct extensive experiments on the PASCAL VOC 2012 and the COCO-Stuff datasets. The experimental results show that the proposed framework can achieve state-of-the-art performance on both datasets. ## 2. Related Work ### 2.1 Few-Shot Learning Few-shot learning is a branch of machine learning that aims to learn from a small number of training examples. It has been widely studied in the fields of computer vision, natural language processing, and reinforcement learning. In the field of computer vision, few-shot learning has been applied to tasks such as image classification, object detection, and semantic segmentation. Image classification is one of the most widely studied tasks in few-shot learning. The goal of image classification is to classify an image into one of a set of predefined classes. In the few-shot setting, the model is only given a small number of labeled images for each class, and it must learn to classify new images into the same classes. Object detection is another important task in few-shot learning. The goal of object detection is to locate and classify objects in an image. In the few-shot setting, the model is only given a small number of labeled images for each class, and it must learn to detect new objects in the same classes. Semantic segmentation is a task that aims to assign a class label to each pixel in an image. text: > We present a novel unsupervised medical image segmentation method based on contrastive registration. Our method is based on the idea that the registration of two images should be easier if they are from the same class. We use this idea to train a network to predict the registration parameters between two images. We then use the predicted parameters to warp one image to the other and use the warped image as a pseudo-label for the other image. We show that this pseudo-label can be used to train a segmentation network. We evaluate our method on the task of segmenting the prostate from MRI images. We show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art unsupervised methods on this task. ## Contrastive Registration for Unsupervised Medical Image Segmentation ### Abstract > We present a novel unsupervised medical image segmentation method based on contrastive registration. Our method is based on the idea that the registration of two images should be easier if they are from the same class. We use this idea to train a network to predict the registration parameters between two images. We then use the predicted parameters to warp one image to the other and use the warped image as a pseudo-label for the other image. We show that this pseudo-label can be used to train a segmentation network. We evaluate our method on the task of segmenting the prostate from MRI images. We show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art unsupervised methods on this task. ### Introduction Unsupervised medical image segmentation is an important problem in medical image analysis. The first approach is to use self-supervised learning. This approach uses the images themselves as the supervision signal. For example, the images can be used to predict the rotation angle or the translation vector between two images. The second approach is to use contrastive learning. text: FedDG: Federated Domain Generalization on Medical Image Segmentation via Episodic Learning in Continuous Frequency Space Abstract: Domain generalization (DG) aims to learn a model that can generalize to unseen domains. In medical image segmentation, DG is particularly important as it can help to alleviate the need for large amounts of annotated data from multiple domains. However, existing DG methods are not suitable for medical image segmentation due to the following challenges: (1) the domain shift is not well-defined in medical image segmentation, (2) the domain shift is not well-captured by existing DG methods, and (3) the domain shift is not well-captured by existing DG methods. To address these challenges, we propose FedDG, a federated domain generalization framework for medical image segmentation. FedDG is based on the idea of episodic learning in continuous frequency space, which enables it to learn domain-invariant features by leveraging the domain shift information in the frequency domain. We also propose a novel frequency-based domain shift metric to measure the domain shift in the frequency domain. Extensive experiments on two medical image segmentation datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of FedDG. text: > We propose a semi-supervised learning framework for medical image segmentation. The framework is based on the idea of dual-task consistency, which enforces the consistency between the segmentation task and the auxiliary task. The auxiliary task is to predict the segmentation mask of the unlabeled data. The segmentation task is to predict the segmentation mask of the labeled data. The framework is trained in an end-to-end manner. The segmentation task is supervised by the labeled data, while the auxiliary task is supervised by the labeled data and the unlabeled data. The framework is evaluated on the task of brain tumor segmentation. The results show that the framework can achieve competitive performance compared to the fully supervised learning framework. ## 2. Related Work ### 2.1 Semi-supervised Learning Semi-supervised learning is a machine learning paradigm that aims to learn from both labeled and unlabeled data. It is a promising approach for medical image segmentation, as it can leverage the large amount of unlabeled data to improve the performance of the segmentation model. There are two main approaches to semi-supervised learning: self-training and consistency regularization. Self-training is a method that uses the predictions of the model on the unlabeled data as pseudo labels and then trains the model on the labeled and pseudo-labeled data. Consistency regularization is a method that enforces the consistency between the predictions of the model on the labeled and unlabeled data. ### 2.2 Medical Image Segmentation Medical image segmentation is a challenging task that aims to identify and delineate the regions of interest in medical images. It is an important step in the diagnosis and treatment of diseases. There are two main approaches to medical image segmentation: supervised learning and unsupervised learning. Supervised learning requires a large amount of labeled data, while unsupervised learning does not require any labeled data. ## 3. text: > We propose a novel image segmentation framework, Co-Seg, to address the problem of label corruption in image segmentation. Label corruption refers to the situation where the ground truth labels are not accurate. In this paper, we propose a novel framework to address the label corruption problem. We first propose a novel loss function, called the co-segmentation loss, to train the segmentation model. The co-segmentation loss is designed to encourage the model to learn the co-segmentation of the corrupted labels. We then propose a novel co-segmentation module to refine the segmentation results. The co-segmentation module is designed to refine the segmentation results by co-segmenting the corrupted labels. We conduct extensive experiments on the Cityscapes dataset and the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset. The experimental results show that our method can effectively address the label corruption problem and achieve state-of-the-art performance. ## 2. Related Work ### 2.1 Image Segmentation Image segmentation is a fundamental task in computer vision. It aims to assign a label to each pixel in an image. In recent years, deep learning has achieved great success in image segmentation. Fully convolutional networks (FCNs) [1] are the first deep learning based method for image segmentation. FCNs use a fully convolutional network to extract features from the input image and then use a deconvolutional network to generate the segmentation results. FCNs have achieved great success in image segmentation. However, FCNs are not designed to address the label corruption problem. To address the label corruption problem, some methods have been proposed. For example, [2] proposes a method to address the label corruption problem by using a generative adversarial network (GAN). [3] proposes a method to address the label corruption problem by using a conditional random field (CRF). [4] proposes a method to address the label corruption problem by using a self-supervised learning method. However, these methods are not designed to address the label corruption problem. ### 2.2 Co-Segmentation Co-segmentation is a task that aims to segment the same object in different images. text: > We propose a novel framework for knowledge transfer in medical image segmentation. The proposed framework is network-agnostic, which means that it can be applied to any segmentation network. The framework consists of two components: a feature extractor and a feature decoder. The feature extractor is a pre-trained segmentation network, which is used to extract features from the input image. The feature decoder is a lightweight network that is trained to decode the features into a segmentation mask. The feature extractor and the feature decoder are trained jointly in an end-to-end manner. The proposed framework can be used for both supervised and unsupervised knowledge transfer. In the supervised setting, the feature extractor is pre-trained on a large dataset of annotated images, and the feature decoder is trained on a smaller dataset of annotated images. In the unsupervised setting, the feature extractor is pre-trained on a large dataset of unlabeled images, and the feature decoder is trained on a smaller dataset of unlabeled images. The proposed framework has been evaluated on two medical image segmentation tasks: brain tumor segmentation and liver segmentation. The results show that the proposed framework outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in both tasks. ## 2. Introduction Medical image segmentation is an important task in medical image analysis. It is used to identify and delineate the regions of interest in medical images. This task is challenging due to the complexity of medical images and the lack of annotated data. In recent years, deep learning has shown great success in medical image segmentation. However, the performance of deep learning models is highly dependent on the amount of annotated data available. This is a major challenge in medical image segmentation, as it is often difficult and expensive to obtain large amounts of annotated data. One way to address this challenge is to transfer knowledge from a source domain to a target domain. This can be done in a supervised or unsupervised manner. In the supervised setting, a model is trained on a large dataset of annotated images from the source domain, and then fine-tuned on a smaller dataset of annotated images from the target domain. In the unsupervised setting, a model is trained on a large dataset of unlabeled images from the source domain, and then fine-tuned on a smaller dataset of unlabeled images from the target domain. text: We propose a novel framework for multimodal and semi-supervised image segmentation. Our method is based on a disentanglement-alignment-fusion strategy. First, we disentangle the input image into a set of latent representations, each of which is associated with a specific modality. Then, we align the latent representations across modalities by minimizing a modality-invariant loss. Finally, we fuse the aligned latent representations to generate the segmentation mask. Our method is able to leverage the complementary information from multiple modalities and can be easily extended to semi-supervised learning by incorporating unlabeled data. Extensive experiments on three multimodal datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of our method. text: > In this paper, we propose a novel semi-supervised instance segmentation framework, which is able to leverage unlabeled data to improve the performance of instance segmentation. Our method is based on a simple yet effective idea: we first train a segmentation model on labeled data, and then use the trained model to generate pseudo labels for unlabeled data. The pseudo labels are then used to train a new segmentation model, which is expected to be better than the previous one. To improve the quality of pseudo labels, we propose a mask-guided sample selection strategy, which selects samples that are likely to be correctly labeled by the segmentation model. Our method is simple and effective, and it can be easily applied to existing semi-supervised instance segmentation frameworks. This approach has been shown to be effective in many tasks, such as image classification [1], object detection [2], and semantic segmentation [3]. Another popular approach is consistency regularization, which enforces the model to make consistent predictions on different views of the same data. text: We present a novel deep learning framework for the joint reconstruction and segmentation of the cardiac walls in 3D from CT data. The proposed method, called PC-U Net, is based on a 3D U-Net architecture with a novel loss function that combines a pixel-wise loss and a perceptual loss. The pixel-wise loss is based on the mean squared error between the predicted and the ground truth segmentation, while the perceptual loss is based on the mean squared error between the feature maps of the encoder and decoder parts of the network. The proposed method is evaluated on a dataset of 100 CT scans of the heart. The results show that the proposed method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in terms of the Dice similarity coefficient and the Hausdorff distance. The proposed method is also shown to be robust to noise and artifacts in the CT data. The following is the full text of the abstract for a research paper titled "Deep Learning for Cardiac Segmentation in CT Images: A Review" from arxiv.org: Cardiac segmentation in CT images is a challenging task due to the presence of noise and artifacts in the images. Deep learning methods have shown promising results in this area. In this paper, we review the recent advances in deep learning for cardiac segmentation in CT images. We discuss the different deep learning architectures that have been proposed for this task, including convolutional neural networks, recurrent neural networks, and generative adversarial networks. We also discuss the different datasets that have been used for training and evaluating these methods. Finally, we discuss the challenges and future directions in this area. text: > We propose a novel shape-aware semi-supervised 3D semantic segmentation framework for medical images. The proposed framework consists of a shape-aware feature extractor and a shape-aware segmentation network. The shape-aware feature extractor is designed to extract shape-aware features from the input 3D medical image. The shape-aware segmentation network is designed to segment the input 3D medical image based on the shape-aware features. The shape-aware feature extractor and the shape-aware segmentation network are trained jointly in an end-to-end manner. The shape-aware feature extractor is trained to extract shape-aware features that are useful for the shape-aware segmentation network to segment the input 3D medical image. The shape-aware segmentation network is trained to segment the input 3D medical image based on the shape-aware features extracted by the shape-aware feature extractor. The proposed framework is evaluated on the BraTS 2019 dataset. The experimental results show that the proposed framework outperforms the state-of-the-art semi-supervised 3D semantic segmentation frameworks for medical images. ## 2. It is difficult to obtain a large amount of labeled data in many real-world applications. Semi-supervised learning is a promising approach to address this problem. Semi-supervised learning aims to learn a model from a small amount of labeled data and a large amount of unlabeled data. text: The paper presents a human-in-the-loop system for the analysis of tool wear in industrial machining processes. The system is based on a deep learning model that predicts the tool wear from the images of the machined surface. The model is trained on a large dataset of images and the corresponding tool wear values. The system uses the uncertainty of the model predictions to determine when to ask the user for help. The user is then presented with a set of images and asked to select the one that best represents the tool wear. The system uses the user's input to update the model and improve its predictions. The system is evaluated on a dataset of images of machined surfaces and the corresponding tool wear values. The results show that the system is able to improve the model's predictions and reduce the uncertainty of the predictions. text: \begin{abstract} \textbf{Motivation:} Cardiac segmentation is a fundamental step in cardiac image analysis. However, the annotation of cardiac segmentation is labor-intensive and time-consuming. To alleviate the annotation burden, we propose a novel framework, Dual-Teacher, which integrates intra-domain and inter-domain teachers to learn from unlabeled data. \textbf{Method:} We first train an intra-domain teacher to learn from unlabeled data. Then, we train an inter-domain teacher to learn from the pseudo-labeled data generated by the intra-domain teacher. Finally, we train a student network to learn from the pseudo-labeled data generated by the inter-domain teacher. \textbf{Results:} We evaluate our method on the public dataset, the 2017 ACDC Challenge dataset. The experimental results show that our method outperforms the state-of-the-art methods. \textbf{Conclusion:} Our method can effectively alleviate the annotation burden of cardiac segmentation. \end{abstract} \end{document}
label: ai
title: Dual-Teacher: Integrating Intra-domain and Inter-domain Teachers for Annotation-efficient Cardiac Segmentation

text: > We present a novel approach for robust semantic segmentation in adverse weather conditions. Our method is based on a fast video-sequence segmentation algorithm that is able to segment a video sequence into a set of homogeneous regions. The segmentation is performed in a coarse-to-fine manner, where the coarse segmentation is obtained by means of a fast and robust algorithm, and the fine segmentation is obtained by means of a more accurate but slower algorithm. The coarse segmentation is used to obtain a set of candidate regions, and the fine segmentation is used to select the best candidate region for each pixel. The proposed method is evaluated on the Cityscapes dataset, and the results show that it is able to achieve a high accuracy even in adverse weather conditions. ## The Cityscapes Dataset The Cityscapes dataset is a large-scale dataset for semantic urban scene understanding. It contains a diverse set of high-quality images, annotated with pixel-level semantic labels. The dataset is designed to be challenging, with a wide variety of scenes, weather conditions, and object classes. The dataset is publicly available and can be used for research and development of semantic segmentation algorithms. The Cityscapes dataset contains a total of 5000 high-resolution images, with a resolution of 2048 x 1024 pixels. text: We propose a novel self-supervised learning framework for 3D medical image segmentation. Our method is based on the idea of inpainting, where we train a network to predict the missing parts of a 3D image. We propose a novel inpainting strategy that leverages the region-of-interest (ROI) information to guide the inpainting process. Our method is based on the idea of supervoxel inpainting, where we first segment the image into supervoxels and then inpaint each supervoxel individually. We show that our method can achieve competitive performance compared to fully supervised methods, while requiring no manual annotations. Our method is also more robust to noise and missing data compared to other self-supervised methods. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on two challenging medical image segmentation tasks: brain tumor segmentation and liver segmentation. Our method achieves state-of-the-art performance on both tasks, while requiring no manual annotations. The convolutional layer is used to extract features from the input image, the max-pooling layer is used to reduce the dimensionality of the feature map, the fully connected layer is used to classify the features into different classes, and the softmax layer is used to compute the probability of each class. text: > We propose a semi-supervised few-shot learning framework for medical image segmentation. The proposed framework is based on a novel meta-learning approach that leverages the unlabeled data to improve the segmentation performance of the few-shot learning model. The proposed framework consists of two stages: a meta-training stage and a meta-testing stage. In the meta-training stage, the model is trained on a large number of labeled images and a small number of unlabeled images. The model is then used to generate pseudo labels for the unlabeled images. In the meta-testing stage, the model is fine-tuned on a small number of labeled images and the pseudo labels generated in the meta-training stage. The proposed framework is evaluated on the task of brain tumor segmentation in MRI images. text: > We present a deep learning model for unified perceptual parsing, which jointly performs object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. Our model is based on a deep grouping network, which is a fully convolutional network that performs grouping of pixels into objects. The grouping is performed by a recurrent neural network that is trained to predict the next grouping step based on the current grouping state. The grouping network is trained end-to-end with a loss function that combines the losses for object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. We show that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset for all three tasks. ## Deep Grouping Model for Unified Perceptual Parsing ### Abstract We present a deep learning model for unified perceptual parsing, which jointly performs object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. Our model is based on a deep grouping network, which is a fully convolutional network that performs grouping of pixels into objects. The grouping is performed by a recurrent neural network that is trained to predict the next grouping step based on the current grouping state. The grouping network is trained end-to-end with a loss function that combines the losses for object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. We show that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the PASCAL VOC 2012 dataset for all three tasks. ### Introduction Perceptual parsing is the task of understanding the visual content of an image. It is a fundamental problem in computer vision, with many applications in image understanding, robotics, and autonomous driving. In this paper, we focus on the problem of unified perceptual parsing, which jointly performs object detection, semantic segmentation, and instance segmentation. Object detection is the task of detecting and localizing objects in an image. It is a fundamental problem in computer vision, with many applications in image understanding, robotics, and autonomous driving. Semantic segmentation is the task of assigning a semantic label to each pixel in an image. It is a fundamental problem in computer vision, with many applications in image understanding, robotics, and autonomous driving. Instance segmentation is the task of assigning a semantic label to each pixel in an image and also identifying which instance of the object the pixel belongs to. text: We propose a variational image segmentation model based on normalized cut with adaptive similarity and spatial regularization. The model is formulated as a minimization problem of a functional consisting of three terms: the first term is the normalized cut functional, the second term is the adaptive similarity term, and the third term is the spatial regularization term. The adaptive similarity term is introduced to improve the segmentation accuracy by adaptively adjusting the similarity between the image and the segmentation. The spatial regularization term is introduced to improve the segmentation accuracy by incorporating the spatial information of the image. The proposed model is solved by the alternating direction method of multipliers. The experimental results on several benchmark datasets show that the proposed model can achieve better segmentation accuracy than the state-of-the-art methods. text: > We propose a new method for computing valid p-values for image segmentation. The method is based on the selective inference framework, which is a general method for computing valid p-values for any hypothesis test. The method is applicable to any segmentation algorithm that produces a segmentation map and a segmentation score. The segmentation score is used to compute a p-value for the hypothesis that the segmentation map is the best possible segmentation map. The method is illustrated on the problem of segmenting a brain MRI image into gray matter, white matter, and cerebrospinal fluid. The method is also illustrated on the problem of segmenting a brain MRI image into multiple tissue types. The method is shown to be computationally efficient and to produce valid p-values. ## References - [1] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [2] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [3] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [4] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [5] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [6] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [7] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [8] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [9] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [10] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [11] [Selective Inference: A Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/1805.04004) - [12] [Selective Inference: A Review

text: In this paper, we propose a novel method for exploiting clinically available delineations for CNN-based segmentation in radiotherapy treatment planning. We first train a CNN to segment the target and organs at risk (OARs) from CT images. Then, we use the trained CNN to generate a set of pseudo-segmentations for the target and OARs. We then use the pseudo-segmentations to train a second CNN to segment the target and OARs. We show that the second CNN can achieve better segmentation performance than the first CNN. We also show that the second CNN can achieve better segmentation performance than a CNN that is trained on manually annotated segmentations. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our method on a dataset of head and neck cancer patients. Our results show that our method can improve the segmentation performance of the CNN by up to 10%. text: In this paper, we proposed a novel mutual consistency network (MC-Net+) to effectively exploit the unlabeled hard regions for semi-supervised medical image segmentation. The MC-Net+ model is motivated by the observation that deep models trained with limited annotations are prone to output highly uncertain and easily mis-classified predictions in the ambiguous regions (e.g. adhesive edges or thin branches) for the image segmentation task. Leveraging these region-level challenging samples can make the semi-supervised segmentation model training more effective. Therefore, our proposed MC-Net+ model consists of two new designs. First, the model contains one shared encoder and multiple sightly different decoders (i.e. using different up-sampling strategies). The statistical discrepancy of multiple decoders' outputs is computed to denote the model's uncertainty, which indicates the unlabeled hard regions. Second, a new mutual consistency constraint is enforced between one decoder's probability output and other decoders' soft pseudo labels. In this way, we minimize the model's uncertainty during training and force the model to generate invariant and low-entropy results in such challenging areas of unlabeled data, in order to learn a generalized feature representation. We compared the segmentation results of the MC-Net+ with five state-of-the-art semi-supervised approaches on three public medical datasets. Extension experiments with two common semi-supervised settings demonstrate the superior performance of our model over other existing methods, which sets a new state of the art for semi-supervised medical image segmentation.
label: human
title: Enforcing Mutual Consistency of Hard Regions for Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation

text: Semantic segmentation of fine-resolution urban scene images plays a vital role in extensive practical applications, such as land cover mapping, urban change detection, environmental protection and economic assessment. Driven by rapid developments in deep learning technologies, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) have dominated the semantic segmentation task for many years. Convolutional neural networks adopt hierarchical feature representation and have strong local context extraction. However, the local property of the convolution layer limits the network from capturing global information that is crucial for improving fine-resolution image segmentation. Recently, Transformer comprise a hot topic in the computer vision domain. Vision Transformer demonstrates the great capability of global information modelling, boosting many vision tasks, such as image classification, object detection and especially semantic segmentation. In this paper, we propose an efficient hybrid Transformer (EHT) for semantic segmentation of urban scene images. EHT takes advantage of CNNs and Transformer, learning global-local context to strengthen the feature representation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that EHT has higher efficiency with competitive accuracy compared with state-of-the-art benchmark methods. Specifically, the proposed EHT achieves a 67.0% mIoU on the UAVid test set and outperforms other lightweight models significantly. The code will be available soon.
label: human
title: Efficient Hybrid Transformer: Learning Global-local Context for Urban Sence Segmentation

text: The CNN-based methods have achieved impressive results in medical image segmentation, but it failed to capture the long-range dependencies due to the inherent locality of convolution operation. Transformer-based methods are popular in vision tasks recently because of its capacity of long-range dependencies and get a promising performance. However, it lacks in modeling local context, although some works attempted to embed convolutional layer to overcome this problem and achieved some improvement, but it makes the feature inconsistent and fails to leverage the natural multi-scale features of hierarchical transformer, which limit the performance of models. In this paper, taking medical image segmentation as an example, we present MISSFormer, an effective and powerful Medical Image Segmentation tranSFormer. MISSFormer is a hierarchical encoder-decoder network and has two appealing designs: 1) A feed forward network is redesigned with the proposed Enhanced Transformer Block, which makes features aligned adaptively and enhances the long-range dependencies and local context. 2) We proposed Enhanced Transformer Context Bridge, a context bridge with the enhanced transformer block to model the long-range dependencies and local context of multi-scale features generated by our hierarchical transformer encoder. Driven by these two designs, the MISSFormer shows strong capacity to capture more valuable dependencies and context in medical image segmentation. The experiments on multi-organ and cardiac segmentation tasks demonstrate the superiority, effectiveness and robustness of our MISSFormer, the exprimental results of MISSFormer trained from scratch even outperforms state-of-the-art methods pretrained on ImageNet, and the core designs can be generalized to other visual segmentation tasks. The code will be released in Github.
label: human
title: MISSFormer: An Effective Medical Image Segmentation Transformer

text: Modern deep neural networks struggle to transfer knowledge and generalize across domains when deploying to real-world applications. Domain generalization (DG) aims to learn a universal representation from multiple source domains to improve the network generalization ability on unseen target domains. Previous DG methods mostly focus on the data-level consistency scheme to advance the generalization capability of deep networks, without considering the synergistic regularization of different consistency schemes. In this paper, we present a novel Hierarchical Consistency framework for Domain Generalization (HCDG) by ensembling Extrinsic Consistency and Intrinsic Consistency. Particularly, for Extrinsic Consistency, we leverage the knowledge across multiple source domains to enforce data-level consistency. Also, we design a novel Amplitude Gaussian-mixing strategy for Fourier-based data augmentation to enhance such consistency. For Intrinsic Consistency, we perform task-level consistency for the same instance under the dual-task form. We evaluate the proposed HCDG framework on two medical image segmentation tasks, i.e., optic cup/disc segmentation on fundus images and prostate MRI segmentation. Extensive experimental results manifest the effectiveness and versatility of our HCDG framework. Code will be available once accept.
label: human
title: HCDG: A Hierarchical Consistency Framework for Domain Generalization on Medical Image Segmentation

text: Machine learning has been utilized to perform tasks in many different domains such as classification, object detection, image segmentation and natural language analysis. Data labeling has always been one of the most important tasks in machine learning. However, labeling large amounts of data increases the monetary cost in machine learning. As a result, researchers started to focus on reducing data annotation and labeling costs. Transfer learning was designed and widely used as an efficient approach that can reasonably reduce the negative impact of limited data, which in turn, reduces the data preparation cost. Even transferring previous knowledge from a source domain reduces the amount of data needed in a target domain. However, large amounts of annotated data are still demanded to build robust models and improve the prediction accuracy of the model. Therefore, researchers started to pay more attention on auto annotation and labeling. In this survey paper, we provide a review of previous techniques that focuses on optimized data annotation and labeling for video, audio, and text data.

title: A Survey on Machine Learning Techniques for Auto Labeling of Video, Audio, and Text Data

Therefore, recent methods try to use un-supervised domain adaptation (UDA) methods to borrow in-formation from labeled data from other datasets (source do-mains) to a new dataset (target domain). However, due tothe absence of labels in the target domain, the performance ofUDA methods is much worse than that of the fully supervisedmethod. Our Box-Adapt is a two-stage methodthat first performs joint training on the source and target do-mains, and then conducts self-training with the pseudo-labelsof the target domain. text: Transformers have shown impressive performance in various natural language processing and computer vision tasks, due to the capability of modeling long-range dependencies. Recent progress has demonstrated to combine such transformers with CNN-based semantic image segmentation models is very promising. However, it is not well studied yet on how well a pure transformer based approach can achieve for image segmentation. In this work, we explore a novel framework for semantic image segmentation, which is encoder-decoder based Fully Transformer Networks (FTN). Specifically, we first propose a Pyramid Group Transformer (PGT) as the encoder for progressively learning hierarchical features, while reducing the computation complexity of the standard visual transformer(ViT). Then, we propose a Feature Pyramid Transformer (FPT) to fuse semantic-level and spatial-level information from multiple levels of the PGT encoder for semantic image segmentation. Surprisingly, this simple baseline can achieve new state-of-the-art results on multiple challenging semantic segmentation benchmarks, including PASCAL Context, ADE20K and COCO-Stuff. The source code will be released upon the publication of this work.
label: human
title: Fully Transformer Networks for Semantic Image Segmentation
